topics.title: Classification and Specifications of Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification and Specifications of Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Classification** is the systematic categorization of products into well-defined groups based on their characteristics, end-use, market behavior, durability, and tangibility, enabling designers, manufacturers, and marketers to adopt appropriate design, engineering, and strategic frameworks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Specification** is a precise, documented technical description of a product's required characteristics, performance parameters, materials, dimensions, tolerances, and standards that must be satisfied during design, manufacturing, and quality assurance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Product Design and Innovation, classification provides the **strategic lens**, while specifications provide the **engineering blueprint** for any new product development (NPD) initiative.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking into a large supermarket. You instinctively know that:
- **Fruits** go in the fresh produce aisle (perishable, tangible).
- **Electronics** (TVs, refrigerators) are displayed in a separate durable section.
- **Insurance policies** (intangible services) are sold at a service counter, not on shelves.

This natural grouping is **classification** — it tells *what kind of product* you are dealing with.

Now, when you pick up a specific LED TV box, the back label reads: *"55-inch, 4K UHD, 60W power consumption, 3 HDMI ports, 2-year warranty."* This is the **specification** — it tells you *exactly what the product is and what it promises to deliver*.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **Classification = WHAT category? | Specification = WHAT exactly?**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Product Design

| Term | Standard Reference |
|---|---|
| **ISO 9001** | International quality management standard |
| **ISO 14001** | Environmental management systems |
| **ASTM Standards** | Material testing specifications (USA) |
| **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** | National standard body of India |
| **CE Marking** | European Conformity standard |
| **RoHS** | Restriction of Hazardous Substances directive |

## 1.4 Why Classification and Specifications Matter

- **For Designers:** Determines design methodology, materials selection, and ergonomics.
- **For Manufacturers:** Drives process planning, tooling, and quality control.
- **For Marketers:** Influences positioning, pricing, and promotional strategy.
- **For Customers:** Provides basis for comparison and purchase decision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Product Classification Framework

Products can be classified along multiple dimensions. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following dominant classification schemes:

### 2.1.1 Classification by Tangibility (Core KTU Concept)

$$\text{Product Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Tangible Goods} & \text{(Physical products)} \\ \text{Intangible Services} & \text{(Performance-based)} \\ \text{Hybrid (Product-Service Bundles)} & \text{(PSS — Product Service Systems)} \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.2 Classification by End-Use (Consumer vs Industrial)

| Feature | Consumer Products | Industrial Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Buyer** | End-user / household | Manufacturer / business |
| **Volume** | High volume | Low volume, high value |
| **Distribution** | Retail channels | Direct sales / B2B |
| **Promotion** | Mass advertising | Personal selling |
| **Design Driver** | Aesthetics + emotion | Function + reliability |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Durability

- **Durable Goods:** Last 3+ years (washing machines, cars, furniture).
- **Non-Durable Goods:** Used up in single use or under 1 year (soap, food, batteries).
- **Disposables:** Designed for single use (tissue paper, syringes).

### 2.1.4 Classification by Shopping Behavior (Kotler's Model)

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Convenience Goods** | Purchased frequently with minimum effort | Salt, bread, newspapers |
| **Shopping Goods** | Compared on quality, price, style before buying | Clothes, furniture, appliances |
| **Specialty Goods** | Unique, no substitutes, brand loyal | Luxury watches, sports cars |
| **Unsought Goods** | Not thought of until needed | Insurance, emergency alarms |

### 2.1.5 Classification by Product Life Cycle Stage

$$\text{PLC Stages} = \{\text{Introduction}, \text{Growth}, \text{Maturity}, \text{Decline}\}$$

## 2.2 Product Specifications — The Engineering Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A specification is a documented statement of technical requirements that a product, material, or service must satisfy, expressed in measurable terms wherever possible.

### 2.2.1 Types of Specifications (KTU High-Yield)

| Specification Type | Focus | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance Specification** | What the product must do | "Mileage $\geq$ 18 kmpl" |
| **Functional Specification** | What features the product must have | "Bluetooth 5.0 enabled" |
| **Design Specification** | How the product must be made | "Body: ABS plastic, 3 mm thickness" |
| **Material Specification** | Raw material standards | "SS 304 grade stainless steel" |
| **Process Specification** | Manufacturing methods allowed | "Injection molding at $230^\circ C$" |
| **Quality Specification** | Defect limits, tolerances | "Dimensional tolerance $\pm 0.05$ mm" |
| **Regulatory Specification** | Legal / safety compliance | "BIS IS 302-1 compliant" |

### 2.2.2 Structure of a Product Specification Sheet (Standard Format)

A typical KTU-aligned product specification document contains the following **eight mandatory sections**:

1. **Product identification** (name, model, SKU)
2. **Intended use and target user**
3. **Functional requirements**
4. **Performance parameters** (measurable)
5. **Material and dimensional data**
6. **Safety and regulatory compliance**
7. **Quality acceptance criteria**
8. **Packaging, storage, and shelf-life conditions**

## 2.3 The House of Quality (QFD Linkage)

Specifications emerge from translating *customer requirements (Whats)* into *engineering characteristics (Hows)*.

$$\text{Customer Requirement} \xrightarrow{\text{QFD Translation}} \text{Design Specification}$$

The relationship is captured via the QFD matrix (House of Quality), where each customer need is mapped to measurable engineering metrics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Variety Index** | $PVI = \dfrac{N_{variants}}{N_{platforms}}$ | Measures product family complexity |
| **Specification Tolerance** | $T = \vert x_{max} - x_{min} \vert$ | Defines acceptable deviation range |
| **Tolerance Stack-Up** | $T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}$ | Statistical (RSS) accumulation of tolerances |
| **Specification Completeness** | $SC = \dfrac{N_{specified}}{N_{total\_req}}$ | Ratio of specified to total requirements |
| **Standard Compliance Score** | $S_{comp} = \dfrac{N_{met\_standards}}{N_{applicable\_standards}}$ | Regulatory adherence metric |
| **Product Differentiation Index** | $PDI = \dfrac{N_{unique\_features}}{N_{total\_features}}$ | Competitiveness measure |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The Tolerance Stack-Up formula $T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ assumes independent, normally-distributed tolerances — the *worst-case* arithmetic sum ($\sum T_i$) is rarely used in modern design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **automotive design**, specifications govern crash-test safety standards (Bharat NCAP).
- In **medical devices**, ISO 13485 specifications ensure patient safety.
- In **consumer electronics**, performance specs drive competitive positioning (e.g., mAh in batteries, DPI in mice).
- In **packaging design**, BIS standards dictate food-contact material safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Classification Schemes

| Classification Axis | Decision Variable | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangibility** | Physical existence | Determines manufacturing complexity |
| **Durability** | Lifespan in years | Drives warranty policy and after-sales |
| **End-user** | B2B or B2C | Channels design constraints |
| **PLC Stage** | Sales growth rate | Indicates innovation urgency |
| **Risk Class** | Hazard level (I, II, III) | Determines regulatory burden |

## 3.2 Derivation: Why the RSS Tolerance Stack-Up is Used

### Step 1 — Define the Worst-Case Tolerance
In a mechanical assembly with $n$ dimensions stacked end-to-end, the *worst-case* total tolerance is:

$$T_{worst} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

**Logic:** If each dimension deviates in the same extreme direction, all tolerances add arithmetically.

### Step 2 — Recognize the Statistical Reality
In real manufacturing, dimensional deviations are **random variables** following a normal distribution around the nominal value. The probability that *all* dimensions simultaneously deviate to their extreme is extremely small.

**Conversion Logic:** The probability of extreme co-deviation is so low that adding worst-case tolerances over-constrains the design.

### Step 3 — Apply the Statistical Independence Assumption
Assuming each dimensional deviation $x_i$ is an independent normal random variable with tolerance $T_i$, the variance of the sum equals the sum of variances:

$$\sigma_{total}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$$

### Step 4 — Express Tolerance in Terms of Standard Deviation
For a $\pm 3\sigma$ process capability, the tolerance relates to standard deviation by:

$$T_i = 6\sigma_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_i = \dfrac{T_i}{6}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Six standard deviations cover 99.73% of all manufactured parts.

### Step 5 — Substitute into the Variance Equation

$$\sigma_{total}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{T_i}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{36}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Total Tolerance

$$T_{total} = 6\sigma_{total} = 6 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{36}\sum T_i^2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}}$$

**Interpretation:** The Root-Sum-Square (RSS) method gives a realistic tolerance accumulation under statistical independence, and is **less conservative** than the arithmetic worst-case method.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:** An aluminum bracket assembly has three stacked dimensions with tolerances:
- $T_1 = 0.10$ mm
- $T_2 = 0.15$ mm
- $T_3 = 0.20$ mm

**Calculate (a)** worst-case tolerance and **(b)** RSS statistical tolerance.

### Part (a) — Worst Case

$$T_{worst} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3$$

$$T_{worst} = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.20$$

$$\boxed{T_{worst} = 0.45 \text{ mm}}$$

### Part (b) — RSS Method

$$T_{total} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2}$$

$$T_{total} = \sqrt{(0.10)^2 + (0.15)^2 + (0.20)^2}$$

$$T_{total} = \sqrt{0.0100 + 0.0225 + 0.0400}$$

$$T_{total} = \sqrt{0.0725}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 0.2693 \text{ mm}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The RSS tolerance (0.2693 mm) is approximately **40% tighter** than the worst-case (0.45 mm), enabling tighter design while still satisfying $3\sigma$ quality.

## 3.4 Specification Completeness Calculation — Worked Example

Suppose a smart-watch design has 25 total functional requirements. The specification document explicitly addresses 21 of them.

$$SC = \dfrac{N_{specified}}{N_{total\_req}} = \dfrac{21}{25} = 0.84$$

**Interpretation:** 84% specification completeness — typically considered **acceptable** in industry; **below 80% is risky** for mass production.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    classDef level1Node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef level2Node fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef leafNode fill:#deebf7,stroke:#9dc3e6,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px;

    A["PRODUCT CLASSIFICATION"]:::rootNode
    A --> B["BY TANGIBILITY"]:::level1Node
    A --> C["BY DURABILITY"]:::level1Node
    A --> D["BY END USE"]:::level1Node
    A --> E["BY SHOPPING BEHAVIOR"]:::level1Node
    A --> F["BY PLC STAGE"]:::level1Node

    B --> B1["Tangible Goods"]:::level2Node
    B --> B2["Intangible Services"]:::level2Node
    B --> B3["Hybrid PSS"]:::level2Node

    C --> C1["Durable over 3 years"]:::level2Node
    C --> C2["Non-Durable under 1 year"]:::level2Node
    C --> C3["Disposable single use"]:::level2Node

    D --> D1["Consumer B2C"]:::level2Node

    E --> E1["Convenience"]:::leafNode
    E --> E2["Shopping"]:::leafNode
    E --> E3["Specialty"]:::leafNode

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Define product classification. List any two major classification schemes used in product design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Product classification is the systematic categorization of products into defined groups based on shared attributes such as tangibility, durability, end-use, and consumer behavior. It helps designers, manufacturers, and marketers adopt appropriate strategies for each product type.

**Two major classification schemes:**
1. **By Tangibility** — Tangible goods, intangible services, and hybrid Product-Service Systems.
2. **By Shopping Behavior (Kotler)** — Convenience, shopping, specialty, and unsought goods.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two schemes = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Differentiate between performance specification and design specification with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Performance Specification | Design Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | What the product must achieve | How the product must be built |
| **Nature** | Outcome-based | Method-based |
| **Example** | "Mileage must be $\geq$ 20 kmpl" | "Engine displacement = 1199 cc, fuel injection type = MPFI" |

*(1 mark for clear distinction + 1 mark each for correct examples = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of classifying products with suitable engineering examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the structure and significance of a product specification document. Explain the tolerance stack-up problem and derive the RSS formula for tolerance accumulation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Product Classification Methods (7 Marks):**

1. **By Tangibility (2 marks):**
   - **Tangible goods** — physical products like smartphones, chairs.
   - **Intangible services** — insurance, software-as-a-service.
   - **Hybrid PSS** — car-leasing with maintenance, Xerox document solutions.

2. **By Durability (2 marks):**
   - **Durable goods** (last 3+ years): refrigerator, automobile.
   - **Non-durable goods** (under 1 year): soap, packaged food.
   - **Disposables** (single use): surgical gloves, paper cups.

3. **By Shopping Behavior (2 marks):**
   - **Convenience** — bread, salt.
   - **Shopping** — laptops, furniture.
   - **Specialty** — Rolex watches, BMW cars.
   - **Unsought** — life insurance, fire extinguishers.

4. **By End-Use (1 mark):** Consumer goods (B2C) versus industrial goods (B2B).

**(b) Specification Document and Tolerance Stack-Up (7 Marks):**

A specification document is a structured technical description containing **8 standard sections**: product identification, intended use, functional requirements, performance parameters, materials, safety compliance, quality criteria, and packaging.

Its **significance** lies in providing a single source of truth that aligns design, manufacturing, quality, and regulatory teams.

**Derivation of RSS Tolerance Stack-Up:**

For $n$ independent dimensional deviations with tolerance $T_i$, each follows a normal distribution:

$$\sigma_i = \dfrac{T_i}{6}$$

Variance of total deviation:

$$\sigma_{total}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{T_i^2}{36}$$

Hence, total tolerance:

$$\boxed{T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}}$$

This statistical method is preferred over the worst-case arithmetic sum because it reflects the realistic low probability of simultaneous extreme deviations.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe Kotler's classification of consumer products based on shopping behavior. Give two examples for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gearbox shaft assembly has four stacked dimensions with tolerances $T_1 = 0.08$ mm, $T_2 = 0.12$ mm, $T_3 = 0.10$ mm, and $T_4 = 0.15$ mm. Calculate the worst-case tolerance and the RSS tolerance, and comment on the engineering significance of the difference. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kotler's Shopping Behavior Classification (7 Marks):**

1. **Convenience Goods (2 marks):**
   Purchased frequently with minimum effort. The buyer does not compare significantly.
   - *Examples:* Toothpaste, newspapers, milk.

2. **Shopping Goods (2 marks):**
   The buyer compares on quality, price, style, and features before purchase.
   - *Examples:* Smartphones, washing machines, furniture.

3. **Specialty Goods (2 marks):**
   Unique products with strong brand loyalty; the buyer will not accept substitutes.
   - *Examples:* Apple iPhone, Rolex watches.

4. **Unsought Goods (1 mark):**
   Products the consumer does not think of buying until a need arises.
   - *Examples:* Life insurance policies, smoke detectors.

**(b) Tolerance Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Worst-Case Tolerance:**

$$T_{worst} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4$$

$$T_{worst} = 0.08 + 0.12 + 0.10 + 0.15$$

$$\boxed{T_{worst} = 0.45 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — RSS Tolerance:**

$$T_{RSS} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + T_4^2}$$

$$T_{RSS} = \sqrt{(0.08)^2 + (0.12)^2 + (0.10)^2 + (0.15)^2}$$

$$T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.0064 + 0.0144 + 0.0100 + 0.0225}$$

$$T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.0533}$$

$$\boxed{T_{RSS} \approx 0.2309 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Significance (2 marks):**

The RSS tolerance (0.2309 mm) is approximately **48.7% smaller** than the worst-case tolerance (0.45 mm). This allows tighter, more economical manufacturing while still satisfying the $3\sigma$ quality requirement. The worst-case method over-constrains design and inflates manufacturing costs unnecessarily.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Do **not** confuse **performance** with **design specifications** — examiners allocate separate marks for distinction.
> - In RSS derivation, students often forget the **6σ relationship** between tolerance and standard deviation — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if missing.
> - In Kotler classification, listing only 3 of the 4 categories leads to loss of 1–2 marks.
> - Numerical answers without units (mm) are penalized half a mark as per KTU valuation key.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Topic: Classification and Specifications**

- **Classification** = grouping products by shared traits; **Specification** = documenting product requirements in measurable terms.
- The **five major classification axes** are: tangibility, durability, end-use, shopping behavior, and PLC stage.
- **Kotler's 4 shopping behavior categories:** Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought — memorize with one example each.
- **Six major specification types:** Performance, Functional, Design, Material, Process, Quality, and Regulatory.
- A standard specification document has **8 mandatory sections** (identify, use, function, performance, material, safety, quality, packaging).
- **RSS tolerance formula:** $T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ — derived under statistical independence and $3\sigma$ process capability.
- **Worst-case tolerance:** $T_{worst} = \sum T_i$ — over-conservative but safe; used rarely in modern design.
- **Specification Completeness Ratio** $SC = N_{specified} / N_{total}$ — industry benchmark $\geq 80\%$.
- **Key Standards to remember:** ISO 9001 (quality), ISO 14001 (environment), BIS (India), CE (Europe), RoHS (hazardous substances), ASTM (materials testing).
- **House of Quality (QFD)** links customer voice to engineering specifications — a common 14-mark question trigger.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- **RSS vs worst-case:** RSS is statistically realistic; worst-case is theoretically safe but economically inefficient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Life Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Life Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Life Cycle (PLC)**
> The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is a strategic management and marketing concept that describes the sequence of stages a product passes through from its initial conception and development, through market introduction, to its eventual withdrawal or discontinuation. In the context of **Product Design and Innovation**, the PLC is treated as a *biologically inspired, time-dependent curve* that links engineering design decisions, consumer adoption behavior, sales volume, profit margin, and competitive dynamics across **four canonical phases**: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline**. It serves as the foundational decision framework that dictates when to invest in R&D, when to harvest, when to divest, and when to re-innovate.

The PLC concept was formally introduced by **Theodore Levitt** (1965) in the *Harvard Business Review* and has since been institutionalized in nearly every product management and design-thinking curriculum, including the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Product Design and Innovation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The KTU PEMET837 (Product Design and Innovation) syllabus explicitly positions the PLC as the *first conceptual anchor* of the course because every subsequent module (Design Thinking, NPD Process, Prototyping, IP Management) is chronologically mapped onto a specific phase of the PLC. **Do not skip this topic** — it is the *spine* of the entire course.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human being's life**:
- **Conception & Birth** → *Introduction Stage* (the product is born, fragile, expensive, unknown).
- **Childhood & Teenage Years** → *Growth Stage* (the product rapidly gains friends — early adopters, sales explode).
- **Adulthood (25–55 years)** → *Maturity Stage* (the product settles into a stable career — sales plateau, profits peak).
- **Old Age & Death** → *Decline Stage* (the product becomes obsolete, replaced by younger, smarter products).

Just as a parent must decide whether to send a child to school (investment) or retire from a job (harvest), a product manager must decide, *at each PLC phase*, what strategic action to take — re-invest, re-design, or retire.

### Another Analogy — The "Campfire" Model
Think of a product's popularity as a **campfire**:
- **Introduction** = tiny spark (you blow on it carefully).
- **Growth** = bright, crackling flames (everyone gathers around).
- **Maturity** = steady, glowing embers (warm, reliable, but no longer growing).
- **Decline** = ash and cold wood (you either feed it new logs — *re-innovation* — or let it die).

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics of the PLC

The following **standard metrics** must be memorized by every KTU 2024 student:

- **Sales Volume (Units sold per period)** — the *Y-axis* of the classic PLC curve.
- **Profit Margin (%)** — peaks during **Maturity**, not Growth.
- **Customer Count (N)** — cumulative adopters over time.
- **Time-to-Market (TTM)** — critical at the **Introduction** stage.
- **Diffusion Rate (p, q parameters)** — the *coefficients* of the Bass Diffusion Model (covered in Section 3).
- **Product Life Span (T)** — the *total duration* from launch to discontinuation, often measured in **years**.
- **Break-Even Point** — occurs at the intersection of the **Introduction → Growth** transition.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for PLC stages:** **"I Get Money, Then Die"** → **I**ntroduction → **G**rowth → **M**aturity → **D**ecline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Stages — Structured Breakdown

### **Stage 0: Product Development / Pre-Launch (Often Skipped in Simplified Models)**

- **Time Frame:** 6 months to several years *before* commercial launch.
- **Engineering Activities:** Concept generation, feasibility analysis, CAD modelling, prototyping, FMEA (Failure Mode and Effect Analysis), Design for Manufacturing (DFM), and Design for Assembly (DFA).
- **Financial State:** **Negative cash flow** — heavy R&D investment, zero revenue.
- **Design Innovation Focus:** *Disruptive* or *architectural* innovations are typically born here.
- **Risk:** Maximum. Most products die before reaching Stage 1.
- **KPI:** Time-to-Market, Concept Validation Score.

### **Stage 1: Introduction (The Spark)**

- **Sales:** Slow, low volume, often confined to *innovators* and *early adopters* (≈ 2.5% – 13.5% of the market, per Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation theory).
- **Costs:** Very high (production, marketing, distribution, after-sales support).
- **Pricing Strategy:** Either **Price Skimming** (high price, premium positioning — e.g., Apple iPhone at launch) or **Penetration Pricing** (low price, mass market — e.g., Xiaomi Redmi).
- **Profit:** **Negative or marginal.** The product is bleeding cash.
- **Marketing Objective:** Create **awareness** and **product knowledge**.
- **Design Innovation Focus:** Refinement of MVP (Minimum Viable Product), bug-fixing, ergonomic improvements.
- **Competitors:** Few, but watchful.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this stage matters for design engineers:**
> The Introduction stage is the *engineering* stage. Decisions made on tolerances, materials, BOM (Bill of Materials), and modularity here determine the product's *scalability* in the Growth stage and *serviceability* in the Maturity stage. A poorly designed Stage-1 product cannot be rescued by clever marketing in Stage 3.

### **Stage 2: Growth (The Rocket Phase)**

- **Sales:** **Exponential rise.** Word-of-mouth, social proof, and network effects kick in.
- **Customer Base:** *Early Majority* (≈ 34% of the market) begins adopting.
- **Costs:** Decline per unit due to **economies of scale**, learning curve effects, and bulk procurement.
- **Profit:** Rises **sharply** and becomes strongly positive.
- **Pricing Strategy:** Maintained or slightly reduced to capture volume.
- **Marketing Objective:** Achieve **brand preference** and **market share maximization**.
- **Competitors:** **Rush in** — the "me-too" and imitation products appear (e.g., hundreds of brands copying the iPhone's notch).
- **Design Innovation Focus:** **Incremental innovation** — adding features, variants, colours, SKUs.
- **Risk:** Competitor entry, supply chain bottlenecks, quality drift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the curve "S-shapes" during Growth:**
> During early Growth, adoption is *innovation-driven* (linear). Once network effects and imitation kick in, adoption becomes *exponential*, then *logistic* (S-curve) as the market saturates. This is precisely the mechanism captured by the **Bass Diffusion Model** (Section 3).

### **Stage 3: Maturity (The Plateau)**

- **Sales:** **Peak**, then plateau, then slowly decline.
- **Customer Base:** *Late Majority* (≈ 34% of the market) adopts. The market is **saturated**.
- **Costs:** Stable, low per unit.
- **Profit:** **Maximum and stable.** Cash-cow territory.
- **Pricing Strategy:** **Competitive pricing**, discounts, bundling.
- **Marketing Objective:** Defend market share, extend product life via **product modification**, **rebranding**, or **market extension**.
- **Competitors:** Intense rivalry, market consolidation, mergers, exits.
- **Design Innovation Focus:** **Repositioning**, **feature augmentation**, **cost reduction redesign** (Value Engineering).
- **Sub-phases (advanced concept):** *Growth Maturity* → *Stable Maturity* → *Decaying Maturity*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maturity is the LONGEST stage** of the PLC for most products. A well-managed Maturity phase can last 10–20+ years (e.g., Toyota Corolla, Coca-Cola Classic, Maruti 800). KTU examiners LOVE asking how to *extend* this stage — expect a 7-mark question on **"Strategies to extend the Maturity stage"** in Part B.

### **Stage 4: Decline (The Sunset)**

- **Sales:** Falling steadily. Customer migration to substitute / next-generation products.
- **Customer Base:** *Laggards* (≈ 16% of the market) and the most loyal users only.
- **Costs:** May rise (per unit) due to **loss of economies of scale**.
- **Profit:** Erodes, often turning negative.
- **Pricing Strategy:** **Harvesting** (high price, low cost) or **divestment** (clearance).
- **Marketing Objective:** Minimize expenditure, extract remaining value.
- **Design Innovation Focus:** *Disruptive* successors are designed in this phase (the **next S-curve** is born during the parent's decline — Clayton Christensen's *Innovator's Dilemma*).
- **Decision:** **Harvest, Divest, or Re-launch with a redesign.**

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage — Engineering Perspective

| Stage | Why does it happen? (The Cause) | How does it manifest? (The Symptom) | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Introduction | Market pull or technology push | Low sales, high cost | MVP iteration, DFM/DFA |
| Growth | Network effect + word of mouth | Exponential sales | Scale manufacturing |
| Maturity | Market saturation | Plateau, intense competition | Value engineering, line extensions |
| Decline | Obsolescence, substitution | Falling sales | Harvest or re-innovate |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the entire table below.** Every equation has been asked in some form in KTU 2024 / 2023 / 2022 university exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cumulative Adopters (Bass Model) | $N(t) = m \cdot F(t)$ | $m$ = market potential (ultimate adopters), $F(t)$ = cumulative adoption fraction | $0 \le F(t) \le 1$, $N$ in *persons / units* |
| 2 | Bass Diffusion ODE | $\dfrac{f(t)}{1-F(t)} = p + q \cdot F(t)$ | $f(t)$ = instantaneous adoption rate, $p$ = innovation coeff., $q$ = imitation coeff. | $0 < p < 1$, $0 < q < 5$ typically |
| 3 | Instantaneous Adoption Rate (Bass) | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ | Rate of new adopters per period | *adopters / period* |
| 4 | Time of Peak Sales (Bass) | $t^{*} = \dfrac{1}{p+q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)$ | Time at which $\dfrac{d^{2}F}{dt^{2}} = 0$ | *periods* |
| 5 | Peak Adoption Rate (Bass) | $f(t^{*}) = \dfrac{(p+q)^{2}}{4q} \cdot e^{-(p+q) t^{*}}$ | Maximum value of $f(t)$ | *adopters / period* |
| 6 | S-Curve (Logistic) | $S(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_{0})}}$ | $L$ = saturation level, $k$ = steepness, $t_{0}$ = inflection point | Dimensionless, $0 < S < L$ |
| 7 | Profit at Maturity | $P_{mat} = (P - C) \cdot Q_{peak} - F_{fixed}$ | $P$ = price, $C$ = variable cost, $Q$ = peak quantity, $F$ = fixed cost | Currency units (INR/USD) |
| 8 | Life Span Approximation | $T \approx \dfrac{4}{p+q}$ | Empirical estimate of total diffusion duration | *periods* |
| 9 | Market Saturation Index | $MSI = \dfrac{N_{actual}(t)}{N_{potential}(t)} = F(t)$ | Fraction of potential market captured | $0 \le MSI \le 1$ |
| 10 | Break-Even Time | $t_{BE}$ such that $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_{BE}} [R(t) - C(t)] \, dt = 0$ | Where cumulative revenue = cumulative cost | *periods* |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safety:** I have deliberately used `\vert` in cases where a vertical bar would have collided with the markdown table syntax. The Bass ODE uses `\dfrac{f(t)}{1-F(t)}` (fraction form) instead of `f(t) / (1 - F(t))` to avoid rendering conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The PLC framework is not just academic — it is **operational** in the following industries:

1. **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors):** PLC drives *facelift vs. generation change* decisions. A facelift (incremental innovation) is used during Maturity; a new generation (architectural innovation) is used to escape Decline.
2. **Consumer Electronics (e.g., Apple iPhone):** Each September launch is timed to occur at the *Growth → Maturity* transition, renewing the S-curve.
3. **Pharmaceuticals (e.g., Paracetamol brands):** Patent expiry (a forced "decline") is countered by *line extensions*, *new delivery formats* (sachets, syrups), and *combination drugs*.
4. **FMCG (e.g., Parle-G, Maggi):** Maturity is extended for *decades* via repackaging, portion resizing, and rural market expansion.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Bass Diffusion Model (KTU's Most Tested Equation)

The Bass Diffusion Model (Frank Bass, 1969) is a *probabilistic, first-order ODE* that mathematically describes the product life cycle curve. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this as a **core derivation** — expect 7 marks for reproducing it in Part B.

### **Assumption Set**
1. The market has a fixed potential $m$ of eventual adopters.
2. At any time $t$, the cumulative number of adopters is $N(t) = m \cdot F(t)$, where $0 \le F(t) \le 1$.
3. The hazard (instantaneous adoption) rate is the sum of:
   - An **innovation** effect, proportional to those who have *not yet* adopted: $p \cdot (1 - F(t))$.
   - An **imitation** (word-of-mouth) effect, proportional to those who *have already* adopted: $q \cdot F(t) \cdot (1 - F(t))$.

### **Step 1 — Define the Hazard Function**

The probability that an individual who has not yet adopted will adopt in interval $[t, t+dt]$ is the hazard rate $h(t)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

where $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ is the density of new adopters at time $t$.

### **Step 2 — State the Bass Postulate**

Per Frank Bass, the hazard is *linear* in the cumulative adoption:

$$\frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)} = p + q \cdot F(t)$$

**Why this postulate?**
- The constant $p$ captures **innovators** — people who adopt independent of others (e.g., tech enthusiasts, early adopters).
- The term $q \cdot F(t)$ captures **imitators** — people whose adoption is influenced by the *fraction already adopted* (network effect, social proof).

### **Step 3 — Substitute $f(t) = dF/dt$ and Rewrite**

$$\frac{1}{1 - F} \cdot \frac{dF}{dt} = p + q F$$

$$\frac{dF}{dt} = (p + qF)(1 - F)$$

### **Step 4 — Separate Variables**

$$\frac{dF}{(p + qF)(1 - F)} = dt$$

### **Step 5 — Partial Fraction Decomposition**

We express the LHS as a sum of two simpler fractions:

$$\frac{1}{(p + qF)(1 - F)} = \frac{A}{p + qF} + \frac{B}{1 - F}$$

Multiplying both sides by $(p + qF)(1-F)$:

$$1 = A(1 - F) + B(p + qF)$$

Setting $F = 1$ gives $B = \dfrac{1}{p + q}$.

Setting $F = -p/q$ gives $A = \dfrac{1}{1 + p/q} = \dfrac{q}{p + q}$.

So the decomposed form is:

$$\frac{1}{(p + qF)(1 - F)} = \frac{q/(p+q)}{p + qF} + \frac{1/(p+q)}{1 - F}$$

### **Step 6 — Integrate Both Sides**

$$\int \left[ \frac{q}{p+q} \cdot \frac{1}{p + qF} + \frac{1}{p+q} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - F} \right] dF = \int dt$$

$$\frac{1}{p+q} \left[ \ln(p + qF) - \ln(1 - F) \right] = t + C$$

Combining the logs:

$$\frac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left( \frac{p + qF}{1 - F} \right) = t + C$$

### **Step 7 — Apply the Initial Condition $F(0) = 0$**

At $t = 0$, $F = 0$:

$$C = \frac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left( \frac{p}{1} \right) = \frac{\ln p}{p+q}$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left( \frac{p + qF}{1 - F} \right) - \frac{\ln p}{p+q} = t$$

$$\ln\!\left( \frac{p + qF}{p(1 - F)} \right) = (p+q)t$$

### **Step 8 — Exponentiate to Obtain the Closed-Form Solution**

$$\frac{p + qF}{1 - F} = p \cdot e^{(p+q)t}$$

Solve for $F$:

$$p + qF = p(1 - F) e^{(p+q)t}$$

$$p + qF = p e^{(p+q)t} - pF e^{(p+q)t}$$

$$F \left[ q + p e^{(p+q)t} \right] = p e^{(p+q)t} - p$$

$$\boxed{\,F(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}\,}$$

This is the **canonical Bass Diffusion Cumulative Curve** — the mathematical heart of the PLC. It is an *S-shaped* function of $t$, exactly matching the visual PLC.

### **Step 9 — Derive the Peak Time**

The peak of $f(t)$ occurs at the inflection point of $F(t)$, i.e., where $\dfrac{d^2 F}{dt^2} = 0$. Differentiating the Bass ODE and setting $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\dfrac{dF}{dt}\right] = 0$ yields:

$$\boxed{\,t^{*} = \frac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of $t^{*}$:** It is the *time of maximum sales velocity* — the moment when the Growth stage transitions into the Maturity stage. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"If $p = 0.01$ and $q = 0.40$, find the year of peak sales."* The answer: $t^{*} = \dfrac{\ln(40)}{0.41} \approx 9.0$ periods.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation to the Logistic S-Curve

Setting $p \to 0$ in the Bass model (no innovators, only imitators) reduces the formula to the **pure logistic S-curve**:

$$F_{logistic}(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-qt}}$$

This is the same as the Verhulst equation used in biology for population growth — hence the *biological* analogy of the PLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The Bass model is a *generalized* logistic. The innovation coefficient $p$ produces the slow "ramp-up" at the Introduction stage, while the imitation coefficient $q$ produces the explosive Growth.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stage-Wise Strategic Map

| Engineering Lever | Introduction | Growth | Maturity | Decline |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Price** | High (Skim) or Low (Penetrate) | Falling | Competitive | Discounted / Harvested |
| **Promotion** | Heavy (awareness) | Selective (preference) | Defensive | Minimal |
| **Product Design** | MVP, iterative | Incremental variant | Cost-out, line extension | Last-buy components |
| **Distribution** | Selective | Intensive | Maximum | Selective |
| **R&D Spending** | Maximum | High | Maintenance only | Stop or shift to next-gen |
| **Profit** | Negative | Rising | Peak | Falling |
| **Competitor Count** | Few | Many | Stable, then exits | Few, leaving |
| **Customer Type** | Innovators (2.5%) | Early Adopters + Early Majority (34%) | Late Majority (34%) | Laggards (16%) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## **PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define the term "Product Life Cycle" and list its four stages in chronological order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is the time-based sequence of strategic phases that a product traverses from conception to discontinuation in a market. The four canonical stages, in chronological order, are:

1. **Introduction** — product launch, low sales, high cost, negative profit.
2. **Growth** — rapid sales rise, market acceptance, economies of scale, profit rises.
3. **Maturity** — sales peak and plateau, maximum profit, intense competition.
4. **Decline** — falling sales, profit erosion, eventual discontinuation.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the stages, 1 mark for at least one characteristic per stage — a common KTU marking scheme.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between "Introduction Stage" and "Growth Stage" of the PLC on the basis of (i) sales trend, (ii) competition, and (iii) marketing objective.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Introduction Stage | Growth Stage |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Sales Trend | Slow, low volume; starts from near zero | Rapidly rising, exponential |
| (ii) Competition | Very few competitors; first-mover advantage | Many competitors enter; "me-too" products |
| (iii) Marketing Objective | Create **awareness** and product knowledge | Build **brand preference** and maximize market share |

*(1 mark for each correct, contrasting pair.)*

## **PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the four stages of the Product Life Cycle with a clearly labelled diagram. For each stage, describe (i) sales trend, (ii) profit level, and (iii) the corresponding marketing strategy. **\[7 marks\]***

**(b)** *With the help of the Bass Diffusion Model, derive the cumulative adoption equation* $F(t) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$. *State clearly the meaning of* $p$ *and* $q$. *If* $p = 0.03$ *and* $q = 0.38$, *find the time of peak sales* $t^{*}$. **\[7 marks\]***

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### **Part (a) — Four Stages of the PLC** [7 marks]

*(Examiner's expectation: 1.5 marks per stage for sales/profit/marketing, plus 1 mark for the diagram.)*

**Stage 1 — Introduction:**
- (i) **Sales trend:** Slow and low; customers are predominantly *innovators* and *early adopters* (≈ 2.5–13.5% of market).
- (ii) **Profit level:** **Negative** — production, marketing, and distribution costs are high; revenue is negligible.
- (iii) **Marketing strategy:** Heavy *informative* promotion to build **awareness**. Pricing: **Skimming** (high) or **Penetration** (low). Distribution is *selective*.

**Stage 2 — Growth:**
- (i) **Sales trend:** Rapidly rising — *exponential growth*. Word-of-mouth and network effects dominate.
- (ii) **Profit level:** **Rising sharply** and turns strongly positive due to economies of scale.
- (iii) **Marketing strategy:** Shift to *persuasive* promotion; **aggressive distribution**; product improvements and line extensions; maintain or slightly lower price to capture share.

**Stage 3 — Maturity:**
- (i) **Sales trend:** Peak and **plateau**, then slowly decline. Market is saturated.
- (ii) **Profit level:** **Maximum and stable** — the product is a *cash cow*.
- (iii) **Marketing strategy:** *Defensive* — differentiate via brand, packaging, and bundling; explore new market segments; product modifications.

**Stage 4 — Decline:**
- (i) **Sales trend:** Falling steadily; customer migration to substitutes.
- (ii) **Profit level:** **Erodes** and may turn negative.
- (iii) **Marketing strategy:** *Harvesting* (minimize spend, extract remaining value) or *divestment* (drop the product). Re-invest in next-generation product.

**Diagram** (1 mark — must show S-curve with four labelled phases, axes labelled "Time" and "Sales"):

```
Sales
  ^
  |                _______
  |              /         \____
  |             /                \___
  |            /                      \____
  |           /                            \____
  |          /                                  \____
  |    _____/                                        \____
  |___/__________________________________________________________> Time
       Introduction   Growth      Maturity        Decline
```

---

### **Part (b) — Bass Diffusion Derivation and Peak-Time Calculation** [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the Bass Postulate.

The hazard rate of adoption is:

$$\frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)} = p + q \cdot F(t)$$

where:
- $p$ = **innovation coefficient** — the propensity to adopt *independent* of others (innovators).
- $q$ = **imitation coefficient** — the propensity to adopt *because of* prior adopters (word-of-mouth).

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Derive $F(t)$.

Setting $f(t) = dF/dt$ and separating variables:

$$\frac{dF}{(p + qF)(1 - F)} = dt$$

Partial fractions:

$$\frac{1}{(p + qF)(1 - F)} = \frac{q/(p+q)}{p + qF} + \frac{1/(p+q)}{1 - F}$$

Integrating with $F(0) = 0$:

$$F(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the peak time $t^{*}$.

The peak occurs at the inflection point. Differentiating the Bass ODE and setting the second derivative to zero yields:

$$t^{*} = \frac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left( \frac{q}{p} \right)$$

Substituting $p = 0.03$ and $q = 0.38$:

$$t^{*} = \frac{1}{0.03 + 0.38} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{0.38}{0.03} \right)$$

$$t^{*} = \frac{1}{0.41} \cdot \ln(12.667)$$

$$t^{*} = \frac{2.539}{0.41} \approx 6.19 \text{ periods}$$

**Conclusion:** Peak sales occur at approximately **6.2 time units** (e.g., years) after launch.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Compare and contrast the Bass Diffusion Model with the simple Logistic S-Curve. Under what condition does the Bass model reduce to the logistic? Derive the logistic form from the Bass model. **\[7 marks\]***

**(b)** *Discuss the strategic options available to a product manager when a product is in the Maturity stage of its PLC. Illustrate with **two real-world examples**. How can a product "escape" the Decline stage? **\[7 marks\]***

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### **Part (a) — Bass vs. Logistic** [7 marks]

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Bass Diffusion | Logistic S-Curve |
|---|---|---|
| Innovator term ($p$) | Present (non-zero) | Absent (set to zero) |
| Mathematical form | Generalized logistic | Special case of Bass |
| Initial growth | Slow ramp-up (innovator-driven) | Pure exponential from $t=0$ |
| Realism | Higher (captures both innovator and imitator) | Lower (imitator-only) |
| Use case | Durable goods, technology products | Commodities, simple repurchase goods |

**Reduction condition (1 mark):** The Bass model reduces to the pure logistic when $p \to 0$, i.e., when there are *no independent innovators* and all adoption is driven by imitation.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from the Bass closed form:

$$F(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

Take the limit $p \to 0$. Note that as $p \to 0$:

$$\frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t} \to \infty \text{ (denominator dominates)}$$

and the numerator $1 - e^{-(p+q)t} \to 1 - e^{-qt}$.

Applying L'Hôpital or algebraic manipulation:

$$F_{logistic}(t) = \lim_{p \to 0} F(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-qt}}$$

This is the **standard logistic S-curve** with carrying capacity 1 and growth rate $q$.

---

### **Part (b) — Maturity Stage Strategies and Escape from Decline** [7 marks]

**Maturity Stage Strategies (4 marks):**

A product manager has *four* primary strategic options during Maturity:

1. **Market Modification** — find new users or new uses for the product.
   *Example:* Maggi noodles launched "Maggi Atta Noodles" to target health-conscious consumers in Maturity.

2. **Product Modification** — alter features, quality, style, or packaging.
   *Example:* Maruti Suzuki launching the *Swift* with cosmetic facelifts every 3 years during its Maturity phase.

3. **Marketing Mix Modification** — change price, promotion, or distribution.
   *Example:* Coca-Cola offering discount bundles and larger pack sizes during Maturity in India.

4. **Repositioning** — alter the brand image to attract a new demographic.
   *Example:* Apple's iPod transitioned from "music player" to "lifestyle accessory" during Maturity.

**Escaping the Decline Stage (3 marks):**

A product can escape decline by **launching a successor S-curve** through one of the following:

- **Disruptive Innovation** — introduce a *lower-cost* or *new-technology* version of the product (Clayton Christensen). *Example:* Netflix's DVD-by-mail escaped Blockbuster's decline by becoming a *streaming* disruptor.
- **Adjacent Market Expansion** — *Example:* Apple iPod → iPhone → iPad → Apple Watch (continuous new S-curves).
- **Brand Reinvention** — *Example:* Lego was in decline in the early 2000s; brand reinvention through licensed themes (Star Wars, Harry Potter) reversed the curve.
- **Acquisition / Merger** — *Example:* Hindustan Unilever acquiring new brands to escape the decline of legacy soap brands.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the *S-curve* (cumulative) with the *bell curve* (rate).** The Bass equation $F(t)$ is the *cumulative* S-curve; the *bell curve* is $f(t) = dF/dt$. Mixing them up costs **2–3 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Omitting units in $t^{*}$ calculation.** Always write $t^{*} = 6.19$ **years** (or months/periods), not just "6.19". Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for missing units.
> 3. **Forgetting the Bass ODE assumption** $0 < p < 1$ and $q > 0$. If a student writes $p > q$ without justification, they lose a mark.
> 4. **Maturity is NOT the *peak sales* stage — it is the *peak profit* stage.** Sales peak in *late Growth / early Maturity*; profit peaks in *late Maturity*. This nuance is worth **1 mark** in Part A.
> 5. **Drawing a triangular PLC instead of an S-curve.** The PLC is a *smooth, S-shaped* curve (Bass-derived), not a sharp triangle. A bell-curve sketch is acceptable for $f(t)$ but not for $F(t)$.
> 6. **In the Bass derivation, students often skip the partial-fraction step.** This step is worth **2 marks**; skipping it directly loses those marks.
> 7. **Rogers' Diffusion percentages (2.5%, 13.5%, 34%, 34%, 16%) must add to 100%** — students sometimes misquote them, e.g., "innovators are 5%". The correct figure is **2.5%** (Everett Rogers, 1962).

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

### **A. Definitions (must be word-perfect)**
- ☐ **Product Life Cycle (PLC):** Time-based sequence of strategic phases from conception to discontinuation.
- ☐ **Bass Diffusion Model (1969):** Probabilistic model separating innovation ($p$) and imitation ($q$) effects.
- ☐ **Innovators (2.5%):** Independent, risk-tolerant early buyers.
- ☐ **Early Adopters (13.5%):** Opinion leaders, influencers.
- ☐ **Early Majority (34%):** Pragmatic, deliberate buyers.
- ☐ **Late Majority (34%):** Skeptical, price-sensitive, adopt under peer pressure.
- ☐ **Laggards (16%):** Traditionalists, last to switch.
- ☐ **Diffusion of Innovation (Rogers, 1962):** The bell-curve adoption pattern.
- ☐ **S-Curve (Logistic):** Cumulative adoption trajectory.
- ☐ **Cash Cow (BCG terminology):** A product in Maturity with high market share and low growth — yields maximum cash.

### **B. Stage-Wise Quick Recall (KEEP this table memorized)**

| Stage | Sales | Profit | Customers | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Introduction | Low | Negative | Innovators | Awareness, MVP |
| Growth | Rising fast | Rising | Early Adopters, Early Majority | Scale, lock in |
| Maturity | Peak, plateau | **Maximum** | Late Majority | Differentiate, extend |
| Decline | Falling | Eroding | Laggards | Harvest, re-innovate |

### **C. The Six Equations You Must Be Able to Write Blindfolded**
1. Bass ODE: $\dfrac{f(t)}{1-F(t)} = p + q F(t)$
2. Bass Cumulative: $F(t) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$
3. Peak Time: $t^{*} = \dfrac{1}{p+q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)$
4. Logistic Limit: $F_{logistic}(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-qt}}$
5. Cumulative Adopters: $N(t) = m \cdot F(t)$
6. Break-Even: $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_{BE}} [R(t) - C(t)] \, dt = 0$

### **D. Three Concepts Most Frequently Tested in KTU**
- ☐ The Bass model derivation (full marks: 7).
- ☐ Strategies to extend the Maturity stage (4 strategies: market, product, marketing-mix, repositioning).
- ☐ Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation — the five adopter categories with their **exact** percentages.

### **E. One Real-World Example for Each Stage (Memorize One)**
- ☐ **Introduction:** Apple Vision Pro (2024 launch, premium skimming).
- ☐ **Growth:** Jio (2016–2018, explosive Indian telecom adoption).
- ☐ **Maturity:** Maggi Noodles (mass-market plateau, 80%+ household penetration in India).
- ☐ **Decline:** BlackBerry phones (replaced by iOS/Android smartphones).

### **F. Common Cross-Links with Other KTU Modules**
- ☐ PLC ↔ **Design Thinking Module:** Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test maps onto Introduction stage.
- ☐ PLC ↔ **NPD Process Module:** Stage-gate processes are designed for the Introduction stage.
- ☐ PLC ↔ **IP Management Module:** Patent filing is timed during Introduction to lock in competitive advantage during Growth.
- ☐ PLC ↔ **Branding Module:** Brand equity is *built* during Growth, *harvested* during Maturity, *protected* during Decline.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"PLC is not a curve, it is a decision framework. Every stage demands a different design, marketing, and financial action. The Bass Model quantifies the curve. Rogers' diffusion explains the customers. KTU tests whether you can connect both."*

**End of Module 1 — Product Life Cycle Notes**
*Prepared under the KTU 2024 Scheme | Course PEMET837 | Product Design and Innovation*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Mix
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Product Mix: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Product Mix (also called Product Assortment or Product Portfolio)** is the complete set of all products and product lines offered for sale by a particular company or organization to its target market. It represents the strategic composition of a firm's total product offering, designed to satisfy diverse customer needs, maximize market coverage, and optimize revenue generation.

According to **Philip Kotler** (the universally accepted KTU benchmark text), the Product Mix is characterized by four critical dimensions:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Dimensions of Product Mix**
> 1. **Width (Breadth):** The number of distinct product lines a company carries.
> 2. **Length:** The total number of products (Stock Keeping Units – SKUs) within the entire mix.
> 3. **Depth:** The average number of variants, models, or versions offered within each product line.
> 4. **Consistency (Consonance):** The degree to which different product lines are related in terms of usage, production, distribution channels, or target consumers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a massive **Indian Kirana Store turned into a Modern Supermarket** 🛒:

- The store owner decides to sell **Atta (flour)**, **Rice**, **Spices**, **Toiletries**, and **Beverages** → these are 5 different **Product Lines**.
- Under Atta, he stocks **Aashirvaad**, **Pillsbury**, **Annapurna**, **Organic Tattva** → these are **variants** (depth).
- The total number of flour brands + rice brands + spice brands + ... = **Length**.
- The number of categories (Atta, Rice, Spices...) = **Width**.
- Now, is "Spices" related to "Toiletries"? Both are FMCG, sold on the same shelf, used by the same homemaker → High **Consistency**.
- If he suddenly starts selling **Laptops**, the consistency drops dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Examiners frequently test students on the ability to *calculate* and *interpret* the four dimensions given a product list. Always present your calculations as a clear table before writing the interpretation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Product Mix Engineering

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Unit / Dimension |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Width | $W$ | Count (number of lines) |
| Length | $L$ | Count (number of items/SKUs) |
| Depth | $D$ | Count (average variants per line) |
| Consistency | $C$ | Qualitative Index (0 to 1) |
| Average Depth | $\bar{D}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Dimensions: Operational Logic Breakdown

### A. Width (Breadth)
- **Why it matters:** A wider mix diversifies business risk. If one product line fails (e.g., print media), other lines (e.g., digital) sustain revenue.
- **How to measure:** Simply count the number of distinct product lines.
- **Engineering example:** Tata Motors has product lines for Cars (Nexon, Harrier), Commercial Vehicles (Trucks, Buses), and EVs (Tiago EV). Each is a separate line.

### B. Length
- **Why it matters:** Length signals the *absolute size* of the catalog. A very long product mix indicates mass-market penetration but increases inventory costs.
- **How to measure:** Sum up all individual SKUs across all product lines.

### C. Depth
- **Why it matters:** Deep product lines satisfy niche customer preferences (different sizes, colors, prices). It builds customer loyalty.
- **How to measure:** Count variants of a single product line, then average across all lines.

### D. Consistency
- **Why it matters:** High consistency means shared production facilities, distribution networks, and brand equity. Low consistency means the firm is a conglomerate.
- **How to measure:** Qualitative assessment — looking at end-use, production process, distribution channel, and customer base overlap.

## 2.2 Strategic Adjustments to the Product Mix

KTU examiners love asking *why* a company expands or contracts its mix. The four strategic levers are:

1. **Product Line Stretching:** Adding items *beyond* the current range.
   - **Down-market stretch:** Introducing cheaper versions (e.g., Toyota launching Datsun).
   - **Up-market stretch:** Launching premium versions (e.g., Samsung Galaxy S Ultra).
   - **Two-way stretch:** Both directions simultaneously.
2. **Product Line Filling:** Adding more items *within* the existing range to reduce cannibalization and capture more shelf space.
3. **Product Line Modernization:** Updating existing products with new features/technology.
4. **Product Line Pruning:** Deleting unprofitable items to focus resources.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. They appear in nearly every KTU University Exam question on Product Mix.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $W = \text{Number of Product Lines}$ | Width | Given a product list |
| 2 | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{W} N_i$ | Length = sum of items in each line | Total SKU count |
| 3 | $\bar{D} = \dfrac{L}{W}$ | Average Depth | Required for "depth" questions |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{\text{Shared Attributes}}{\text{Total Attributes Considered}}$ | Consistency Index | Conceptual/qualitative |
| 5 | $\text{Cannibalization Rate} = \dfrac{S_{\text{new}} - S_{\text{old}}}{S_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | New vs old sales | Stretching/Filling decisions |
| 6 | $\text{Sales Mix} = \dfrac{S_i}{\sum S_i} \times 100$ | Sales contribution of each product | Portfolio optimization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real product design firms, Product Mix analysis informs the *DFM (Design for Manufacturing)* budget, BOM (Bill of Materials) planning, and JIT (Just-In-Time) inventory strategies. A long mix inflates working capital requirements significantly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A mix spanning Hatchbacks, Sedans, and SUVs allows risk-hedging across fuel-price cycles.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Apple maintains a *narrow but deep* mix (few lines, deep variants) — high consistency.
- **FMCG:** Hindustan Unilever maintains an *extremely wide and long* mix — low consistency but maximum coverage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU numerical questions on Product Mix typically provide a list of products grouped into lines and ask for Width, Length, Average Depth, and a consistency interpretation. Below is a **fully solved master example** followed by analytical sub-problems.

## 3.1 Master Problem: Samsung India Product Mix

**Problem Statement:** *Samsung India offers the following products grouped into product lines:*
- **Mobiles:** Galaxy S24, Galaxy A54, Galaxy M14 (3 items)
- **Tablets:** Galaxy Tab S9, Galaxy Tab A8 (2 items)
- **Televisions:** Crystal UHD, QLED 4K, The Frame (3 items)
- **Wearable Tech:** Galaxy Watch 6, Galaxy Buds Pro (2 items)

*Calculate the Width, Length, Average Depth, and discuss Consistency.*

### Step 1: Identify Product Lines
The four lines are: Mobiles, Tablets, Televisions, Wearable Tech.

$$W = 4 \quad \text{(Width)}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Length (Total Products)
We sum the items in each line:

$$L = N_{\text{Mobiles}} + N_{\text{Tablets}} + N_{\text{TVs}} + N_{\text{Wearables}}$$

$$L = 3 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 10 \quad \text{units}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Average Depth
Average depth is the mean number of variants per product line:

$$\bar{D} = \dfrac{L}{W} = \dfrac{10}{4}$$

$$\bar{D} = 2.5 \quad \text{variants per line}$$

### Step 4: Consistency Discussion
All four product lines (Mobiles, Tablets, TVs, Wearables) are:
- **Consumer electronics** → shared production expertise (semiconductor displays, battery tech).
- Distributed through the **same retail channels** (Samsung SmartPlazas, online portals).
- Targeted at the **same tech-savvy consumer segment**.

Therefore, **Consistency is HIGH** → Samsung exhibits strong *consonance*.

---

## 3.2 Sub-Problem A: Stretching Strategy Analysis

**Problem:** *A company sells "SoftCare" detergent in one size (1 kg). It decides to introduce a 500 g pack at ₹80 and a 5 kg pack at ₹750. Identify the type of line stretching and calculate the cannibalization if the 1 kg pack sales dropped from 10,000 units to 7,000 units per month, while new pack sales are 3,000 and 1,000 units respectively.*

### Step 1: Identify Stretching Type
Adding a smaller pack (500 g) and a larger pack (5 kg) **both below and above** the current range = **Two-Way Stretch**.

### Step 2: Apply Cannibalization Formula

$$\text{Cannibalization Rate} = \dfrac{S_{\text{new}} - S_{\text{old}}}{S_{\text{old}}} \times 100$$

Here, the 1 kg sales dropped due to customers shifting to new packs:

$$\text{Cannibalization Rate} = \dfrac{7000 - 10000}{10000} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{-3000}{10000} \times 100 = -30\%$$

### Step 3: Interpretation
A **30% drop** in the original 1 kg pack sales indicates significant cannibalization. Net effect analysis:

$$\text{Net Sales Change} = (7000 + 3000 + 1000) - 10000 = 1000 \quad \text{extra units/month}$$

Conclusion: Two-way stretch succeeded in expanding the customer base despite cannibalization.

---

## 3.3 Sub-Problem B: Product Line Pruning Decision

**Problem:** *A product line has 8 variants with the following monthly profits (₹ Lakhs): 5, 8, 12, 2, 15, 1, 7, 4. Recommend pruning if a variant is unprofitable (profit < ₹3 Lakhs).*

### Step 1: Identify Pruning Candidates
Variants with profit $< 3$ Lakhs: **2 Lakhs, 1 Lakh, and ...**

Variants: 5, 8, 12, 2, 15, 1, 7, 4

Those below ₹3 Lakhs: **2 and 1**.

### Step 2: Apply Pruning Logic

$$\text{Prune Variants} = \{x_i \mid x_i < 3\} = \{2, 1\}$$

### Step 3: New Mix Statistics

$$W' = W \quad (\text{same line})$$

$$L' = L - 2 = 8 - 2 = 6$$

$$\bar{D}' = \dfrac{6}{1} = 6 \quad \text{(if single line considered)}$$

Pruning frees up manufacturing capacity and shelf space for the high-performing variants (12 and 15 Lakh).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Product Mix Architecture (Block-Level Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Company Product Mix] --> B[Product Line 1]
    A --> C[Product Line 2]
    A --> D[Product Line 3]
    B --> B1[Variant A1]
    B --> B2[Variant A2]
    B --> B3[Variant A3]
    C --> C1[Variant B1]
    C --> C2[Variant B2]
    D --> D1[Variant C1]
    D --> D2[Variant C2]
    D --> D3[Variant C3]

    A4[Width = 3 Lines] -.-> A
    A5[Length = 8 Products] -.-> A
    A6[Depth varies per line] -.-> A
    A7[Consistency: How related?] -.-> A

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#E0FFFF
    style B2 fill:#E0FFFF
    style B3 fill:#E0FFFF
    style C1 fill:#E0FFFF
    style C2 fill:#E0FFFF
    style D1 fill:#E0FFFF
    style D2 fill:#E0FFFF
    style D3 fill:#E0FFFF
```

## 4.2 Strategic Adjustment Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Identify Product Mix Issue] --> Q1{Is the line underperforming?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Prune or Revitalize?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is market demand shifting?}
    Q2 -- Prune --> R1[Product Line Pruning]
    Q2 -- Revitalize --> R2[Product Line Modernization]
    Q3 -- Upward --> R3[Up-market Stretch]
    Q3 -- Downward --> R4[Down-market Stretch]
    Q3 -- Both --> R5[Two-way Stretch]
    Q3 -- No shift --> R6[Product Line Filling]
    R1 --> End[Optimized Product Mix]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
    R5 --> End
    R6 --> End

    style Start fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style End fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Four-Dimensions Relationship Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    W[Width W] --- L[Length L]
    L --- D[Avg Depth D-bar]
    W --- C[Consistency C]
    D --- C

    W -- Diversification --> S1[Risk Hedging]
    L -- Total SKUs --> S2[Inventory Cost]
    D -- Variants --> S3[Customer Choice]
    C -- Relatedness --> S4[Brand Synergy]

    style W fill:#FF6347
    style L fill:#4682B4
    style D fill:#32CD32
    style C fill:#9370DB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Product Mix. List its four key dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**
Product Mix is the complete set of all products and product lines offered by a company for sale. Its four dimensions are:

1. **Width** – number of product lines.
2. **Length** – total number of products (SKUs).
3. **Depth** – number of variants per product line.
4. **Consistency** – degree of relatedness among product lines.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing dimensions)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Product Line Stretching and Product Line Filling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stretching | Filling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Adds products **beyond** the current range | Adds products **within** the current range |
| Purpose | Reach new customer segments | Reduce cannibalization, capture more shelf space |
| Example | Honda launching City beyond existing variants | Honda launching 3 more City trims in same range |

*(3 marks: 2 for tabular distinction + 1 example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define Product Mix. Explain its four dimensions with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company has 3 product lines with the following products:
- Line 1: A, B, C, D (4 items)
- Line 2: P, Q (2 items)
- Line 3: X, Y, Z, W, V (5 items)

Calculate the Width, Length, and Average Depth. Comment on the Consistency if all three lines are sold through the same distributor and target homemakers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
Product Mix is the strategic combination of all product lines and individual products offered by a firm to its target market, designed to satisfy diverse customer needs and achieve business objectives.

**Four Dimensions with Example (5 marks):**
- **Width:** Number of product lines. Example: ITC has Width = 5 (Cigarettes, FMCG, Hotels, Paperboards, Agri-Business).
- **Length:** Total SKUs across all lines.
- **Depth:** Variants per line. Example: Britannia Marie Gold has depths of size (250g, 500g, 1kg) and flavors.
- **Consistency:** Relatedness. Example: Maruti Suzuki has HIGH consistency (all cars); Reliance has LOW consistency (Jio, Retail, Petrochemicals).

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Width:**
$$W = 3 \quad \text{lines}$$

**Length:**
$$L = 4 + 2 + 5 = 11 \quad \text{products}$$

**Average Depth:**
$$\bar{D} = \dfrac{L}{W} = \dfrac{11}{3} \approx 3.67$$

**Consistency Comment:**
All three lines share the **same distribution channel** and **same target customer (homemakers)** → Consistency is **HIGH**, indicating strong consonance and brand synergy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the different strategic adjustments a company can make to its Product Mix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A detergent brand sells its 1 kg pack at ₹150 with monthly sales of 20,000 units. The company introduces a 2 kg pack at ₹280 and a 500 g pack at ₹80. After launch, the 1 kg sales fall to 12,000 units, while the 2 kg and 500 g packs sell 6,000 and 4,000 units respectively. Calculate the cannibalization rate and net sales change. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

The four strategic adjustments are:

1. **Product Line Stretching (3 marks):**
   - Down-market stretch (cheaper variants).
   - Up-market stretch (premium variants).
   - Two-way stretch.

2. **Product Line Filling (2 marks):**
   - Adding items *within* the existing range.

3. **Product Line Modernization (1 mark):**
   - Updating technology/features.

4. **Product Line Pruning (1 mark):**
   - Deleting unprofitable items.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Cannibalization Rate of 1 kg Pack:**

$$\text{Cannibalization Rate} = \dfrac{12000 - 20000}{20000} \times 100 = \dfrac{-8000}{20000} \times 100 = -40\%$$

**Net Sales Change (Units):**

$$\text{Net Change} = (12000 + 6000 + 4000) - 20000 = 22000 - 20000 = +2000 \text{ units/month}$$

**Net Revenue Change:**
- Old Revenue: $20000 \times 150 = ₹30,00,000$
- New Revenue: $(12000 \times 150) + (6000 \times 280) + (4000 \times 80)$
- $= ₹18,00,000 + ₹16,80,000 + ₹3,20,000 = ₹38,00,000$

$$\text{Net Revenue Gain} = ₹38,00,000 - ₹30,00,000 = ₹8,00,000$$

**Conclusion:** Despite 40% cannibalization, net revenue grew by ₹8 Lakhs → Successful **two-way stretch**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Width with Length.** Width = number of *lines*; Length = total *products*. Marks lost if interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting units in depth calculation.** Always write "variants per line."
> 3. **In numerical problems, ignoring cannibalization impact.** Examiners expect both *rate* and *net change*.
> 4. **Treating Consistency as numerical.** It is qualitative — write a descriptive sentence, not a number (unless calculating an index).
> 5. **Not relating to real-world examples.** At least one Indian brand example fetches extra marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Product Mix = Total offering** of a firm (lines + products).
- ✅ **Four dimensions:** Width, Length, Depth, Consistency.
- ✅ **Width = # of Lines.**
- ✅ **Length = Total SKUs (sum across all lines).**
- ✅ **Average Depth = Length ÷ Width.**
- ✅ **Consistency is qualitative** — based on relatedness of use, production, and distribution.
- ✅ **Stretching:** Down-market, Up-market, Two-way.
- ✅ **Filling:** Within range; **Pruning:** Deleting; **Modernization:** Updating.
- ✅ **Cannibalization formula:** $\frac{S_{\text{new}} - S_{\text{old}}}{S_{\text{old}}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Always give Indian examples** (Tata, ITC, HUL, Samsung, Maruti) — KTU examiners reward contextual answers.
- ✅ **Formula priority order in exams:** Width → Length → Depth → Consistency comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Product Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Product Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Product Design** is the holistic, multidisciplinary process of conceptualizing, creating, and iterating a tangible good or service that satisfies identified user needs, market opportunities, and organizational objectives. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme framing, product design sits at the intersection of **engineering sciences, human factors (ergonomics), industrial aesthetics, manufacturing feasibility, and commercial viability**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"Product Design is the systematic, iterative problem-solving activity that integrates user requirements, functional specifications, form-giving aesthetics, ergonomic considerations, material selection, manufacturing processes, cost constraints, and sustainability metrics to deliver a market-ready artifact or system."*

Formally, the design intent can be expressed as a multi-objective optimization mapping:

$$ D^{*} = \arg\min_{D \in \mathcal{F}} \left[ w_1 \cdot C(D) + w_2 \cdot T(D) - w_3 \cdot U(D) + w_4 \cdot E(D) \right] $$

Where:
- $D^{*}$ = Optimal Design Solution
- $\mathcal{F}$ = Feasible Design Space
- $C(D)$ = Cost Function, $T(D)$ = Time-to-Market
- $U(D)$ = User Value, $E(D)$ = Environmental Impact
- $w_i$ = Strategic weighting factors

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of **Product Design as Cooking a Signature Dish for a Wedding Banquet**:
- The **head chef (designer)** doesn't just follow a recipe — they understand the **guests' palates (user needs)**, the **available kitchen (manufacturing constraints)**, the **budget (cost)**, and the **presentation (aesthetics)**.
- The dish must **taste good (function)**, **look beautiful (form)**, be **easy to eat (ergonomics)**, and arrive **on time (delivery)**.
- A great chef iterates — taste-testing, plating, refining — exactly like a product designer prototyping and testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A product is not just an "object." It is a **bundle of utility, experience, identity, and value** that has been deliberately engineered to fit into a human life.

## 1.3 Why Product Design Matters in the 2024 Industry Landscape

> [!NOTE]
> In the **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 outcome-based framework**, product design is no longer an "elective" — it is a **core competency** for every engineer because:
> 1. **~85% of product failures** are attributable to poor design decisions, not manufacturing defects.
> 2. Modern industry demands **Design Thinking + AI + Sustainability** as a unified discipline.
> 3. KTU's B.Tech graduate attributes (GA5: Modern Tool Usage, GA9: Individual & Team Work) explicitly require design literacy.

---

## 1.4 Core Constituents of a "Product"

A product, in the broadest KTU-recognized sense, comprises **five concentric layers** (popularized by Philip Kotler, adopted in KTU management modules):

| Layer | Description | Example (Smartphone) |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Product** | Fundamental benefit | Communication & connectivity |
| **Generic Product** | Basic functional version | A working phone with dialer |
| **Expected Product** | Standard features a buyer assumes | Battery, screen, charger |
| **Augmented Product** | Differentiators beyond expectation | AI camera, cloud backup |
| **Potential Product** | Future evolution direction | Holographic display, neural I/O |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners often test the 5-layer model** in Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize the hierarchy *from inside-out*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8 Pillars of Product Design (KTU High-Yield Framework)

A KTU examiner expects a student to articulate product design through **eight interlocking pillars**. Each pillar is a *design knob* the engineer must turn:

1. **Functionality** — What the product *does* (the problem it solves).
2. **Reliability** — How consistently it performs over its lifecycle (MTBF metrics).
3. **Usability** — How intuitively a user can operate it (learnability, efficiency).
4. **Aesthetics** — Visual, tactile, and emotional appeal.
5. **Ergonomics** — Fit with human anatomy, cognition, and biomechanics.
6. **Manufacturability (DFM)** — Ease of fabrication with available processes.
7. **Sustainability** — Environmental footprint, recyclability, lifecycle impact.
8. **Affordability** — Cost-to-produce vs. perceived value alignment.

## 2.2 Product Classification Matrix

Products are classified along multiple axes — **KTU examiners love this matrix** as it appears in nearly every prior year paper:

### A. By User Type
- **Consumer Products** — Bought by end-users for personal use (e.g., a water bottle).
- **Industrial/B2B Products** — Bought for business operations (e.g., CNC machine, forklift).
- **Specialty Products** — Unique, high-involvement purchases (e.g., luxury watches, drones).

### B. By Tangibility
- **Tangible Goods** — Physical artifacts.
- **Intangible Services** — Design consulting, SaaS platforms.
- **Hybrid Products** — Physical + digital (e.g., Tesla car + software subscription).

### C. By Lifecycle Stage
- **New / Introduction** — Newly launched, low awareness, high cost.
- **Growth** — Rising adoption, economies of scale kicking in.
- **Maturity** — Peak sales, intense competition, price wars.
- **Decline** — Falling demand, substitution, harvest/discontinue decision.

## 2.3 The Concept of "Design" — Narrow vs. Broad Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Narrow (Engineering) Definition:** Design is the *technical act* of specifying geometry, materials, tolerances, and processes to meet an engineering specification.
>
> **Broad (Industrial/Product) Definition:** Design is the *strategic, human-centered, multidisciplinary act* of conceiving an artifact that delivers *value* across its full lifecycle.

**KTU PEMET837 explicitly follows the BROAD definition** — this is critical for framing answers.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design Optimization Objective** | $D^* = \arg\min f(D)$ subject to $g_i(D) \le 0$ | $f$ = objective, $g_i$ = constraints |
| 2 | **House of Quality (HOQ) Weighting** | $W_j = \sum_{i} r_{ij} \cdot w_i$ | $r_{ij}$ = relationship strength, $w_i$ = customer importance |
| 3 | **Design for X (DFX) Index** | $\text{DFX Score} = \frac{\sum_{k} s_k \cdot \alpha_k}{\sum_{k} \alpha_k}$ | $s_k$ = sub-score, $\alpha_k$ = weight for X-attribute |
| 4 | **Product Life Cycle (PLC) Revenue** | $R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda(t-t^*)^2}$ | Gaussian sales curve, $\lambda$ = decay rate |
| 5 | **Ergonomic Strain Index** | $\text{SI} = \text{EM} \times \text{DM} \times \text{IM} \times \text{CM}$ | Multipliers of effort × duration × intensity × cycles |
| 6 | **Cost-of-Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = C_p + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_o(t) + C_m(t)}{(1+r)^t}$ | $C_p$ = purchase, $C_o$ = operating, $C_m$ = maintenance, $r$ = discount rate |
| 7 | **Failure Rate (Bathtub Curve)** | $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 + \alpha t + \beta e^{-\gamma t}$ | Early-life, random, wear-out phases |
| 8 | **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $\text{CSI} = \frac{\sum (\text{Importance}_i \times \text{Satisfaction}_i)}{N}$ | $N$ = number of attributes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Use the **TCO equation** in any 14-mark answer that mentions "affordability" or "lifecycle costing" — it is a high-scoring, formula-rich inclusion.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti):** Uses the 5-layer model to position hatchbacks vs. SUVs.
- **Medical Devices (e.g., GE Healthcare, Siemens):** Heavy reliance on ergonomics pillar + DFM (Design for Manufacturability).
- **Consumer Electronics (Apple, Samsung):** Mastery of aesthetics + usability drives premium pricing.
