#### Step 5 — Sample Three Concept Variants (Combinations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \;&=\; \big(v_{1,1}\big) \times \big(v_{2,1}\big) \times \big(v_{3,1}\big) \times \big(v_{4,4}\big) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Standard electric drip coffee maker in a paper cup} \\
C_2 \;&=\; \big(v_{1,4}\big) \times \big(v_{2,3}\big) \times \big(v_{3,5}\big) \times \big(v_{4,5}\big) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Foldable bladder heated by candle and squeezed into a travel mug (AeroPress-style)} \\
C_3 \;&=\; \big(v_{1,2}\big) \times \big(v_{2,5}\big) \times \big(v_{3,2}\big) \times \big(v_{4,2}\big) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Removable bottle heated by solar dish and pressurized by a hand pump into a flask (off-grid design)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Notice that **none** of these concepts are the obvious "electric kettle" because the method forces the engineer to consider even unconventional paths. This is the *exact behaviour* KTU examiners want described in a 14-mark Part B answer.

#### Step 6 — Evaluation and Concept Selection

The 625 concepts are screened against a **weighted evaluation matrix** (a concept that scores high on Cost, Time, Safety, and Sustainability is taken to the next phase). The chosen concept is then developed into a *principle solution*, *embodiment*, and *detail design* — which are the later modules of PEMET837.

### 3.2 Analytical Insight — Why the Formula is a Lower Bound

$$
N_{concepts}^{\,actual} \;\geq\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} m_i
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The product formula counts only *one-variant-per-column* combinations. In advanced morphological analysis, designers also explore *combinations of two variants per column* (hybrid concepts), which inflates $N_{concepts}$ further. Always use the "$\geq$" sign in viva-voce answers to demonstrate advanced understanding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow of Morphological Analysis

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete procedural architecture** of the Zwicky method. Each node is a logical step, and the arrows indicate information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Design Brief F_total] --> B[Step 2: Functional Decomposition]
    B --> C{Independent sub-functions f1 to fn?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Step 3: List Variants for each fi]
    C -- No --> E[Refine decomposition to ensure orthogonality]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Step 4: Construct Morphological Chart]
    F --> G[Step 5: Compute N_concepts = product of mi]
    G --> H[Step 6: Generate Concept Variants by 1-per-column selection]
    H --> I[Step 7: Screen against evaluation criteria]
    I --> J{Viable concept found?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Output: Selected Concept to Embodiment Phase]
    J -- No --> L[Re-iterate: add more variants or refine fi]
    L --> D
```

*Reading the diagram:* The chart has a single feedback loop between *Step 7* and *Step 3*. This is intentional — a real morphological session is **iterative**, not a one-shot linear process. KTU expects this loop to be mentioned in a 14-mark question.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Morphological Chart

The following Mermaid block renders the **internal structure** of the chart itself, mapping how the parameters and variants inter-relate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARAMS[Independent Design Parameters]
        P1[f1: Sub-function 1]
        P2[f2: Sub-function 2]
        P3[f3: Sub-function 3]
        P4[f4: Sub-function 4]
    end
    subgraph VAR1[Variants for f1]
        V1A[v1,1]
        V1B[v1,2]
        V1C[v1,3]
    end
    subgraph VAR2[Variants for f2]
        V2A[v2,1]
        V2B[v2,2]
        V2C[v2,3]
    end
    subgraph VAR3[Variants for f3]
        V3A[v3,1]
        V3B[v3,2]
        V3C[v3,3]
    end
    subgraph VAR4[Variants for f4]
        V4A[v4,1]
        V4B[v4,2]
        V4C[v4,3]
    end
    CONCEPT[Concept Variant Ck: One selection from each column]
    P1 --- VAR1
    P2 --- VAR2
    P3 --- VAR3
    P4 --- VAR4
    VAR1 --> CONCEPT
    VAR2 --> CONCEPT
    VAR3 --> CONCEPT
    VAR4 --> CONCEPT
```

*Reading the diagram:* The four columns feed independently into the central **Concept Variant** node. This visually demonstrates the *one-per-column* rule that defines every concept in the morphological method.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Morphological Analysis in product design. Who introduced this method and in which year?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Morphological Analysis, also called the **Zwicky Method**, is a systematic, non-quantitative technique of concept generation in which a complex design problem is decomposed into **independent functional parameters**, and all feasible **variants (working principles)** for each parameter are listed in a *Morphological Chart*. Concepts are then formed by selecting **one variant from each column**. The method was introduced by the Swiss astrophysicist **Fritz Zwicky** in the year **1942** while classifying jet-propulsion concepts and astronomical phenomena. Its key advantage is that it defeats *design fixation* and reveals the true size of the design space. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the formula to compute the total number of concepts from a morphological chart with $n$ independent parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The total number of distinct concepts that can be generated from a morphological chart is given by the **product of variants** in each column:

$$
N_{concepts} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} m_i \;=\; m_1 \times m_2 \times m_3 \times \dots \times m_n
$$

where $n$ is the number of independent parameters and $m_i$ is the number of variants in the $i$-th column. For example, a chart with 4 parameters and 5 variants per column yields $5 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 = 625$ concepts. The formula holds **only when the parameters are independent (orthogonal)**. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Morphology of Design**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks):** Writing *only* a chart without first stating the **overall function** and the **functional decomposition**. Examiners explicitly award 2 marks for "Problem Definition" and "Functional Decomposition".
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks):** Drawing a coupled chart (e.g., columns that depend on each other). Always state in your answer: *"The parameters are mutually independent; choosing a variant in one column does not constrain the choice in another."*
> - **Pitfall 3 (–2 marks):** Forgetting the **formula** $N_{concepts} = \prod m_i$. Even a 14-mark answer is incomplete without this expression.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–1 mark):** Confusing the term *Parameter* (a function) with the term *Variant* (a means). Use the exact KTU keywords: *Parameter = Function/Sub-function*; *Variant = Working Principle/Means*.

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the concept of **Morphology of Design** with reference to the Zwicky method. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** in the product-design process. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A team is tasked with designing a **low-cost, portable, manually-operated water purifier for rural school children**. Construct a complete **Morphological Chart** for the same and compute the total number of concepts it can generate. Clearly state the **overall function**, the **sub-functions**, and the **selected working principles** for at least **three distinct concept variants**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) Concept of Morphology of Design (7 Marks):**

Morphology of Design, or **Morphological Analysis**, is a structured, combinatorial concept-generation technique introduced by **Fritz Zwicky** in **1942**. It is used in the **conceptual design phase** of a product's life cycle. The key idea is to systematically break a complex overall function $F_{total}$ into $n$ independent sub-functions $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n$ (called *parameters*), list $m_i$ candidate working principles (called *variants*) for each sub-function, and arrange these in a tabular grid called the **Morphological Chart (Zwicky Box)**. Total concepts are formed by picking exactly one variant from each column.

**Advantages** (≈ 4 marks): *(i)* **Defeats design fixation** by forcing the designer to list unconventional variants; *(ii)* **Quantifies the design space** through the formula $N_{concepts} = \prod m_i$; *(iii)* **Encourages cross-domain innovation** by recombining principles from different fields; *(iv)* **Easy to communicate visually** to non-technical stakeholders; *(v)* **Inexpensive and quick** — no CAD or prototyping is required; *(vi)* **Excellent for group brainstorming** in a classroom or industry setting.

**Limitations** (≈ 2 marks): *(i)* **Combinatorial explosion** — a chart with 6 parameters and 5 variants per column gives $5^6 = 15{,}625$ concepts, most of which are infeasible; *(ii)* **Coupled parameters** reduce the validity of the multiplicative formula; *(iii)* The method is **purely qualitative** and does not optimize within the design space; *(iv)* Quality of output depends on the **designer's domain knowledge**; *(v)* Many combinations may be physically impossible to realize.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Despite its limitations, morphological analysis is one of the most powerful **systematic creativity tools** in the early design phase and is a prerequisite for the more rigorous concept-evaluation and selection methods taught in the next modules.

---

**(b) Morphological Chart for a Rural Water Purifier (7 Marks):**

**Overall Function:**
$$
F_{total} \;=\; \text{“Convert 1 L of turbid pond/river water into safe, drinkable water for a school child, manually, in } < 10 \text{ min, at } < \text{₹ 300.”}
$$

**Functional Decomposition (Independent Parameters):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &\;=\; \text{Intake the raw water} \\
f_2 &\;=\; \text{Remove suspended solids (sediment, mud)} \\
f_3 &\;=\; \text{Kill or trap pathogens (bacteria, virus)} \\
f_4 &\;=\; \text{Dispense the purified water safely}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Morphological Chart (Variants):**

| $f_1$ Intake | $f_2$ Sediment removal | $f_3$ Pathogen removal | $f_4$ Dispense |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_{1,1}$ Hand-pump suction | $v_{2,1}$ Cloth/fabric strainer | $v_{3,1}$ UV-C LED lamp | $v_{4,1}$ Open steel tumbler |
| $v_{1,2}$ Bucket + pour | $v_{2,2}$ Sand + gravel column | $v_{3,2}$ Ceramic candle filter | $v_{4,2}$ Sealed PET bottle |
| $v_{1,3}$ Pressurized foot pump | $v_{2,3}$ Folded paper (coffee-filter type) | $v_{3,3}$ Boiling over wood | $v_{4,3}$ Tap with raised spout |
| $v_{1,4}$ Siphon with rope pulley | $v_{2,4}$ Settling tank + decanting | $v_{3,4}$ Activated charcoal adsorption | $v_{4,4}$ Coconut-shell cup |
| $v_{1,5}$ Capillary cloth wick | $v_{2,5}$ Centrifugal hand-cranked spinner | $v_{3,5}$ Solar disinfection (SODIS) bottle | $v_{4,5}$ Biodegradable leaf bowl |

**Computing the Design Space:**
$$
N_{concepts} \;=\; 5 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 \;=\; 625 \text{ total concepts}
$$

**Three Sample Concept Variants:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \;&=\; v_{1,2} \times v_{2,1} \times v_{3,3} \times v_{4,4} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pour-through cloth filter, boil in pot, serve in coconut cup (ultra-low-cost)} \\
C_2 \;&=\; v_{1,3} \times v_{2,3} \times v_{3,2} \times v_{4,3} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Foot-pump driven paper-filter and ceramic candle with a tap (mechanical, reusable)} \\
C_3 \;&=\; v_{1,5} \times v_{2,2} \times v_{3,5} \times v_{4,2} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cloth-wick-fed sand column into a SODIS PET bottle (solar, zero-electricity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note for the examiner:* The student has *visibly* demonstrated the four hallmarks of a top-scoring morphology answer — (i) Overall function stated; (ii) Independent parameters listed; (iii) Five variants per column to demonstrate space; (iv) Three distinct concepts selected. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Differentiate between **Morphological Analysis** and the **Design Structure Matrix (DSM)** as tools used in the product design process. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Construct a complete **Morphological Chart** for the design of a **low-cost, manually-operated, portable table fan suitable for a KTU examination hall during a power cut**. Compute the total number of concepts and list **three distinct concept variants** with their working principles. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B:**

**(a) Morphological Analysis vs Design Structure Matrix (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Morphological Analysis | Design Structure Matrix (DSM) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Purpose | **Generate** a wide set of new product concepts. | **Model and analyze** dependencies *between* components/tasks of an existing or proposed product. |
| Origin | Fritz Zwicky, **1942** (Zwicky Box). | Steward, **1981**. |
| Type of Input | A *function structure* and brainstorming of working principles. | A *component list* and their pairwise interaction data. |
| Visual Form | A 2-D table of parameters (rows) and variants (columns). | An $n \times n$ matrix where off-diagonal marks indicate dependencies. |
| Mathematical Basis | Combinatorics: $N_{concepts} = \prod m_i$. | Graph theory and matrix algebra; partitioning/clustering algorithms (e.g., DSM clustering, tearing). |
| Phase of Use | **Early conceptual design** (idea generation). | **Embodiment / detailed design** (architecture optimization). |
| Output | Concept variants to be prototyped. | An optimized product architecture, module boundaries, and a reduced iteration loop. |
| Strength | Defeats design fixation, ensures completeness of *idea space*. | Reveals hidden feedback loops, enables modularization, reduces engineering change propagation. |
| Limitation | Combinatorial explosion; qualitative only. | Requires a well-defined component list; less useful in fuzzy front-end. |

*Conclusion (1 mark):* Both are *systematic* design tools, but they operate at *different phases* of the design process — morphology is for *idea generation*, DSM is for *architecture optimization*. Modern industry uses both in sequence.

---

**(b) Morphological Chart for a Portable Table Fan (7 Marks):**

**Overall Function:**
$$
F_{total} \;=\; \text{“Deliver a cooling breeze to a single student seated at a desk for } 2 \text{ hours, without mains power, costing } < \text{₹ 400.”}
$$

**Functional Decomposition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &\;=\; \text{Generate rotational motion (prime mover)} \\
f_2 &\;=\; \text{Convert rotation to airflow} \\
f_3 &\;=\; \text{Direct / focus the airflow} \\
f_4 &\;=\; \text{Supply the energy for } 2 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Morphological Chart:**

| $f_1$ Prime Mover | $f_2$ Air-flow conversion | $f_3$ Airflow Direction | $f_4$ Energy Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_{1,1}$ Hand-cranked gear | $v_{2,1}$ 3-blade plastic propeller | $v_{3,1}$ Fixed front grille | $v_{4,1}$ Rechargeable Li-ion battery |
| $v_{1,2}$ Foot-pedal slider | $v_{2,2}$ 4-blade soft fabric paddle | $v_{3,2}$ Oscillating gear | $v_{4,2}$ 3 V solar panel + buffer battery |
| $v_{1,3}$ Elastic rubber-band wind-up | $v_{2,3}$ Centrifugal impeller | $v_{3,3}$ Adjustable tilt bracket | $v_{4,3}$ USB-C power bank |
| $v_{1,4}$ Squeeze-handle piston | $v_{2,4}$ Squirrel-cage blower | $v_{3,4}$ Flexible gooseneck duct | $v_{4,4}$ Spring-wound mainspring |
| $v_{1,5}$ Thermoelectric (Peltier + ΔT) driver | $v_{2,5}$ Helical screw fan | $v_{3,5}$ 360° ring duct | $v_{4,5}$ Hand-pumped air reservoir |

**Design Space:**
$$
N_{concepts} \;=\; 5 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 \;=\; 625 \text{ concepts}
$$

**Three Concept Variants:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \;&=\; v_{1,1} \times v_{2,1} \times v_{3,2} \times v_{4,3} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Hand-cranked oscillating 3-blade fan powered by a USB power bank (eco-friendly)} \\
C_2 \;&=\; v_{1,3} \times v_{2,2} \times v_{3,5} \times v_{4,4} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Wind-up spring-driven 4-blade fabric paddle with a 360° ring (silent, exam-friendly)} \\
C_3 \;&=\; v_{1,2} \times v_{2,4} \times v_{3,4} \times v_{4,1} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Foot-pedal-powered squirrel-cage blower on a flexible duct, battery-buffered (sustained breeze)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note for the examiner:* The student has demonstrated **functional independence** (e.g., choosing a hand crank does not force a specific propeller type), making the multiplicative formula valid. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall**
> - **Originator & Year** — Fritz Zwicky, 1942 (Switzerland) — *must be memorized*.
> - **Synonyms** — Morphological Analysis, Zwicky Method, Zwicky Box, Morphological Chart — *all accepted*.
> - **Design Phase** — Used in the **Conceptual Design / Embodiment** phase (Pahl & Beitz terminology).
> - **Core Output** — A *Morphological Chart* with parameters as columns and variants as rows.
> - **Golden Formula** — $N_{concepts} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} m_i$.
> - **Independence Rule** — Parameters must be *orthogonal*; otherwise the formula collapses.
> - **One-Variant-Per-Column** — A *Concept Variant* is exactly one variant chosen from every column.
> - **Strengths** — Combats *design fixation*; quantifies the *design space*; enables *cross-domain innovation*; cheap and visual.
> - **Weaknesses** — Combinatorial explosion; coupled parameters break the formula; qualitative only; output depends on designer knowledge.
> - **Comparison Anchor** — *Morphology = idea-generation* tool; *DSM = architecture-optimization* tool.
> - **Syllabus Tag** — Falls under *Systematic Concept Generation* in Module 1 of PEMET837.
> - **Examiner's Word Triggers** — Use *"Zwicky Box"*, *"Working Principles"*, *"Concept Variants"*, *"Independence of parameters"* to score language-precision marks.
> - **Common Mistake** — Confusing **Parameter (function)** with **Variant (means)**; using only 1–2 variants per column and producing a trivial chart.
> - **Exam Tip** — For a 7-mark chart question, always include **5 variants per column** and **3 sample concepts** to demonstrate both depth and breadth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethics in Product Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethics in Product Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethics in Product Design** is the systematic application of moral principles, professional codes of conduct, and value-based decision-making frameworks throughout the entire product lifecycle — from conceptualization and prototyping to manufacturing, distribution, usage, and end-of-life disposal — to ensure that the resulting product is **safe, socially responsible, environmentally sustainable, legally compliant, and respectful of human dignity and cultural diversity.**

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, ethics in product design forms the foundational **Course Outcome (CO1)** layer, where students are expected to demonstrate awareness of professional responsibility and the impact of engineering solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

- **Professional Ethics:** A code of conduct governing the behaviour of designers, engineers, and managers based on principles of honesty, integrity, fairness, and accountability.
- **Moral Responsibility:** The obligation of a designer to ensure their product does not cause harm to users, society, or the environment.
- **Stakeholder Theory:** The ethical framework that requires a designer to consider the interests of *all* parties affected by a product — not just the end customer or the company.
- **Design for Sustainability (DfS):** An ethical design philosophy that mandates minimizing ecological footprint, optimizing resource utilization, and enabling circular economy principles.
- **Informed Consent (in User Research):** The ethical principle requiring that users participating in design research are fully aware of, and voluntarily agree to, the data collection process.
- **Conflict of Interest:** A situation where a designer's personal or financial interests could compromise their professional judgment.
- **Whistleblowing:** The act of reporting unethical, illegal, or unsafe design/manufacturing practices to appropriate authorities.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Doctor vs The Designer"**
>
> Imagine a doctor prescribing a medicine. Before writing the prescription, the doctor considers: Is it safe? Does the patient understand the side-effects? Is the cost fair? Is it the right medicine for *this* patient, or did a pharmaceutical company offer a bribe to recommend an expensive alternative?
>
> A product designer operates under the **exact same moral obligations**, except their "patient" is the **entire society** and their "prescription" is a product that will touch millions of lives.
>
> Just as a doctor follows the **Hippocratic Oath** ("First, do no harm"), a product designer is ethically bound by an unwritten **"Designer's Oath"** — the product must not harm the user physically, psychologically, financially, or environmentally. When a company designs a low-cost car with faulty brakes to maximize profit, it is the **engineering and design equivalent of medical malpractice.**

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Design Ethics

Every ethical product design decision rests on **three foundational pillars**, often visualized as an intersecting triangle:

1. **People (Social Ethics):** Human safety, accessibility, inclusivity, cultural sensitivity, user privacy, and labour rights.
2. **Planet (Environmental Ethics):** Carbon footprint, material sustainability, biodegradability, recyclability, and pollution prevention.
3. **Profit (Economic Ethics):** Fair pricing, honest marketing, transparent supply chains, and avoidance of planned obsolescence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A design that satisfies *only one* pillar is considered **ethically incomplete**. Sustainable design excellence requires the convergence of all three — this is often referred to as the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** concept.

## 1.5 The Significance of Ethics in the KTU Curriculum

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists ethics as a mandatory component of Module 1 of Product Design and Innovation (PEMET837). This is not a peripheral topic — it is the **moral foundation** upon which all subsequent modules (Design Thinking, Prototyping, IP, etc.) are built. Without a strong ethical compass, the technical skills taught in later modules can be weaponized for harm (e.g., designing addictive algorithms, unsafe medical devices, or environmentally destructive packaging).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Ethical Product Design Decision-Making Framework

Designers rarely face a single "right" or "wrong" choice. Instead, they navigate **ethical dilemmas** — situations where two or more moral principles conflict. The following structured framework, derived from classical engineering ethics literature, provides a systematic approach.

### Step-by-Step Ethical Analysis Process

1. **Identify the Stakeholders:** Map every individual, group, or entity affected by the design decision. *Examples: end users, factory workers, local communities, future generations, shareholders, the natural ecosystem.*

2. **Recognize the Ethical Issues:** Determine which moral principles are at stake. *Examples: safety vs cost, privacy vs personalization, environmental protection vs economic growth.*

3. **Gather Relevant Facts:** Collect empirical data on risks, alternatives, costs, technical feasibility, and legal requirements. *An ethical decision not grounded in fact is mere opinion.*

4. **Evaluate Alternatives Against Ethical Theories:** Apply one or more of the following ethical lenses:
   - **Utilitarian Approach:** Choose the action that produces the *greatest good for the greatest number*.
   - **Deontological (Duty-Based) Approach:** Choose the action that fulfils a moral duty, *regardless of outcome* (e.g., the duty to never lie, the duty to protect human life).
   - **Virtue Ethics Approach:** Choose the action that a *morally exemplary person* (a "good designer") would take.
   - **Rights-Based Approach:** Choose the action that best protects the fundamental *rights* of all stakeholders (right to safety, right to privacy, right to information).

5. **Make the Decision and Justify It:** Select the most ethically defensible option and document the reasoning in a **Design Ethics Logbook** (an industry best practice for traceability).

6. **Implement, Monitor, and Reflect:** After deployment, continuously monitor the product's real-world impact. *Ethics is iterative, not a one-time checkbox.*

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Pedagogical Breakdown

| Stage | Why it Matters | How to Execute in Practice |
|---|---|---|
| Stakeholder Mapping | Hidden stakeholders (e.g., waste pickers, future children) are often harmed by design choices. | Use a *Stakeholder Power-Interest Grid* during early design phases. |
| Issue Recognition | Designers suffer from *moral blindness* — the inability to see the ethical dimension. | Conduct mandatory *Ethics Impact Assessments (EIA)* alongside feasibility studies. |
| Fact Gathering | Decisions made on emotion or assumption are fragile. | Engage cross-functional teams: legal, environmental science, sociology. |
| Theory Application | Different stakeholders value different theories (shareholders = utilitarian; activists = rights-based). | Apply *Principled Negotiation* to find common ground. |
| Documentation | Verbal justifications are easily forgotten or denied. | Maintain a *Design Decision Rationale Document*. |
| Post-Launch Review | Real-world harm may only emerge years later. | Schedule *3-year, 5-year, and 10-year ethical impact reviews*. |

## 2.3 The Twelve Core Principles of Ethical Product Design

The following principles synthesize globally recognized frameworks (IEEE, ACM, NSPE, UN SDG) into a unified checklist tailored for KTU students:

1. **Safety First (Non-Negotiable):** A product must never knowingly endanger human life.
2. **Honesty in Marketing:** Claims made about a product must be truthful, verifiable, and not misleading.
3. **User Privacy and Data Protection:** Personal data is sacred; collect only what is necessary, store it securely, and never sell it without explicit consent.
4. **Inclusivity and Accessibility:** Design for users of *all* abilities, ages, genders, body types, and socioeconomic backgrounds.
5. **Environmental Stewardship:** Minimize carbon footprint, use renewable or recycled materials, and design for disassembly.
6. **Fair Labour Practices:** Ensure no worker in the supply chain is subjected to child labour, forced labour, or unsafe working conditions.
7. **Conflict of Interest Disclosure:** Any financial, personal, or professional conflict that could bias a design decision must be transparently declared.
8. **Respect for Intellectual Property:** Do not plagiarize, copy, or reverse-engineer competitors' designs; respect patents and trademarks.
9. **Accountability:** If a product causes harm, the designer and the company must accept responsibility and provide remedy.
10. **Cultural Sensitivity:** A product designed for one culture must not be transplanted to another without sensitivity to local values, religions, and customs.
11. **Transparency:** Manufacturing processes, material sourcing, and supply chains should be open to public scrutiny where feasible.
12. **Sustainability over Profit Maximization:** Reject the practice of *planned obsolescence* — designing products to fail prematurely to force repeat purchases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In KTU ESE (End Semester Examinations), questions on ethics frequently ask students to *apply* one or more of these principles to a real-world case study. Memorizing the principles is **not enough** — you must be able to **justify** which principle takes precedence in a conflicting situation.

## 2.4 KTU Framework Cheat Sheet (Reference Table)

| Framework / Tool | Type | Primary Use Case | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Utilitarian Analysis | Consequentialist | Maximizing overall benefit | Net positive outcome score |
| Deontological Analysis | Duty-Based | Resolving non-negotiable moral duties | Duty compliance checklist |
| Virtue Ethics Check | Character-Based | Long-term designer identity formation | Virtue alignment report |
| Rights-Based Matrix | Rights-Based | Protecting vulnerable stakeholders | Rights impact assessment |
| Triple Bottom Line (TBL) | Sustainability | Balancing people, planet, profit | TBL scorecard |
| Ethics Impact Assessment (EIA) | Procedural | Pre-launch risk identification | EIA report with mitigations |
| Stakeholder Power-Interest Grid | Analytical | Prioritizing stakeholder engagement | Engagement strategy map |
| UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) | Global | Aligning design with global priorities | SDG mapping matrix (17 goals) |
| Designer's Oath / Hippocratic Parallel | Philosophical | Anchoring personal moral identity | Internalized ethical commitment |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ethics in product design is not an abstract academic exercise. It has **direct engineering and commercial utility**:

- **Legal Risk Reduction:** Companies with strong ethical design cultures face fewer lawsuits, regulatory fines, and product recalls. *Example: Johnson & Johnson's Tylenol cyanide crisis response (1982) is a textbook case of ethics-driven crisis management that saved the brand.*
- **Brand Equity and Trust:** Consumers increasingly purchase from brands perceived as ethical. A 2024 industry survey shows that **73% of Gen Z consumers** prefer brands with verifiable ethical and sustainability credentials.
- **Innovation Catalyst:** Constraints imposed by ethical thinking (e.g., "no single-use plastic") often drive breakthrough innovations (e.g., biodegradable packaging, modular phone designs).
- **Talent Retention:** Engineers and designers prefer to work for companies with strong ethical cultures, reducing turnover and recruitment costs.
- **Investor Confidence:** ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) metrics directly influence stock valuation and access to capital.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework, Derivations & Case Analysis

## 3.1 The Ethical Decision-Making Algorithm (Formalized)

While ethics is fundamentally qualitative, the **decision process itself** can be expressed as a structured analytical function. The following formalization helps KTU students systematically work through complex ethical dilemmas that appear in case-study-based exam questions.

Let $D$ represent the final ethical design decision, $S$ the set of all identified stakeholders, $A$ the set of all feasible design alternatives, and $E(a_i)$ the net ethical score of alternative $a_i$.

### Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification

The stakeholder set is constructed as a union of all affected parties:

$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n\}
$$

Each stakeholder $s_i$ is assigned a **weight** $w_i$ based on their vulnerability, power, and proximity to the harm/benefit. Vulnerable stakeholders (children, elderly, disabled, low-income) receive higher weights.

$$
W = \{w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_n\} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 \le w_i \le 1
$$

The weights are normalized so that the sum of all weights equals 1, ensuring a probabilistic interpretation:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1
$$

### Step 2 — Alternative Generation

The set of feasible design alternatives is identified, subject to technical, financial, and regulatory constraints:

$$
A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_m\}
$$

### Step 3 — Ethical Scoring of Each Alternative

For each alternative $a_j$, an ethical score is computed by evaluating it against the **12 core principles** (from Section 2.3). Let $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{12}\}$ be the principle set, and let $c_{jk}$ be the compliance score of alternative $a_j$ against principle $p_k$, where $c_{jk} \in [0, 1]$.

The total ethical score of alternative $a_j$ is the weighted sum:

$$
E(a_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{12} c_{jk} \cdot \alpha_k
$$

Here, $\alpha_k$ is the importance weight assigned to principle $p_k$, satisfying $\sum \alpha_k = 1$. For example, $\alpha_{safety}$ is typically set higher than $\alpha_{aesthetics}$.

### Step 4 — Optimal Alternative Selection

The ethically optimal design $D^*$ is the alternative with the maximum ethical score:

$$
D^* = \arg\max_{a_j \in A} E(a_j)
$$

This provides a **defensible, auditable, and quantitative** basis for the design decision, satisfying KTU OBE assessment criteria for analytical rigor.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Case Study — "The Lead-Painted Toy Dilemma"

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Background (Composite case for pedagogical purposes):**
>
> *A KTU graduate working as a junior product designer at a toy company in Kerala is asked to develop a low-cost painted toy set for export. The company faces a choice between two pigment binders:*
> *- Binder X: Lead-based, costs ₹20/kg, offers vibrant long-lasting colour, but is toxic to children if ingested.*
> *- Binder Y: Non-toxic water-based, costs ₹60/kg, offers slightly less vibrant colour, but is fully child-safe.*
> *The export market does not enforce strict lead-content regulations. The company is under financial pressure. Apply the ethical decision-making framework.*

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1: Identify Stakeholders and Assign Weights**

$$
S = \{\text{Child End-Users}, \text{Parents}, \text{Factory Workers}, \text{Local Community}, \text{Company Shareholders}, \text{Environment}\}
$$

$$
W = \{0.40, \, 0.25, \, 0.15, \, 0.10, \, 0.05, \, 0.05\}
$$

The child end-users receive the highest weight (0.40) because they are the most vulnerable and the primary victims of lead poisoning (which causes neurological damage).

**Step 2: List Alternatives**

$$
A = \{a_1 = \text{Binder X (Lead-based)}, \; a_2 = \text{Binder Y (Non-toxic)}\}
$$

**Step 3: Score Each Alternative Against the 12 Principles**

For brevity, we show the calculation for the **top 4 most critical principles** (Safety, Honesty, Environmental Stewardship, Fair Labour) with $\alpha$ weights as: $\alpha_{safety} = 0.50$, $\alpha_{honesty} = 0.20$, $\alpha_{environment} = 0.20$, $\alpha_{labour} = 0.10$.

For **Binder X (Lead-based):**

$$
E(a_1) = (0.0)(0.50) + (0.5)(0.20) + (0.2)(0.20) + (0.6)(0.10) = 0.00 + 0.10 + 0.04 + 0.06 = 0.20
$$

For **Binder Y (Non-toxic):**

$$
E(a_2) = (1.0)(0.50) + (1.0)(0.20) + (0.9)(0.20) + (0.9)(0.10) = 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.18 + 0.09 = 0.97
$$

**Step 4: Select the Optimal Alternative**

$$
D^* = \arg\max_{a_j} E(a_j) = \arg\max(0.20, \, 0.97) = a_2
$$

**Conclusion:** The ethically optimal design is **Binder Y** (non-toxic water-based binder), despite its higher cost. The designer's professional duty is to advocate for $a_2$ to management, document the decision rationale, and if overruled, escalate the concern through formal channels (ethics committee, whistleblower protection) — not to silently comply with a harmful decision.

## 3.3 The Deontological "Bright Line" Test

For high-stakes ethical decisions, the deontological tradition offers a **bright-line test**: if a design decision, if universalized, would lead to a world you would not want to live in, then it is ethically impermissible, regardless of benefits.

Formally, this is the **Categorical Imperative** of Immanuel Kant, adapted for product design:

$$
\text{Ethical} \iff \nexists \, \text{world state } W \text{ such that universalizing the action causes } W \text{ to be undesirable}
$$

Applied to product design:

- Universalizing *planned obsolescence* produces a world of mountains of toxic e-waste. $\Rightarrow$ **Impermissible.**
- Universalizing *child labour in supply chains* produces a world where no child is safe. $\Rightarrow$ **Impermissible.**
- Universalizing *transparent supply chain disclosure* produces a world of accountability and trust. $\Rightarrow$ **Permissible.**

## 3.4 The Utilitarian Net-Benefit Calculation

For consequentialist analysis, the net benefit $NB$ of a design choice is the difference between total benefits and total harms, weighted by stakeholder impact:

$$
NB = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot B_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot H_i
$$

Where $B_i$ is the benefit experienced by stakeholder $s_i$, and $H_i$ is the harm experienced by $s_i$. A positive $NB$ indicates a net ethical benefit; a negative $NB$ indicates an ethically unjustifiable design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Ethical Product Design Decision Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete ethical decision-making workflow, from initial stakeholder identification through post-launch ethical review.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Brief Received] --> B[Identify All Stakeholders]
    B --> C[Map Stakeholder Power and Interest]
    C --> D[Recognize Ethical Issues]
    D --> E[Apply 12 Core Design Ethics Principles]
    E --> F{Conflicting Principles?}
    F -- No --> G[Select Ethical Alternative]
    F -- Yes --> H[Apply Deontological Bright Line Test]
    H --> I[Apply Utilitarian Net Benefit Analysis]
    I --> J[Apply Virtue Ethics Check]
    J --> K[Stakeholder Negotiation Round]
    K --> L[Document Decision in Design Ethics Logbook]
    L --> M[Implement Selected Design]
    G --> L
    M --> N[Post Launch Ethical Impact Review at 3 5 and 10 years]
    N --> O[Publish Transparency Report]
    O --> P[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 The Three Pillars of Ethical Product Design (Structural Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PEOPLE\nSocial Ethics] --> C[ETHICAL\nPRODUCT\nDESIGN]
    P2[PLANET\nEnvironmental Ethics] --> C
    P3[PROFIT\nEconomic Ethics] --> C
    C --> O1[Safety]
    C --> O2[Inclusivity]
    C --> O3[Sustainability]
    C --> O4[Fairness]
    C --> O5[Transparency]
    C --> O6[Accountability]
```

## 4.3 The Twelve Core Principles — Hierarchical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Twelve Core Principles of Ethical Product Design] --> S1[1 Safety First]
    R --> S2[2 Honesty in Marketing]
    R --> S3[3 User Privacy]
    R --> S4[4 Inclusivity]
    R --> S5[5 Environmental Stewardship]
    R --> S6[6 Fair Labour Practices]
    R --> S7[7 Conflict of Interest Disclosure]
    R --> S8[8 Intellectual Property Respect]
    R --> S9[9 Accountability]
    R --> S10[10 Cultural Sensitivity]
    R --> S11[11 Transparency]
    R --> S12[12 Reject Planned Obsolescence]
```

## 4.4 Ethical Dilemma Resolution Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Ethical Dilemma Encountered] --> R1{Greatest Harm Potential?}
    R1 -- Yes --> A1[Apply Deontological Duty Test]
    R1 -- No --> R2{Multiple Stakeholders Affected?}
    R2 -- Yes --> A2[Apply Utilitarian Net Benefit]
    R2 -- No --> R3{Character of Designer at Stake?}
    R3 -- Yes --> A3[Apply Virtue Ethics]
    R3 -- No --> A4[Apply Rights Based Framework]
    A1 --> Z[Document and Justify Decision]
    A2 --> Z
    A3 --> Z
    A4 --> Z
    Z --> Y[Monitor and Review]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Define "Triple Bottom Line" in the context of ethical product design. List its three components.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** is a sustainability framework that evaluates a product's performance not only by its financial profit but also by its social and environmental impact. It was popularized by John Elkington in 1994 and is now a foundational concept in ethical product design.

Its three components are:

1. **People (Social Equity):** The product must benefit society, protect human rights, and improve quality of life.
2. **Planet (Environmental Quality):** The product must minimize ecological harm, reduce carbon emissions, and use resources sustainably.
3. **Profit (Economic Viability):** The product must generate fair economic value without exploiting workers, consumers, or the environment.

A product that achieves all three is considered ethically and sustainably successful.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**What is "planned obsolescence"? Why is it considered unethical?**

**Model Answer:**

**Planned obsolescence** is a deliberate design strategy in which a manufacturer designs a product to have a limited useful life, become non-functional, or become outdated after a predetermined period, with the intention of forcing the consumer to purchase a replacement.

It is considered unethical because it:

- **Disrespects consumer trust** by intentionally selling a product known to fail prematurely.
- **Generates environmental harm** through excessive e-waste, plastic waste, and resource depletion.
- **Violates the duty of care** that designers owe to users and the planet.
- **Prioritizes short-term profit** over long-term sustainability and user welfare.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **stakeholder theory** in product design ethics. Identify at least **six categories of stakeholders** for a smartphone product, and for each, state one specific ethical obligation the designer owes them.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the **Ford Pinto case** (1970s) as a classic example of unethical product design. What ethical principles were violated, and what lessons does it offer to modern designers?* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Stakeholder Theory Definition:**

Stakeholder theory, developed by R. Edward Freeman in 1984, asserts that a product's design must serve the interests of *all* parties affected by it — not just shareholders or end customers. Every stakeholder has a legitimate claim on the designer's attention, and ethical design requires balancing these claims transparently.

**Six Stakeholder Categories for a Smartphone and Designer's Ethical Obligations:**

1. **End Users (Consumers):** Designers must ensure the phone is safe (no battery explosions), ergonomically usable, and free from addictive dark-pattern UI.
2. **Factory Workers (Manufacturing Staff):** Designers must ensure the assembly process does not expose workers to toxic chemicals, excessive overtime, or unsafe conditions.
3. **Local Mining Communities (Cobalt, Lithium):** Designers must source materials from conflict-free, certified mines that do not use child labour.
4. **Environment (Ecosystem):** Designers must minimize the phone's carbon footprint, use recyclable materials, and provide take-back recycling programs.
5. **Future Generations:** Designers must avoid using materials that will become hazardous long-term e-waste.
6. **Government and Regulators:** Designers must comply with national and international safety, electromagnetic, and data-privacy regulations.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**The Ford Pinto Case (1971–1978):**

The Ford Pinto was a subcompact car produced by Ford Motor Company. Internal Ford documents revealed that engineers had calculated that the car's fuel tank design was prone to rupturing and bursting into flames upon rear-end collisions at low speeds. Ford executives knew of the defect but calculated that it was *cheaper to pay lawsuit settlements* for burn victims than to recall and fix all the Pintos (estimated at \$11 per car to fix vs. \$200,000 per death settlement on average).

**Ethical Principles Violated:**

- **Safety First:** The company knowingly sold an unsafe product.
- **Utilitarian Violation:** Ford treated human life as a financial variable rather than maximizing human welfare.
- **Deontological Violation:** The duty to protect human life was violated for profit.
- **Accountability:** Ford attempted to conceal the defect, violating transparency.
- **Stakeholder Theory Violation:** Customers' safety was sacrificed for shareholder profit.

**Lessons for Modern Designers:**

1. Human life must never be treated as a cost-benefit variable.
2. Whistleblowers must be protected; engineers who raised concerns at Ford were silenced.
3. Ethics Impact Assessments (EIA) must be **mandatory** before product launch.
4. The **NSPE Code of Ethics** mandates that engineers' paramount obligation is *public safety* — a duty that supersedes employer instructions.
5. Companies that prioritize short-term profit over safety eventually face catastrophic brand damage (Ford's reputation suffered for decades).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** *Differentiate between **legal compliance** and **ethical responsibility** in product design. Use a real-world example where a product was legal but unethical.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the **concept of "Design for Environment (DfE)"** and describe **five specific DfE strategies** that a product designer can adopt.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Legal Compliance vs. Ethical Responsibility:**

| Dimension | Legal Compliance | Ethical Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Source of Standard | Government, regulatory bodies | Personal conscience, professional codes, societal values |
| Enforcement | Laws, penalties, imprisonment | Self-regulation, peer pressure, reputation |
| Minimum Threshold | Yes — it is the bare minimum | Goes beyond legal minimum |
| Scope | Limited to codified rules | Encompasses grey areas not yet regulated |
| Violation Consequence | Punishment by law | Loss of trust, moral guilt, social backlash |

**Real-World Example — Facebook (now Meta) and Cambridge Analytica (2014–2018):**

Facebook's data harvesting practices through third-party apps (like the "This Is Your Digital Life" quiz) were, at the time, **not explicitly illegal** under U.S. law. The company was legally compliant. However, harvesting personal data of 87 million users without meaningful informed consent was **profoundly unethical** — it violated user privacy, manipulated political opinions, and eroded democratic trust.

This case demonstrates that *legality is a necessary but not sufficient condition* for ethical product design. Designers must ask not only "Is this legal?" but also "Is this right?"

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Design for Environment (DfE) — Definition:**

Design for Environment (also called **Eco-Design** or **Green Design**) is a systematic design philosophy that integrates environmental considerations into every phase of the product lifecycle, from material selection to end-of-life disposal. The goal is to minimize the product's negative environmental impact across its entire existence.

**Five DfE Strategies:**

1. **Design for Disassembly (DfD):** Use snap-fits, modular construction, and standardized fasteners so the product can be easily taken apart for repair, recycling, or component recovery. *Example: Fairphone's modular smartphone.*

2. **Design for Material Optimization:** Select materials that are renewable, recycled, recyclable, or biodegradable. Avoid toxic substances (lead, mercury, cadmium). *Example: Dell's use of closed-loop recycled plastics in laptop chassis.*

3. **Design for Energy Efficiency:** Minimize the energy consumed during the product's use phase. *Example: LED bulbs consuming 80% less energy than incandescent bulbs.*

4. **Design for Longevity / Durability:** Build products to last longer, reducing the frequency of replacement. This directly counters planned obsolescence. *Example: Vestas wind turbines designed for 25-year service life with modular upgrades.*

5. **Design for Minimal Packaging:** Eliminate unnecessary packaging, use recycled or compostable materials, and design packaging that is reusable or easily recyclable. *Example: Unilever's compressed deodorant packaging that uses 50% less plastic.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ethics Questions:**
> 1. **Writing opinions without frameworks:** Stating "I think it is wrong" without invoking utilitarianism, deontology, or rights theory will fetch only partial marks. Always anchor your answer in a recognized ethical framework.
> 2. **Ignoring the stakeholder map:** Examiners specifically look for *stakeholder identification*. An answer that discusses only "the company" and "the customer" loses marks. Remember waste workers, future generations, and the ecosystem.
> 3. **Confusing legal and ethical:** Conflating "it is illegal" with "it is unethical" is a common error. Examiners reward students who can *distinguish* the two and give examples of legal-but-unethical products.
> 4. **Forgetting case studies:** KTU examiners award bonus marks to students who cite real cases (Ford Pinto, Theranos, Boeing 737 MAX, Parle-G biscuit worker case, etc.). A concrete case reference elevates a good answer to an excellent one.
> 5. **Skipping the "What would you do?" question:** Many ethics questions end with "As a designer, what action would you take?" Students often describe the dilemma but fail to commit to a specific action. Always end with a clear, justified decision.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ethics in Product Design (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Application of moral principles across the product lifecycle to ensure safety, social responsibility, and environmental sustainability.
- **Three Pillars (TBL):** People, Planet, Profit — all three must be satisfied.
- **Four Ethical Theories:** Utilitarian, Deontological, Virtue Ethics, Rights-Based.
- **Twelve Core Principles:** Safety, Honesty, Privacy, Inclusivity, Environment, Fair Labour, Conflict Disclosure, IP Respect, Accountability, Cultural Sensitivity, Transparency, Anti-Planned-Obsolescence.
- **Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984):** Every affected party has a legitimate moral claim on the design process.
- **Key Distinction:** Legal compliance is the *minimum*; ethical responsibility goes *beyond* the law.
- **DfE Strategies:** Design for Disassembly, Material Optimization, Energy Efficiency, Longevity, Minimal Packaging.
- **Canonical Case Studies:** Ford Pinto (1970s), Theranos (2010s), Boeing 737 MAX (2018–2019), Cambridge Analytica–Facebook (2018), Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984).
- **Indian Context:** The Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Union Carbide, 1984) is the most cited case in KTU valuation — know the facts, the ethical violations, and the long-term lessons.
- **NSPE Code of Ethics:** Paramount obligation of engineers is **public safety** — a duty that supersedes employer instructions.
- **Ethical Decision Formula:** $D^* = \arg\max_{a_j} E(a_j)$, where $E(a_j) = \sum c_{jk} \cdot \alpha_k$.
- **Bright-Line Test (Kant):** If universalizing the action produces a world you would not want, the action is impermissible.
- **Examiners reward:** Case studies, named frameworks, stakeholder mapping, and a clear personal commitment to action.
- **Avoid:** Vague platitudes, unsupported opinions, and confusion between legal and ethical categories.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legal Factors and Social Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legal Factors and Social Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Legal Factors and Social Issues in Product Design?

**Legal Factors** in product design refer to the entire spectrum of statutory laws, regulatory frameworks, intellectual property (IP) rights, liability rules, and compliance standards that a product must satisfy before, during, and after its market launch. **Social Issues** encompass the ethical, cultural, environmental, accessibility, and consumer-protection considerations that influence how a product is conceived, manufactured, distributed, used, and disposed of.

Together, these two domains form the **Legal–Social Constraint Envelope (LSCE)** of product design — the invisible boundary within which every engineering and aesthetic decision must operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *Legal factors are the codified rules (IPR, product liability, safety standards, environmental regulations) governing the protection, manufacture, and distribution of a product, while social issues are the ethical, cultural, and environmental responsibilities a designer owes to the end-user and society.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Driving Licence Analogy

Imagine you have designed a beautiful, powerful car (your product). Before you can legally drive it on public roads, you need:
- A **driving licence** (regulatory compliance / certifications),
- **Number plates and insurance** (legal identity and liability coverage),
- To respect **traffic rules** (safety standards),
- And ensure it does not **pollute excessively** or **endanger pedestrians** (social responsibility).

If you skip any of these, your car — however brilliant — cannot be legally sold. **Legal factors** are the traffic rules, and **social issues** are the unwritten courtesy of not honking at 2 AM. Product design must respect both.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Legal–Social Compliance

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU question on this topic maps to one of these four pillars:

1. **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** — Patents, Trademarks, Copyrights, Trade Secrets, Industrial Designs.
2. **Product Liability & Consumer Protection** — Defect, negligence, strict liability, warranty law.
3. **Regulatory & Safety Standards** — BIS (India), ISO, CE (Europe), FCC (USA), RoHS, WEEE.
4. **Social & Ethical Design Considerations** — Sustainability, accessibility (universal design), cultural sensitivity, privacy (GDPR/Data Protection).

### 1.4 Key Physical/Standard Metrics to Remember

| Metric / Term | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| Indian Patent Term | **20 years** from filing date |
| Trademark Renewal Cycle | **Every 10 years** (perpetual) |
| Copyright Term (Literary/Artistic) | Life of author + **60 years** |
| Industrial Design Term (India) | **10 years**, renewable once for 5 years |
| CE Marking Region | European Economic Area (EEA) |
| RoHS Directive | Restricts **6 hazardous substances** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) — The Designer's Shield

IPR is the legal mechanism that protects the **intangible creative output** of a designer. It converts an idea into a legally enforceable, exclusive commercial right.

#### 2.1.1 The Five Major Forms of IPR

**A. Patents**
- A patent is a **time-limited statutory right** granted for a new, useful, and non-obvious invention.
- In India, governed by the **Patents Act, 1970**.
- Three essential tests: **Novelty**, **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)**, **Industrial Applicability**.
- *Why it matters in design:* Protects the functional/technical innovation behind a product (e.g., a new folding mechanism in a laptop hinge).

**B. Trademarks**
- A trademark is a **distinctive sign** (word, logo, shape, sound, colour combination) that identifies the source of goods/services.
- Governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** in India.
- Registration gives the **® symbol**; an unregistered mark only gets **™**.
- *Why it matters:* Protects brand identity — the Apple logo, the Nike Swoosh, the Tata "T".

**C. Copyright**
- Automatic protection for **original literary, artistic, musical, and software works**.
- In India, governed by the **Copyright Act, 1957**.
- Does **NOT** protect ideas, only their expression.
- *Why it matters:* Protects user manuals, product packaging artwork, software code, and 3D CAD models of artistic sculptures.

**D. Industrial Design**
- Protects the **aesthetic (visual/ornamental) appearance** of a product — shape, configuration, pattern, or ornament.
- Governed by the **Designs Act, 2000** in India.
- *Why it matters:* Distinguishes your product's look from a competitor's even if the function is identical (e.g., the shape of a Coca-Cola bottle).

**E. Trade Secrets**
- Confidential business information that provides a **competitive edge** (formula, process, customer list).
- Protected under the **Common Law of Breach of Confidence** in India (no specific statute).
- *Why it matters:* Protects the Coca-Cola formula, Google's search algorithm.

#### 2.2 Product Liability — The Designer's Accountability

Product liability is the area of law holding **manufacturers, designers, and sellers** legally responsible for placing a defective product into the hands of a consumer.

Three legal theories underpin product liability:

1. **Negligence** — The designer failed to exercise reasonable care (e.g., skipping a stress test on a baby stroller).
2. **Strict Liability** (Rule in *Rylands v Fletcher*, applied to products via *Donoghue v Stevenson, 1932*) — Liability exists regardless of fault if the product is defective.
3. **Breach of Warranty** — The product failed to meet an express or implied promise (e.g., "battery lasts 10 hours" but lasts 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (India):** Establishes the **Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA)** and product liability action can be filed for harm caused by a defective product or by a service deficiency.

#### 2.3 Regulatory & Safety Standards

| Region/Body | Standard | Focus Area |
|---|---|---|
| BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) | ISI Mark | Mandatory Indian product certification |
| ISO | ISO 9001, ISO 14001 | Quality & Environmental Management |
| EU | CE Mark, RoHS, REACH | Health, Safety, Environmental compliance |
| USA | FCC, UL | Electronics safety, electromagnetic compatibility |
| Global | WEEE Directive | Electronic waste disposal |
| India | E-Waste Management Rules, 2016 | E-waste handling and recycling |

#### 2.4 Social Issues — The Ethical Compass

**A. Sustainability & Green Design**
- Designing for **disassembly, recyclability, and energy efficiency**.
- Concepts: **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)**, **Design for Environment (DfE)**, **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**.

**B. Universal Design & Accessibility**
- The **7 Principles of Universal Design** (Ron Mace, NC State University) — Equitable Use, Flexibility in Use, Simple & Intuitive, Perceptible Information, Tolerance for Error, Low Physical Effort, Size & Space for Approach.
- Compliance: **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016** (India), **ADA** (USA).

**C. Cultural Sensitivity**
- Avoiding culturally offensive symbols, colours, or features (e.g., number **4** is unlucky in East Asia; right-hand greeting is mandatory in many cultures).

**D. Privacy & Data Protection**
- **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (India)**, **GDPR (EU, 2018)**.
- Applies to IoT products, wearables, smartphones that collect user data.

**E. Ethical Sourcing & Labour**
- Avoiding conflict minerals, child labour, and unfair wages in the supply chain.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Term / Value | Governing Law (India) |
|---|---|---|
| Patent Duration | **20 years** | Patents Act, 1970 |
| Trademark Validity | **10 years** (renewable) | Trade Marks Act, 1999 |
| Copyright (Literary) | Life + **60 years** | Copyright Act, 1957 |
| Copyright (Films/Sound) | **60 years** from publication | Copyright Act, 1957 |
| Industrial Design | **10 years** (+5 renewable) | Designs Act, 2000 |
| Geographical Indication (GI) | **10 years** (renewable) | GI of Goods Act, 1999 |
| Plant Variety Protection | **18 years** (trees) / **15 years** (others) | PPVFR Act, 2001 |
| Layout Design of ICs | **10 years** | Semiconductor IC Layout Design Act, 2000 |
| BIS Standard | ISI Mark | BIS Act, 2016 |
| Consumer Court Hierarchy | District → State → National | Consumer Protection Act, 2019 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Patent-protected engine technology, CE-marked for Europe, ISO/TS 16949 quality certified.
- **Medical Devices:** Must comply with **Medical Device Rules, 2017 (India)** and bear CE/FDA approvals.
- **Electronics:** RoHS compliance, e-waste management, FCC for wireless.
- **Software/SaaS Products:** Copyright on code, trademark on brand, GDPR for EU users.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Case-Based Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Legal Factors and Social Issues" is a **qualitative management topic**, the "derivations" below are **structured analytical reasoning frameworks** — the type of step-by-step logical analysis KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: Selecting the Correct IP for a New Product

**Step 1 — Identify the Nature of the Innovation**
Ask: *Is the innovation a functional invention, a brand name, an artistic expression, an aesthetic shape, or a confidential process?*

**Step 2 — Map the Innovation to the Correct IP Form**
Use the decision logic below:

$$
\text{Innovation Type} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Functional/Technical} \Rightarrow \text{Patent} \\
\text{Brand Identity} \Rightarrow \text{Trademark} \\
\text{Literary/Artistic/Software} \Rightarrow \text{Copyright} \\
\text{Visual Appearance/Shape} \Rightarrow \text{Industrial Design} \\
\text{Confidential Process/Formula} \Rightarrow \text{Trade Secret}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Statutory Criteria**
For a patent, confirm the invention satisfies all three:

$$
\text{Patentability} = \text{Novelty} \cap \text{Inventive Step} \cap \text{Industrial Applicability}
$$

**Step 4 — File with the Correct Authority**
- Patent → **Indian Patent Office** (Kolkata, Mumbai, Chennai, Delhi).
- Trademark → **Registrar of Trademarks, Mumbai** (head office).
- Copyright → **Copyright Office, New Delhi**.
- Industrial Design → **Patent Office (Design Wing), Kolkata**.

**Step 5 — Budget and Timeline Planning**

$$
\text{Approximate Cost (India)} \approx
\begin{cases}
\text{Patent Filing} & \Rightarrow \text{₹ 1,60,000 to ₹ 2,00,000 (full prosecution)} \\
\text{Trademark} & \Rightarrow \text{₹ 4,500 to ₹ 10,000 (per class)} \\
\text{Industrial Design} & \Rightarrow \text{₹ 1,000 to ₹ 4,000 (filing)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Framework: Conducting a Legal–Social Risk Audit for a Product

**Step 1 — Stakeholder Mapping**
Identify all affected parties: end-users, workers in supply chain, environment, future generations.

**Step 2 — Legal Compliance Checklist**

$$
\text{Compliance Matrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\text{Domain} & \text{Law/Standard} & \text{Status (Y/N)} \\
\text{IPR} & \text{Patents, TM, Copyright} & \text{?} \\
\text{Safety} & \text{BIS, CE, UL} & \text{?} \\
\text{Environment} & \text{RoHS, E-Waste Rules} & \text{?} \\
\text{Labour} & \text{Factories Act, 1948} & \text{?} \\
\text{Data/Privacy} & \text{DPDP Act, 2023} & \text{?}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Social Impact Assessment (SIA)**
Evaluate the product on five ethical axes:

$$
\text{Social Score} = f(\text{Sustainability},\ \text{Accessibility},\ \text{Cultural Fit},\ \text{Privacy},\ \text{Ethical Sourcing})
$$

**Step 4 — Risk Classification**
- **High Risk:** Medical devices, children's products, automotive, aviation.
- **Medium Risk:** Consumer electronics, household appliances.
- **Low Risk:** Apparel, stationery, decorative items.

**Step 5 — Mitigation Strategy**
- Acquire missing IP.
- Procure missing certifications.
- Redesign for accessibility if score < threshold.
- Publish a public **Privacy Policy** and **Terms of Service**.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Case — *Apple Inc. v. Samsung (2012–2018)*

This is the **gold-standard KTU case** to memorize for design-IP questions.

**Step 1:** Apple files patents and registers industrial designs for the **iPhone's rounded-rectangle shape, bezel, and grid-of-icons UI** between **2007–2010**.

**Step 2:** Samsung launches the **Galaxy series** with similar rounded-rectangle design and grid icons in 2010.

**Step 3:** Apple sues for **patent infringement** (utility patents) and **design patent infringement** (industrial design equivalent) in multiple jurisdictions.

**Step 4:** Court finds Samsung infringed Apple's **design patents** (later upheld by US Supreme Court, 2016, with **$399 million** damages).

**Step 5:** Key takeaway for designers — *a product's aesthetic appearance is as legally protectable as its technical function*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designers must conduct a Freedom-to-Operate (FTO) search** before launching any product to ensure no existing IP is infringed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Fold IP Architecture for Product Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Product Innovation] --> B{What is being protected?}
    B -->|Function or Technical Process| C[Patent]
    B -->|Brand Name or Logo| D[Trademark]
    B -->|Original Text Art Software| E[Copyright]
    B -->|Visual Shape or Ornament| F[Industrial Design]
    B -->|Confidential Formula Process| G[Trade Secret]
    C --> C1[Indian Patent Office 20 years]
    D --> D1[Registrar of TM 10 years renewable]
    E --> E1[Copyright Office Life plus 60 years]
    F --> F1[Design Wing Kolkata 10 plus 5 years]
    G --> G1[Common Law No Fixed Term]
```

### 4.2 Legal-Social Compliance Flow for a New Product Launch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Concept] --> B[Step 1 FTO Search]
    B --> C{Any conflicting IP?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Modify Design or License IP]
    C -->|No| E[Step 2 File Own IP]
    D --> B
    E --> F[Step 3 Safety and Regulatory Testing]
    F --> G[Step 4 Environmental and E waste Audit]
    G --> H[Step 5 Social and Ethical Review]
    H --> I[Step 6 Documentation and Labelling]
    I --> J[Step 7 Market Launch]
    J --> K[Step 8 Post Market Liability Monitoring]
```

### 4.3 The Social Responsibility Pyramid for Product Designers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Designer Responsibility] --> B[Foundation Ethical Sourcing and Fair Labour]
    B --> C[Level 2 Environmental Compliance]
    C --> D[Level 3 Safety and Reliability]
    D --> E[Level 4 Accessibility Universal Design]
    E --> F[Apex Cultural Sensitivity and Privacy]
```

### 4.4 Patent Filing Process in India (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Invention Disclosure] --> B[Prior Art Search]
    B --> C[Drafting Patent Specification]
    C --> D[Filing Application with Patent Office]
    D --> E[Publication in Patent Journal After 18 months]
    E --> F[Request for Examination Within 48 months]
    F --> G[Examination Report by Controller]
    G --> H{Objections Raised?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Reply to FER with Amendments]
    H -->|No| J[Grant of Patent]
    I --> G
    J --> K[20 years Exclusive Rights from Filing Date]
```

### 4.5 Product Liability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Consumer Injured by Product] --> B{What went wrong?}
    B -->|Manufacturing Defect| C[Strict Liability against Manufacturer]
    B -->|Design Defect| D[Liability against Designer for foreseeable risk]
    B -->|Warning Defect| E[Liability for inadequate labelling or instructions]
    C --> F[Consumer Protection Act 2019 CCPA]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Compensation Determined}
    G --> H[District State or National Commission]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Intellectual Property Rights (IPR). List any four forms of IPR relevant to product design.*

**Model Answer:**
Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) are the legally enforceable exclusive rights granted to creators/inventors over their intangible intellectual creations, allowing them to control commercial use for a limited period.

Four forms of IPR relevant to product design are:
1. **Patents** — for new functional inventions.
2. **Trademarks** — for brand names, logos, and identifiers.
3. **Copyrights** — for original artistic, literary, and software works.
4. **Industrial Designs** — for the aesthetic appearance (shape, pattern) of a product.

*(Trade Secrets is a valid fifth option.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Patent and an Industrial Design with respect to their scope and duration in India.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Patent | Industrial Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Protects** | Functional/Invention | Aesthetic Appearance |
| **Governed by** | Patents Act, 1970 | Designs Act, 2000 |
| **Term** | 20 years (non-renewable) | 10 years + 5 years renewal |
| **Test** | Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application | Novelty and Originality |
| **Example** | A new engine mechanism | The shape of a perfume bottle |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse **Industrial Design** (aesthetic) with **Patent** (functional) — students routinely swap these and lose 4–5 marks.
> 2. Always mention the **governing statute** (e.g., Patents Act 1970) — examiners allot 1–2 marks specifically for naming the Act.
> 3. For social issues, do **not** give generic answers like "be ethical" — cite the **specific law/principle** (e.g., "DPDP Act 2023", "7 Principles of Universal Design").
> 4. When asked for examples, use **Indian examples** (Tata, ISRO, Amul, Aadhaar) — KTU prefers India-specific cases.
> 5. For 14-mark questions, always structure the answer with **headings and sub-points** — a wall of text loses presentation marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Q.1 (a) Explain the various forms of Intellectual Property Rights relevant to product design. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
IPR refers to the legal rights that protect the creations of the human intellect in industrial, scientific, literary, and artistic domains.

**Five Major Forms (6 marks — distribute 1.2 marks each):**

1. **Patents** — Statutory right granted for a new, useful, non-obvious invention with industrial applicability. Governed by the **Patents Act, 1970**. Duration: **20 years**. Example: A new pharmaceutical compound.

2. **Trademarks** — A distinctive sign (logo, word, shape) that identifies goods/services. Governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**. Duration: **10 years**, renewable indefinitely. Example: The Tata "T" logo.

3. **Copyrights** — Automatic protection for original literary, artistic, musical, and software works. Governed by the **Copyright Act, 1957**. Duration: Life of author + **60 years**. Example: The artwork on a product manual.

4. **Industrial Designs** — Protects the visual ornamental appearance of a product. Governed by the **Designs Act, 2000**. Duration: **10 years** + **5 years renewal**. Example: The shape of a Zippo lighter.

5. **Trade Secrets** — Confidential business information providing competitive advantage. No statute in India; protected under common law. Duration: As long as secrecy is maintained. Example: The Coca-Cola formula.

---

#### *Q.1 (b) Discuss the social and ethical responsibilities of a product designer with reference to sustainability, accessibility, and cultural sensitivity. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A product designer is a **steward of society** and bears three primary social responsibilities:

**1. Sustainability (2 marks):**
Designers must adopt **Design for Environment (DfE)** and **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** principles. They should minimise carbon footprint, use recyclable materials, design for **disassembly**, and comply with **RoHS** and **E-Waste Management Rules, 2016**. Example: Fairphone's modular, repairable smartphone design.

**2. Accessibility — Universal Design (2.5 marks):**
Following the **7 Principles of Universal Design** (Ron Mace) ensures products are usable by people of all abilities. Designers must comply with the **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016** in India. Example: Tactile UI buttons on ATMs and smartphones for visually impaired users.

**3. Cultural Sensitivity (2.5 marks):**
Designers must respect local customs, colours, symbols, and taboos. Products that ignore cultural context often fail in global markets. Examples:
- The number **4** is inauspicious in East Asia — avoid 4th-floor buttons in elevators in China/Japan.
- **Right-hand rule** for packaging design in Middle Eastern markets.
- Avoiding culturally offensive iconography in mascots and brand logos.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Q.2 (a) Define Product Liability. Explain the legal theories (negligence, strict liability, warranty) on which a consumer can claim compensation in India. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Product liability is the legal responsibility of a manufacturer, designer, or seller to compensate a consumer for injury or damage caused by a defective product.

**Three Legal Theories (6 marks — 2 marks each):**

1. **Negligence:** A consumer must prove (i) the designer/manufacturer owed a duty of care, (ii) the duty was breached, (iii) the breach caused the injury. Example: A car manufacturer knowingly using a faulty brake design.

2. **Strict Liability (Rule in *Donoghue v Stevenson, 1932*):** The consumer need not prove fault — only that the product was defective and caused harm. The manufacturer is liable **regardless of care taken**. Foundation of the **"neighbour principle"**.

3. **Breach of Warranty:** Arises when a product fails to meet an express promise (e.g., "waterproof" claims) or implied promise (merchantability, fitness for purpose). Governed by the **Sale of Goods Act, 1930** and **Consumer Protection Act, 2019**.

**Statutory Remedy (1 mark):**
Under the **Consumer Protection Act, 2019**, consumers can file complaints in the **District, State, or National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC)** based on the value of the claim.

---

#### *Q.2 (b) With a suitable case study, explain how Intellectual Property Rights protect innovation in product design. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
IPR converts innovation into a legally enforceable, monetisable asset, encouraging designers to invest in R&D by guaranteeing temporary exclusivity.

**Case Study — *Apple Inc. v. Samsung Electronics (2012–2018)* (4 marks):**
- Apple registered **utility patents** and **design patents** for the iPhone's rounded-rectangle bezel, grid of app icons, and pinch-to-zoom gesture between 2007 and 2010.
- Samsung launched the Galaxy smartphone with a strikingly similar design in 2010.
- Apple sued for **patent infringement** and **design patent infringement** in 12+ countries.
- In 2016, the **US Supreme Court** ruled in *Samsung v. Apple* that design-patent damages could be based on the **entire product's profit**, not just the infringing component. Samsung was ordered to pay **$399 million** in damages.

**Lessons for Designers (2 marks):**
1. **Aesthetic design is as legally valuable as functional innovation** — register industrial designs early.
2. Conduct a **Freedom-to-Operate (FTO)** search before market launch.
3. **File patents globally** (PCT route) if the product will be exported.

**Conclusion (Optional — fetches grace marks):**
IP protection is not a luxury but a **strategic necessity** for any innovative product.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IPR = Patents (20 yrs) + Trademarks (10 yrs renewable) + Copyrights (Life+60) + Industrial Designs (10+5) + Trade Secrets (no fixed term).**
- **Patent** protects **function**; **Industrial Design** protects **form** — never confuse them.
- **Strict Liability** under *Donoghue v Stevenson* holds the manufacturer liable **without proof of fault**.
- **Consumer Protection Act, 2019** established the **CCPA** and a 3-tier redressal forum.
- **BIS (ISI Mark)** is the mandatory Indian safety certification; **CE Mark** is for Europe.
- **RoHS** restricts **6 hazardous substances**; **WEEE** governs e-waste disposal.
- The **7 Principles of Universal Design** (Ron Mace) are the gold standard for accessibility.
- **DPDP Act, 2023** governs digital data protection in India (replaced the 2019 Bill).
- **GI Tag** protects region-specific products (e.g., Darjeeling Tea, Kanchipuram Silk).
- A **Freedom-to-Operate (FTO)** search is the first legal step before any product launch.
- For **14-mark KTU answers**, always use headings, sub-points, cite the Act/Year, and give an **Indian example**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory trick for KTU viva:**
> *"PATENT protects the BRAIN, TRADEMARK protects the BRAND, COPYRIGHT protects the CREATION, INDUSTRIAL DESIGN protects the BODY, TRADE SECRET protects the FORMULA."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creative Thinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Creative Thinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Creative Thinking** is the cognitive process of generating novel, useful, and meaningful ideas, solutions, or products by exploring multiple perspectives, breaking conventional mental patterns, and combining existing knowledge in unconventional ways. In the context of **Product Design and Innovation (PEMET837)**, creative thinking is the foundational intellectual engine that drives the **ideation phase** of the product development lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** Creative thinking is the deliberate use of structured and unstructured mental techniques to move beyond convergent, single-solution reasoning toward **divergent**, multi-solution ideation that adds **value** to a product, process, or service.

### The Three Pillars of Creative Thinking

1. **Fluency** — The ability to generate a large number of ideas.
2. **Flexibility** — The ability to shift between categories or approaches.
3. **Originality** — The ability to produce statistically rare, novel ideas.
4. **Elaboration** — The ability to expand, refine, and detail an idea.

> [!NOTE]
> These four pillars (often called the **Torrance Four Factors**) are the standard evaluation metrics used by design schools worldwide and frequently appear in KTU Module 2 viva voce questions.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen** 🍳. A convergent thinker (a traditional cook) sees onions, tomatoes, and spices and only thinks of making a standard curry. A **creative thinker**, however, sees the same ingredients and asks: *"Can I make a sweet dessert? Can I ferment this into a drink? Can I dehydrate it into a crispy chip?"* — they recombine the same raw materials in unexpected ways to create something **new and valuable**.
