| Assembly & Service Manuals | Field operations | CO4 — Design |
| DFMEA / PFMEA (Failure Mode Analysis) | Risk mitigation | CO5 — Evaluate |

### 1.4 Governing Standards (Industry-Recognized)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Frameworks**
> - **VDI 2221** (German) and **Pahl & Beitz** — systematic detailing steps.
> - **ISO 128** — Technical drawing principles.
> - **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101** — Geometrical Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T).
> - **ISO 2768** — General tolerances for linear and angular dimensions.
> - **ISO 1302** — Surface texture indication.
> - **DFMA® (Boothroyd-Dewhurst)** — Design for Manufacture & Assembly heuristics.

### 1.5 Position in the Overall Design Workflow

The detail design stage is entered only when the embodiment design has been validated against requirements and the principle solution has been frozen. Any major change at this stage is **exponentially more expensive** than at the concept stage — a well-known rule of thumb in engineering economics is the **1-10-100 Rule** (1× at concept, 10× at embodiment, 100× at detail/manufacturing).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Detail Design

> [!NOTE]
> Detail design is **not** a single activity. It is a structured set of six interdependent sub-activities. KTU examiners frequently test these as 7-mark or 14-mark sub-parts.

1. **Dimensioning & Tolerancing**
   - Functional vs. non-functional dimensions
   - Plus/minus (bilateral/unilateral) vs. GD&T (geometric)
   - ISO 286 fit system (Hole basis / Shaft basis)
   - ISO 2768-m (medium) / -c (coarse) / -f (fine)

2. **Material Specification Refinement**
   - Exact grade (e.g., *AISI 304* not "stainless steel")
   - Heat treatment condition (e.g., *quenched & tempered to 35 HRC*)
   - Compliance marks (RoHS, REACH)

3. **Surface Engineering**
   - Surface roughness parameters: **Ra** (arithmetical mean), **Rz** (mean peak-to-valley), **Rt** (total height)
   - Surface lay, waviness, primary vs. secondary texture
   - Functional surfaces (bearing, sealing, fatigue-critical)

4. **Fastener & Joint Selection**
   - Bolts (ISO 4014/4017), screws (ISO 4762 socket head), rivets, welds, snap-fits
   - Thread series, strength grade (e.g., **8.8**, **10.9**, **12.9**)

5. **Manufacturing Process Selection (DFM)**
   - Casting, forging, machining, sheet metal, injection molding, additive
   - Minimum wall thickness, draft angle, parting line location

6. **Assembly Process Planning (DFA)**
   - Number of parts minimization (Boothroyd index)
   - Insertion direction analysis
   - Fastener reduction, modular sub-assemblies

### 2.2 Why Detail Design Matters — Engineering Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1-10-100-1000 Rule of Change Cost**
> A design change at the **concept stage** costs ~$1. A change at **embodiment** costs ~$10. A change at **detail design** costs ~$100. A change after the product is in the **field** costs ~$1,000+. This is why detail design must be *rigorous* and *frozen* before tooling release.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation, parameter, and unit a KTU 2024 student must know for any numerical sub-question on Detail Design. Note: vertical bars inside formulas are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ISO 286 Fit System** — Hole basis | $N_{\max} = ES$ ; $N_{\min} = EI$ | $N$ in **µm** | ES = upper deviation, EI = lower deviation |
| 2 | Fundamental Deviation (hole H) | $EI = 0$ | µm | Reference hole for hole-basis fits |
| 3 | Fundamental Deviation (shaft h) | $es = 0$ | µm | Reference shaft for hole-basis fits |
| 4 | Tolerance Grade (IT) | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | $i$ in µm, $D$ in mm | Standard tolerance unit for $D \le 500$ mm |
| 5 | Tolerance for grade IT-n | $T = n \cdot i$ | µm | $n$ is the IT grade number (01, 0, 1 … 18) |
| 6 | Allowance (interference/clearance) | $J_{\min} = EI_{\text{hole}} - es_{\text{shaft}}$ | µm | Positive = clearance, negative = interference |
| 7 | Tolerance Stack-up (worst case) | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | µm | Arithmetic sum — conservative |
| 8 | Tolerance Stack-up (statistical, RSS) | $T_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{\,2}}$ | µm | Root-Sum-Square for normal distributions |
| 9 | Surface Roughness — Ra | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | µm | Arithmetic mean deviation |
| 10 | Surface Roughness — Rz | $R_z = \dfrac{R_{1} + R_{2} + R_{3} + R_{4} + R_{5}}{5}$ | µm | Mean of 5 max peak-to-valley |
| 11 | Surface Roughness — Rt | $R_t = R_{\max} - R_{\min}$ | µm | Total height in evaluation length |
| 12 | Preferred Ra values (ISO 1302) | 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3, 12.5 | µm | Geometric series ratio 2 |
| 13 | Material Utilization Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{stock}}} \times 100\%$ | % | DFM efficiency indicator |
| 14 | DFA Assembly Efficiency (Boothroyd) | $E_m = \dfrac{N_m \cdot t_a}{t_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | dimensionless | $N_m$ = min parts, $t_a$ = avg time |
| 15 | Cost per Part (DFMA) | $C_p = C_m + C_t + C_a$ | ₹ / \$ | Material + Tooling + Assembly |
| 16 | Theoretical Minimum No. of Parts | $N_{\min} = f(N_{\text{relative motion}}, N_{\text{disassembly}})$ | count | Boothroyd heuristic |
| 17 | Draft Angle (molding/casting) | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | degrees | Typical $\alpha = 0.5° - 2°$ |
| 18 | Minimum Wall Thickness (injection) | $t_{\min} = k \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | mm | $k$ = material factor, $L$ = flow length |
| 19 | Welding Shrinkage Allowance | $L_{\text{cut}} = L_{\text{final}} \cdot (1 + \epsilon_s)$ | mm | $\epsilon_s$ = linear shrinkage fraction |
| 20 | Screw Pre-load Force | $F_M = \dfrac{F_K \cdot 0.8 \cdot d_2}{W \cdot \sin(\lambda) + 0.8 \cdot d_2 \cdot (\mu_T/2)}$ | N | K-T relationship, bolted joint |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive** (e.g., Maruti, Tata, Bosch India): Detail design output drives the **BOP (Bill of Process)** for Tier-1 suppliers; GD&T ensures global part interchangeability.
- **Heavy Engineering** (e.g., BHEL, L&T): Tolerance stack-ups determine alignment of turbines and pressure vessels.
- **Consumer Electronics** (e.g., Samsung, Apple India R&D): Surface finish (Ra) on cosmetic shells is a **marketing differentiator**.
- **Medical Devices** (e.g., Meril, Stryker India): Detail design includes **biocompatibility, sterilization method, and ISO 13485** traceability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Statistical Tolerance Stack-Up (Root-Sum-Square Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks students to *compare* worst-case vs. statistical tolerance accumulation. Statistical (RSS) is the engineering best-practice when dimensions are independent and normally distributed.

**Problem Setup:**
A shaft passes through three components in series. Each component has its own thickness tolerance. We want the clearance between the shaft shoulder and the third component.

**Given:**
- Component 1 thickness: $T_1 = 200 \pm 50 \,\mu\text{m}$  ⇒  $T_1 = 100 \,\mu\text{m}$
- Component 2 thickness: $T_2 = 150 \pm 40 \,\mu\text{m}$  ⇒  $T_2 = 80 \,\mu\text{m}$
- Component 3 thickness: $T_3 = 100 \pm 30 \,\mu\text{m}$  ⇒  $T_3 = 60 \,\mu\text{m}$

**Step 1 — Express each tolerance as a standard deviation equivalent**

For uniform distribution over the tolerance band, $\sigma_i = \dfrac{T_i}{\sqrt{3}}$. For a normal-distribution assumption (more realistic in CNC machining), use $\sigma_i \approx \dfrac{T_i}{6}$ (since the tolerance is the ±3σ spread).

We adopt the **6σ** (normal) convention:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{100}{6} = 16.67 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$\sigma_2 = \frac{80}{6} = 13.33 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$\sigma_3 = \frac{60}{6} = 10.00 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Sum variances (independent random variables)**

$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^{2} = \sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2} + \sigma_3^{2}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^{2} = (16.67)^{2} + (13.33)^{2} + (10.00)^{2}$$

Evaluating each term:
$$(16.67)^{2} = 277.89$$
$$(13.33)^{2} = 177.69$$
$$(10.00)^{2} = 100.00$$

Adding:
$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^{2} = 277.89 + 177.69 + 100.00 = 555.58 \,\mu\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root to obtain total standard deviation**

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{555.58} = 23.57 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to total tolerance (3σ band for 99.73% confidence)**

$$T_{\text{RSS}} = \pm \, 3 \sigma_{\text{total}} = \pm \, 3 \times 23.57 = \pm \, 70.71 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with worst-case arithmetic sum**

$$T_{\text{worst}} = 100 + 80 + 60 = 240 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate savings in tolerance budget**

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{worst}} - T_{\text{RSS}} = 240 - 141.42 = 98.58 \,\mu\text{m}$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{98.58}{240} \times 100\% \approx 41.1\%$$

**Conclusion:** The statistical method relaxes the assembly tolerance budget by **~41%**, allowing looser, cheaper individual parts while maintaining 99.73% yield.

### 3.2 Derivation B — ISO 286 Hole-Basis Fit Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
Select a fit between a 40 mm shaft and its mating hole for a "running fit" application.

**Step 1 — Identify the fit category**
Running fit → clearance fit → use **H7/g6** (medium running fit, hole-basis).

**Step 2 — Determine the fundamental deviation for the hole (H)**
For H-holes, the lower deviation is on the reference line:
$$EI = 0 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the tolerance grade IT7 for D = 40 mm**
Use the standard tolerance unit formula:
$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$$

Substituting $D = 40$:
$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{40} + 0.001 \cdot 40$$

Cube root of 40:
$$\sqrt[3]{40} = 3.420$$

Multiplying:
$$i = 0.45 \times 3.420 + 0.001 \times 40 = 1.539 + 0.040 = 1.579 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute IT7 tolerance**
For $D = 40$ mm, the standard ISO table gives IT7 = 25 µm. Cross-checking with the multiplier:
$$T_{IT7} \approx 16 \cdot i = 16 \times 1.579 \approx 25.3 \,\mu\text{m}$$

(Use 25 µm from the standard table — the 16i formula is an approximation.)

**Step 5 — Compute hole limits**
$$ES_{\text{hole}} = EI + IT7 = 0 + 25 = +25 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$EI_{\text{hole}} = 0 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$\therefore \text{Hole} = 40^{+0.025}_{+0.000} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Determine shaft deviation for g6**
For shaft-basis g, upper deviation $es = -f(D)$, where for IT6 at 40 mm, $f \approx 12$ µm (from ISO tables).
$$es_{\text{shaft}} = -12 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$IT6 = 16 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$ei_{\text{shaft}} = es - IT6 = -12 - 16 = -28 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$\therefore \text{Shaft} = 40^{-0.012}_{-0.028} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Compute clearances**
$$N_{\max} = ES_{\text{hole}} - ei_{\text{shaft}} = 25 - (-28) = 53 \,\mu\text{m}$$
$$N_{\min} = EI_{\text{hole}} - es_{\text{shaft}} = 0 - (-12) = 12 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The H7/g6 fit at 40 mm yields a clearance range of **+12 µm to +53 µm** — a robust running fit suitable for bearings, sliding sleeves, and precision bushings.

### 3.3 Derivation C — DFA Assembly Efficiency (Boothroyd Method)

**Problem Setup:** A pump assembly originally has 14 parts. After redesign, 4 parts were eliminated. Original assembly time = 280 s, minimum theoretical time = 180 s.

**Step 1 — Compute the Boothroyd Design Efficiency**
$$E_m = \frac{N_m \cdot t_a}{t_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Identify parameters**
$$N_m = 14 - 4 = 10 \text{ parts}$$
$$t_a = \frac{t_{\text{total, new}}}{N_m} = \frac{180}{10} = 18 \text{ s/part}$$
$$t_{\text{total}} = 180 \text{ s (theoretical)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency**
$$E_m = \frac{10 \times 18}{180} \times 100\% = \frac{180}{180} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Interpretation:** Achieving $E_m = 100\%$ is the *theoretical optimum*; in industrial practice, $E_m \ge 60\%$ is considered excellent, and $E_m \ge 80\%$ is a world-class DFA benchmark.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Manufacturing Process

| Process | Best Ra (µm) | Min Wall (mm) | Unit Cost @ Volume | Lead Time | Material Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sand Casting | 12.5 – 25 | 5 | Low | Long | All metals |
| Die Casting | 0.8 – 3.2 | 1 | Very Low (high vol) | Med | Al, Zn, Mg |
| CNC Machining | 0.2 – 1.6 | 0.5 | High | Short | All metals, plastics |
| Injection Molding | 0.4 – 1.6 | 0.4 | Low (high vol) | Long | Thermoplastics |
| Sheet Metal | 1.6 – 6.3 | 0.5 | Low | Short | Steel, Al |
| 3D Printing (SLA/SLS) | 6.3 – 25 | 0.3 | Very High (low vol) | Short | Polymers, some metals |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU visual**. Examiners may present a scenario and ask students to justify process selection. Always reference **Volume × Tolerance × Material × Cost** in the justification.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Detail Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Validated Embodiment Design] --> B[Freeze Functional Dimensions]
    B --> C[Apply ISO 286 Fit System]
    C --> D[GD&T per ASME Y14.5]
    D --> E[Material Grade Specification]
    E --> F[Surface Finish Selection ISO 1302]
    F --> G[Fastener and Joint Detailing]
    G --> H[DFM and DFA Review]
    H --> I{Quality Gate Passed?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Generate 2D Drawings and 3D Models]
    I -- No --> B
    J --> K[Bill of Materials BOM]
    K --> L[Assembly and Service Manuals]
    L --> M[Tooling Release and Manufacturing Handoff]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Tolerance Specification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Functional Requirement Identified] --> P2{Is it a Critical Dimension?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Tight Tolerance and GD&T Callout]
    P2 -- No --> P4[ISO 2768 General Tolerance]
    P3 --> P5[Statistical Stack Up Analysis RSS]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Tolerance Allocated to Each Feature]
    P6 --> P7[Drawing Released with Datum Reference Frame]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Inputs, Outputs, and Stakeholders of Detail Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Embodiment Layout] --> DD[Detail Design Phase]
    IN2[Material Property Database] --> DD
    IN3[Manufacturing Process Constraints] --> DD
    IN4[Regulatory Standards ISO ASME BIS] --> DD
    DD --> OUT1[Detailed Engineering Drawings]
    DD --> OUT2[Bill of Materials]
    DD --> OUT3[Process Sheets and Inspection Plans]
    DD --> OUT4[Service and Maintenance Manual]
    OUT1 --> STK1[Manufacturing Cell]
    OUT2 --> STK2[Procurement Team]
    OUT3 --> STK3[Quality Department]
    OUT4 --> STK4[Field Service Engineers]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Decomposition of Detail Design Activities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Detail Design] --> a1[Geometric Definition]
    root --> a2[Material Definition]
    root --> a3[Process Definition]
    a1 --> a1a[Dimensional Tolerances]
    a1 --> a1b[GD&T Symbols]
    a1 --> a1c[Datum Scheme]
    a2 --> a2a[Grade and Standard]
    a2 --> a2b[Heat Treatment]
    a2 --> a2c[Surface Treatment]
    a3 --> a3a[Primary Manufacturing]
    a3 --> a3b[Joining Methods]
    a3 --> a3c[Inspection Plan]
```

### 4.5 Input-Process-Output (IPO) Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Embodiment Drawings] --> proc[Detail Design Engine]
    inB[Standards Library] --> proc
    inC[Cost Target] --> proc
    proc --> outA[Frozen Engineering Package]
    outA --> dest[Manufacturing Execution]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU exams:** The IPO / SIPOC / block-level schematics are *high-scoring* visual answers. Whenever a 7-mark question asks to "explain detail design," a properly labeled block diagram can fetch 3–4 marks by itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define "Detail Design" in the context of systematic product design. List four key documents generated in this phase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Detail Design is the final stage of the product design methodology in which the validated embodiment design is elaborated into a complete, unambiguous, and manufacturing-ready technical specification, including all dimensions, tolerances, materials, surface finishes, and assembly details.

**Four Key Documents (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. Detailed engineering drawings (2D + 3D CAD models)
2. Bill of Materials (BOM)
3. Manufacturing process sheets / Inspection plans
4. Assembly and service manuals

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **State and briefly explain the difference between worst-case and statistical (RSS) tolerance stack-up methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Worst-case (Arithmetic) Method (1.5 marks):** $T_{\text{total}} = \sum T_i$. Assumes all dimensions simultaneously reach their extreme limits. Highly conservative; gives the largest tolerance band, requiring tighter (more expensive) individual tolerances.
- **Statistical / RSS Method (1.5 marks):** $T_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^{2}}$. Assumes dimensions are independent and normally distributed; provides a more realistic estimate of process variation. Allows looser individual tolerances and reduces manufacturing cost while maintaining acceptable yield.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Choose ONE of the following questions. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7).**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Manufacturing Process & Material Selection**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> (a) A 250 mm × 100 mm × 30 mm component is to be produced in **AISI 316L stainless steel** for a marine pump. The dimensional tolerance on a critical bore of Ø40 mm is **±25 µm**, and the required surface finish is **Ra 0.8 µm**. Compare **CNC machining** versus **investment casting** for this part. Justify your recommendation with reference to tolerance capability, surface finish, cost, and lead time. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Explain the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly (DFA)** methodology. A product originally had 17 parts with a total assembly time of 340 seconds. After DFA, the part count was reduced to 11, and the total assembly time dropped to 220 seconds. Calculate the **DFA efficiency** and **theoretical minimum part count** if the average ideal time per part is 15 seconds. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

| Criterion | CNC Machining | Investment Casting |
|---|---|---|
| Tolerance Capability | ±10 to ±50 µm (achievable) | ±200 to ±500 µm (typical) |
| Surface Finish Ra | 0.2 to 1.6 µm (excellent) | 1.6 to 6.3 µm (poor) |
| Cost @ Low Volume (1–50) | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| Cost @ High Volume (10k+) | High | Low |
| Lead Time | 1–3 weeks | 6–10 weeks (pattern + casting) |
| Material Utilization | Low (chips waste) | High (near-net shape) |

**Justification (full 7 marks):**
- The critical bore tolerance of **±25 µm** (IT6–IT7) and **Ra 0.8 µm** are **comfortably achievable by CNC machining** but would be **very difficult by investment casting** without secondary machining. (2 marks)
- CNC machining eliminates the need for tooling (patterns), which is beneficial for **low-to-medium volumes**. (1 mark)
- Casting, while cheaper at high volume, requires secondary finishing to meet the Ra target, **eroding cost advantage** at low volume. (1 mark)
- For a marine pump (corrosion resistance is key in 316L), both processes can handle the material, but CNC preserves the passivated surface integrity. (1.5 marks)
- **Recommendation:** Choose **CNC Machining** for the initial production (first 500–1000 units) due to lower lead time, tighter tolerance, and superior surface finish. Switch to **investment casting + finish machining** if volume exceeds 5,000 units annually. (1.5 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Methodology (3 marks):**
1. **Minimize part count** — combine parts that do not need relative motion or separate materials.
2. **Optimize insertion** — use vertical, single-axis insertions; eliminate screws, washers, and captive fasteners.
3. **Ease of handling** — parts should be self-locating and self-fastening.
4. **Secondary operations** — minimize re-orientations and tool changes.

**DFA Efficiency Calculation (4 marks):**
- Given: $N_{\text{original}} = 17$, $N_{\text{new}} = 11$, $t_{\text{original}} = 340$ s, $t_{\text{new}} = 220$ s, $t_a = 15$ s.
- Theoretical minimum parts:
$$N_{\min} = \frac{t_{\text{new}}}{t_a} = \frac{220}{15} \approx 14.67$$
  Rounding down to integer: $N_{\min} = 14$ parts (since 15 parts would exceed 220 s).
- New DFA efficiency:
$$E_m = \frac{N_{\min} \cdot t_a}{t_{\text{new}}} \times 100\% = \frac{14 \times 15}{220} \times 100\% = \frac{210}{220} \times 100\% \approx 95.45\%$$
- Improvement: Original efficiency
$$E_{m,\text{orig}} = \frac{17 \times 15}{340} \times 100\% = 75\%$$
- **Conclusion:** DFA redesign improved efficiency by **~20.45 percentage points**, with potential annual assembly cost savings.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Tolerance Engineering & GD&T**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**
> (a) A shaft of nominal diameter **50 mm** mates with a hole in a precision gearbox. Using the **ISO 286 hole-basis fit system**, calculate the hole and shaft limits for an **H7/k6** (transition) fit. Show all steps, including the standard tolerance unit calculation. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Explain the difference between **GD&T and traditional plus/minus tolerancing**. With a neat sketch, describe the meaning of **position tolerance Ø0.05 M at MMC** on a 4-hole bolt pattern. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Standard tolerance unit for D = 50 mm (1 mark):**
$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 = 0.45 \times 3.684 + 0.05 = 1.658 + 0.05 = 1.708 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — IT7 and IT6 from ISO tables (1 mark):**
For $D = 50$ mm:
$$IT7 = 25 \,\mu\text{m}, \quad IT6 = 16 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Hole (H7) limits (1.5 marks):**
- $EI = 0$ µm (H-holes)
- $ES = EI + IT7 = 0 + 25 = +25$ µm
- $$\therefore \text{Hole} = \varnothing \, 50^{+0.025}_{+0.000} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft (k6) limits (1.5 marks):**
- For k-shaft at $D = 50$ mm, fundamental deviation (from ISO tables) $es = +18$ µm
- $IT6 = 16$ µm
- $ei = es - IT6 = 18 - 16 = +2$ µm
- $$\therefore \text{Shaft} = \varnothing \, 50^{+0.018}_{+0.002} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum and minimum fit (2 marks):**
- $$N_{\max} = ES_{\text{hole}} - ei_{\text{shaft}} = 25 - 2 = +23 \,\mu\text{m (clearance)}$$
- $$N_{\min} = EI_{\text{hole}} - es_{\text{shaft}} = 0 - 18 = -18 \,\mu\text{m (interference)}$$

**Conclusion:** The H7/k6 fit at Ø50 mm is a **transition fit** with clearance ranging from −18 µm to +23 µm. It permits light interference pressing or sliding assembly, suitable for coupling hubs, gear pulleys, and bearing rings.

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Difference between GD&T and traditional ± tolerancing (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional ± | GD&T |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Implicit (edge-to-edge) | Explicit datums |
| Form errors | Not controlled | Controlled (flatness, cylindricity) |
| Tolerance zone | Rectangular box | Geometric (cylindrical, conical, profile) |
| Inspection | Calipers, micrometers | CMM, gauges, functional |
| Function | None built-in | Functional feature control frame |
| Bonus Tolerance | Not available | Available via MMC, LMC modifiers |

**Position Tolerance Explanation with Sketch (4 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The callout `⊕ Ø0.05 Ⓜ` means: *Each of the 4 holes must lie within a cylindrical tolerance zone of diameter 0.05 mm, measured from the true position, when the hole is at its **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)**, i.e., at its smallest allowable diameter.*

**Sketch Description (text-based — to be redrawn by student):**
- Datum reference frame: A (primary) = back face; B (secondary) = left side.
- 4 holes on a rectangular pattern.
- Feature Control Frame attached to each hole: `⊕ Ø0.05 Ⓜ A B`
- At MMC, tolerance zone is Ø0.05 mm.
- As the hole departs from MMC (i.e., becomes larger), the position tolerance zone can **expand** — this is the **bonus tolerance** advantage of MMC.

**Engineering Benefit:** MMC ensures the fastener can always be installed; bonus tolerance accommodates manufacturing variation without rejecting parts.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–4 Marks on Detail Design Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Hole-Basis vs. Shaft-Basis** — In hole-basis fits, the hole's lower deviation EI = 0 (for H). Many students mistakenly set EI = 0 for the *shaft* — read the question carefully!
> 2. **Mixing IT grade formulas** — The $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001D$ formula is valid only for $D \le 500$ mm. For $D > 500$ mm, different formulas apply.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — Tolerances are in **µm**, dimensions in **mm**. Always convert.
> 4. **Confusing Ra and Rz** — Ra is the arithmetic mean deviation; Rz is the mean of 5 peak-to-valley measurements. They are **not interchangeable** (typically $Rz \approx 4 \times Ra$ to $7 \times Ra$).
> 5. **No sketch on GD&T questions** — KTU 2024 scheme **demands a sketch** on GD&T questions. A textual answer without a labeled datum reference frame loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping "Justification" in process selection** — Don't just pick a process; *justify* using tolerance × volume × material × cost.
> 7. **Sign convention errors on fits** — Clearance is **positive**, interference is **negative**. Mixing signs reverses the conclusion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Detail Design (Module 2)**

- ☐ Detail Design = the **final, manufacturing-ready specification phase** of the systematic design methodology (Pahl & Beitz / VDI 2221).
- ☐ Six pillars: **Dimensioning, Material Spec, Surface Engineering, Fasteners, DFM, DFA.**
- ☐ ISO 286 fit system: **Hole-basis (H-holes, EI=0)** is the global default.
- ☐ Tolerance unit formula: $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001D$ µm for $D \le 500$ mm.
- ☐ Worst-case stack-up: arithmetic sum; Statistical (RSS): square-root-sum-square.
- ☐ Surface roughness standard: ISO 1302; preferred values: **0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3, 12.5 µm**.
- ☐ GD&T standards: **ASME Y14.5** and **ISO 1101** — callouts use a Feature Control Frame.
- ☐ MMC modifier (Ⓜ) allows **bonus tolerance**; LMC modifier (Ⓛ) does the opposite.
- ☐ DFA efficiency (Boothroyd): $E_m = (N_m \cdot t_a) / t_{\text{total}} \times 100\%$; aim for **≥ 60%**.
- ☐ Process selection always balances: **Volume × Tolerance × Material × Cost × Lead Time**.
- ☐ The **1-10-100-1000 rule** justifies why detail design must be thorough and "frozen."
- ☐ DFMEA / PFMEA is part of risk-aware detail design; ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 / AS9100 documentation is mandatory.
- ☐ Draft angles (0.5°–2°) and minimum wall thickness (0.4–2 mm) are critical in casting/molding.
- ☐ **Always draw a labeled diagram / block schematic** for full-mark answers in KTU 2024.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question contains a numerical tolerance or fit problem, **show every step** — definition of terms, table lookup, unit conversion, formula application, and final answer with units. KTU examiners reward methodology as much as the final value. A correct final number with no work shown may fetch only 1–2 marks out of 7.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Design: Generation, Selection and Embodiment of Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Design** is the phase of the product development process in which the design team identifies the working principles, generates multiple solution variants (concepts) for the design problem, evaluates them systematically, and finally combines the most promising partial solutions into a single **product concept** that best satisfies the requirements specification (PRD/ERS).

According to the **Pahl & Beitz systematic design methodology** (the standard reference framework adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Design courses), conceptual design consists of three sequential, iterative sub-phases:

1. **Concept Generation (Abstraction → Search for Working Principles → Combination)**
2. **Concept Selection (Evaluation against criteria using a Decision Matrix)**
3. **Embodiment of Concept (Refinement into a concrete, technically feasible layout)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition (Verbatim Expected Answer):**
> "Conceptual design is the phase of design where the problem is abstracted to its functional core, working principles are identified, solution concepts are generated and combined, and the most promising concept is selected and embodied — forming the bridge between the specification and the detailed design."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cooking Recipe" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are asked to cook a meal for 50 guests, but you have no fixed recipe. What do you do?
> - **Step 1 (Abstraction):** You ask: "What does the meal *need to do*?" — Feed 50 people, taste good, be ready in 2 hours, cost under ₹2000. These are your **requirements** (functions).
> - **Step 2 (Generation):** You brainstorm: biryani, pasta, fried rice, dosa, sandwiches, etc. Each is a **concept variant**.
> - **Step 3 (Selection):** You score each option against your constraints (cost, time, taste, equipment) using a scoring matrix.
> - **Step 4 (Embodiment):** You pick "Biryani", then decide on the *concrete recipe* — basmati rice, dum cooking, chicken/mutton, masala ratios, serving vessels. This is the **embodiment**.

**Conceptual Design = the creative + analytical bridge between "what we want" (Specification) and "what we will build" (Detailed Design).**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary Standards)

| Term | Standard Definition | KTU 2024 Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Function (F)** | A logical operation independent of any solution | $F = \{f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n\}$ |
| **Working Principle (WP)** | A physical effect used to fulfill a function | e.g., "Leverage," "Capillary action" |
| **Concept Variant** | A specific combination of working principles for sub-functions | $C_i = \{WP_{i,1}, WP_{i,2}, \dots\}$ |
| **Concept** | The overall technical solution idea that satisfies the design problem | Embodied in sketches, CAD block models |
| **Overall Concept** | The winning concept variant after selection | The candidate passed to embodiment |
| **Embodiment** | The geometric, spatial, and material concretization of the overall concept | First embodiment → definitive embodiment |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Conventions in Conceptual Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded Key Metrics / Standard Scores Used in KTU Boards:**
> - **Pugh Concept Selection Score** → ranges from $-n$ to $+n$ where $n$ = number of selection criteria.
> - **Weighted Score** $= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ where $w_i$ is the weight of criterion $i$ and $r_{ij}$ is the rating of concept $j$ on criterion $i$. **Weights must satisfy:** $\sum w_i = 1.0$ (or 100 %).
> - **Concept Variety Coefficient** → used to ensure generation phase produces diverse concepts.
> - **Technical-Economic Criteria Split:** typically 60 % technical / 40 % economic in B2B products.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Sub-Phases — Logical Flow

### **Phase 2.1: Generation of Concepts**

The generation phase is a **divergent, creative, and non-evaluative** process. It follows the Pahl–Beitz–Wallace problem-solving cycle.

**Step 2.1.1 — Abstraction of the Problem**

The concrete product brief is reformulated into an **abstract, function-oriented** problem statement.

> [!NOTE]
> Example — A table fan:
> - **Concrete problem:** "Design a fan with 3 blades, 1200 RPM, ₹1500 cost."
> - **Abstract problem:** "Increase the velocity of air in a room by a controllable amount using low-cost electrical energy."

This abstraction unlocks radically different solutions (bladeless fan, evaporative cooler, ceiling fan, USB desk fan).

**Step 2.1.2 — Establishment of the Function Structure**

The overall function is broken down into sub-functions using a **black-box model**, then a **function structure** (a chain of sub-functions and flows of energy / material / signal).

$$
F_{\text{overall}} \;=\; f_1 \circ f_2 \circ f_3 \circ \dots \circ f_n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The function structure is **solution-neutral**. It represents *what must be done*, not *how it is done*. KTU 2024 boards explicitly credit this abstraction in 14-mark answers.

**Step 2.1.3 — Search for Working Principles**

For **each** sub-function, a **Working Principle Catalogue** is consulted. The classical catalogues include:

- **Koller catalogues** (mechanical: leverage, cam, screw, gear, hydraulic, pneumatic, magnetic)
- **Roth catalogues** (physical effects: thermal expansion, piezoelectricity, photoelectric effect, Bernoulli principle)
- **VDI 2225** standard catalogue

**Step 2.1.4 — Combination into Concept Variants**

Each sub-function is paired with one or more working principles, and a **morphological matrix** is constructed. Combinations yield a vast concept space:

$$
N_{\text{concepts}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} m_i
$$

where $m_i$ = number of working principles available for sub-function $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> If a function structure has 5 sub-functions and each has 4 working principles, the theoretical concept space is $4^5 = 1024$ concepts. This shows why **systematic selection** is mandatory.

---

### **Phase 2.2: Selection of Concept**

This is a **convergent, analytical** process. The goal is to choose the most promising concept variant based on the requirements specification.

**Step 2.2.1 — Establish Selection Criteria**

Criteria are derived from the **Requirements List (PRD)** and grouped into:

- **Technical criteria (T):** e.g., function fulfillment, safety, reliability, manufacturability, weight
- **Economic criteria (E):** e.g., production cost, lifecycle cost, market price
- **Other criteria:** ergonomics, aesthetics, recyclability, legal compliance

**Step 2.2.2 — Assign Weights**

Weights reflect the **relative importance** of each criterion. Sum of weights = 1.0 (or 100 %).

**Step 2.2.3 — Evaluate Concepts**

Two standard KTU-prescribed methods:

**Method A — Pugh Concept Selection Matrix (Qualitative)**

A **datum (reference) concept** is chosen (often an existing product or a "do-nothing" baseline). Each other concept is rated against the datum using:

- $+1$ → Better than datum
- $0$ → Equal to datum
- $-1$ → Worse than datum

$$
S_{\text{Pugh},j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{ij}
$$

where $c_{ij} \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$. Concept with the highest score wins.

**Method B — Weighted Decision Matrix (Quantitative)**

$$
S_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}
$$

where:
- $w_i$ = weight of criterion $i$ (with $\sum w_i = 1$)
- $r_{ij}$ = rating (on a 0–10 scale) of concept $j$ on criterion $i$
- $S_j$ = total weighted score of concept $j$

The concept with the highest $S_j$ is selected.

**Step 2.2.4 — Iterate**

Often, the best concept is **further refined** by borrowing working principles from lower-scoring concepts — called **concept combination** or **hybridization**.

---

### **Phase 2.3: Embodiment of the Concept**

The selected concept is now **made concrete**. Embodiment answers: *"What exact components, layouts, materials, and manufacturing routes will realize this concept?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU expects two levels of embodiment:**
> 1. **Preliminary (First) Embodiment:** Overall layout, principal dimensions, material selection rationale, rough assembly.
> 2. **Definitive Embodiment:** Detailed tolerances, manufacturing process selection (DFM), DFA analysis, costed BOM, technical risk mitigation.

Key embodiment activities:

- **Layout sketches / layout drawings** (draft, intermediate, final)
- **Material selection** using Ashby charts (performance indices $P = f(\text{properties})$)
- **Process selection** (casting, forging, injection molding, additive manufacturing)
- **DFA (Design for Assembly) — Boothroyd index:**
$$
\text{DFA efficiency} = \frac{N_{\text{min}}}{N_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%
$$
where $N_{\text{min}}$ = theoretical minimum number of parts and $N_{\text{actual}}$ = actual number of parts.
- **DFM analysis** — rules for castability, machinability, weldability.
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA):**
$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$
where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection (each 1–10). High RPN items are redesigned.
- **Cost estimation** using **DFA costed method:**
$$
C_{\text{unit}} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{component}} + C_{\text{assembly}} + C_{\text{overhead}}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete, board-ready formula set** for the Conceptual Design module. Memorize column headers, units, and the constraint row.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol Meaning | Equation | Units / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of concept variants in morphological matrix | $N_{C}$ | $N_C = \prod_{i=1}^{n} m_i$ | Pure number; $m_i \geq 1$ |
| 2 | Pugh score of concept $j$ | $S_{Pugh,j}$ | $S_{Pugh,j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{ij}, \quad c_{ij} \in \{-1,0,+1\}$ | Range $[-n, +n]$ |
| 3 | Weighted decision-matrix score of concept $j$ | $S_j$ | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | Range depends on rating scale |
| 4 | Normalized weight constraint | — | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1.0$ (or 100 %) | Mandatory; lose marks if violated |
| 5 | DFA efficiency | $\eta_{\text{DFA}}$ | $\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \dfrac{N_{\min}}{N_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | FMEA Risk Priority Number | RPN | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Range $[1, 1000]$; S,O,D $\in [1,10]$ |
| 7 | Unit product cost | $C_{\text{unit}}$ | $C_{\text{unit}} = C_{\text{mat}} + C_{\text{comp}} + C_{\text{asm}} + C_{\text{ovh}}$ | ₹ / $ / € |
| 8 | Ashby performance index (general) | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{F^a}{M^b \cdot C^c}$ | Used for material selection |
| 9 | Cost-weighted economic score | $S_E$ | $S_E = \dfrac{C_{\text{baseline}}}{C_j}$ | Ratio $\geq 1$ preferred |
| 10 | Concept hybridisation gain | $\Delta S$ | $\Delta S = S_{\text{hybrid}} - \max(S_{\text{parents}})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ to justify |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Conceptual Design is used in industry:**
> - **Automotive OEM** (Tata, Maruti, Tesla): generating 100+ concept variants for a new car platform, narrowing to 3 for prototype.
> - **Medical Devices** (Philips, Medtronic): embodiment must satisfy FDA / ISO 13485 risk analysis (FMEA-driven).
> - **Consumer Electronics** (Apple, Samsung): concept selection heavily weighted on aesthetics and ergonomics; DFA used to minimize part count (e.g., unibody MacBook).
> - **Aerospace** (Boeing, HAL): embodiment uses fail-safe redundancy, often 2-of-3 voting, demanding RPN < 100.
> - **KTU B.Tech Projects:** mandatory in capstone projects — generating ≥ 3 concepts, Pugh matrix, embodiment sketch, BOM, costed DFA.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example: "Design of a Manual Juice Extractor for a Low-Income Household"

This is a **canonical KTU 2018–2024 question pattern** — a 14-mark problem requiring all three sub-phases.

### **Step 3.1.1 — Requirements Extraction**

| S.No | Requirement (Demand) | Wish (Optional) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extract juice from citrus (lemon, orange, sweet lime) | — |
| 2 | Output ≥ 50 ml per fruit | ≥ 80 ml per fruit |
| 3 | Manual operation; no electricity | — |
| 4 | Cycle time ≤ 60 s per fruit | ≤ 30 s |
| 5 | Easy to clean | Dishwasher-safe |
| 6 | Production cost ≤ ₹150 | ≤ ₹100 |
| 7 | No sharp accessible edges | — |
| 8 | Lifespan ≥ 1 year | ≥ 3 years |

### **Step 3.1.2 — Function Abstraction (Black Box)**

> [!NOTE]
> The black box is **solution-neutral**. The inputs are the fruit + manual force; the output is juice. Energy conversion must occur.

**Inputs (flows):**
- Material: fruit
- Energy: human muscle force
- Signal: operator intention

**Output (flows):**
- Material: juice + pulp residue
- Energy: none (or waste)
- Signal: none

### **Step 3.1.3 — Function Structure Decomposition**

We break $F_{\text{overall}}$ into sub-functions:

$$
F_{\text{overall}} = f_1 \circ f_2 \circ f_3 \circ f_4
$$

where:
- $f_1$ = Hold / Position the fruit
- $f_2$ = Apply compressive / shearing force
- $f_3$ = Break the internal cells (rupture juice sacs)
- $f_4$ = Channel juice out / separate from pulp

### **Step 3.1.4 — Working Principle Catalogue (per sub-function)**

| Sub-function | Working Principle Options |
|---|---|
| $f_1$ (Hold) | Conical cavity, Clamp, Adhesion, Gravity well |
| $f_2$ (Compress) | Lever, Screw, Cam, Hydraulic lever, Toggle |
| $f_3$ (Rupture) | Tapered cone, Reamer, Serrated teeth, Corrugated rollers |
| $f_4$ (Channel) | Sieve, Filter cone, Funnel, Centrifugal channel |

### **Step 3.1.5 — Morphological Matrix Construction**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU 14-mark high-yield table**. You must draw it in the answer sheet with the $\square$ symbols representing available combinations.

| Sub-function | **WP-A** | **WP-B** | **WP-C** | **WP-D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1$ Hold | Conical cavity | Spring clamp | Magnetic | Gravity well |
| $f_2$ Compress | Lever arm | Screw thread | Cam handle | Toggle |
| $f_3$ Rupture | Tapered reamer | Serrated cone | Twin rollers | Bristles |
| $f_4$ Channel | Sieve disc | Funnel | Filter cone | Centrifuge |

**Number of variants:**
$$
N_C = 4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 4^4 = 256 \text{ concepts (theoretical maximum)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Obviously we cannot evaluate 256 concepts. The **generation phase** aims to keep 4–6 promising variants, and the **selection phase** narrows to 1. Practical filtering is by:
> - Physical compatibility (e.g., magnetic + citrus = no, corrosion)
> - Manufacturing feasibility at ₹150 (e.g., centrifuge = too expensive)
> - Safety (e.g., sharp serrated teeth = fails safety demand)

### **Step 3.1.6 — Practical Concept Filtering (Down-selection to 4 candidates)**

After feasibility filtering:

- **Concept 1 (C1):** Lever-cone-sieve (traditional Indian lemon-squeezer)
- **Concept 2 (C2):** Screw-reamer-funnel (screw-press type)
- **Concept 3 (C3):** Lever-serrated cone-filter cone (kitchen squeezer with filter)
- **Concept 4 (C4 — Datum):** Existing market product (bought for ₹200) — used as Pugh datum

### **Step 3.1.7 — Weighted Decision Matrix Construction**

**Selection criteria with weights:**

| i | Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Juice yield per fruit | 0.25 | Demand 1 + 2 |
| 2 | Cycle time | 0.10 | Demand 4 |
| 3 | Manufacturing cost | 0.20 | Demand 6 |
| 4 | Ease of cleaning | 0.15 | Demand 5 |
| 5 | Operator safety | 0.15 | Demand 7 |
| 6 | Durability | 0.10 | Demand 8 |
| 7 | Aesthetic / ergonomics | 0.05 | Wish |
| **Total** | — | **1.00** | $\sum w_i = 1.00$ ✓ |

**Rating on 0–10 scale:**

| Criterion | $w_i$ | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 (Datum) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Yield | 0.25 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| 2. Cycle time | 0.10 | 7 | 5 | 7 | 8 |
| 3. Cost | 0.20 | 9 | 6 | 8 | 6 |
| 4. Cleanability | 0.15 | 7 | 5 | 9 | 6 |
| 5. Safety | 0.15 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| 6. Durability | 0.10 | 8 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| 7. Aesthetics | 0.05 | 6 | 5 | 8 | 9 |
| **Weighted Score $S_j$** | — | **7.85** | **6.70** | **8.20** | **6.95** |

**Detailed score computation for Concept 3 (C3) — full derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C3} &= (0.25)(8) + (0.10)(7) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(9) + (0.15)(9) + (0.10)(7) + (0.05)(8) \\
S_{C3} &= 2.00 + 0.70 + 1.60 + 1.35 + 1.35 + 0.70 + 0.40 \\
S_{C3} &= 8.10
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Concept 3 wins with the highest weighted score. It is selected as the **Overall Concept** for embodiment.

### **Step 3.1.8 — Pugh Cross-check (using C4 as datum)**

| Criterion | C4 (Datum) | C1 | C2 | C3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield | 0 | +1 | +1 | +1 |
| Cycle time | 0 | 0 | -1 | 0 |
| Cost | 0 | +1 | -1 | +1 |
| Cleanability | 0 | 0 | -1 | +1 |
| Safety | 0 | +1 | -1 | +1 |
| Durability | 0 | 0 | +1 | 0 |
| Aesthetics | 0 | -1 | -1 | -1 |
| **Pugh Score $S_{Pugh}$** | **0** | **+2** | **−3** | **+3** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pugh method confirms: **C3 is the strongest concept** with $S_{Pugh} = +3$.

### **Step 3.1.9 — Embodiment of the Selected Concept (C3)**

> [!NOTE]
> Embodiment = making the concept real. KTU expects: layout sketch (text-described), material selection with Ashby justification, DFA analysis, FMEA table, BOM.

**Overall layout (text description):**
A two-piece hinged clamshell device. Lower half is a bowl with a raised conical reamer (serrated, polypropylene) for citrus. Upper half has a lever arm pivoted at the rear, with a matching cone pressing downward. A removable fine-mesh filter cone sits in the bowl spout.

**Material selection (Ashby performance index):**

For the reamer cone — needs stiffness + food contact + low cost. Performance index:
$$
P_{\text{cone}} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho \cdot C_p}
$$

| Candidate | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (g/cm³) | $C_p$ (₹/kg) | $P_{\text{rel}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PP (Polypropylene) | 1.5 | 0.91 | 120 | **2.45** |
| ABS | 2.3 | 1.05 | 180 | 1.46 |
| Aluminum 6061 | 69 | 2.70 | 320 | 0.62 |
| Stainless 304 | 193 | 8.00 | 280 | 0.42 |

**Decision: Polypropylene (food-grade, low cost, injection-moldable).**

**DFA Analysis:**

| Component | Function | Can it be combined? | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lever arm | Apply force | No | Pivoting required |
| Hinge pin | Pivot | No | Standard part |
| Upper cone | Press fruit | No | Reamer geometry needed |
| Lower bowl | Hold + Channel | Maybe | Combine with filter holder? |
| Filter mesh | Filter pulp | No | Removable for cleaning |
| Bowl spout | Pour juice | Yes — merge with bowl | Single piece |
| Latch | Hold closed | No | Functional |

Theoretical minimum parts $N_{\min} = 5$. Actual = 6. DFA efficiency:
$$
\eta_{\text{DFA}} = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 = \frac{5}{6} \times 100 = 83.3\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industry benchmark: $\eta_{\text{DFA}} \geq 75\%$ is acceptable; $\geq 90\%$ is excellent. **83.3 % passes.**

**FMEA on Concept C3:**

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ | RPN | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lever cracks at pivot | Device unusable | 7 | 3 | 4 | 84 | Increase pin diameter from 4 mm to 6 mm |
| 2 | Filter mesh tears | Pulp in juice | 5 | 4 | 5 | 100 | Use 304 SS mesh, 80 µm |
| 3 | Latch disengages mid-press | Splash | 4 | 2 | 3 | 24 | Add detent click |
| 4 | Hinge pin corrodes | Stiff action | 6 | 3 | 6 | 108 | Use nylon bush + SS pin |
| 5 | Bowl cracks if dropped | Leakage | 7 | 5 | 5 | 175 | Reinforce rib at bottom |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Items with RPN > 100 must be redesigned. Items 4 and 5 require action.

**Costed BOM:**

| Part | Material | Process | Qty | Unit cost ₹ | Subtotal ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upper body | PP | Injection molding | 1 | 28 | 28 |
| Lower body | PP | Injection molding | 1 | 25 | 25 |
| Reamer cones | PP | Injection molding | 2 | 6 | 12 |
| Filter mesh | SS 304 | Stamping | 1 | 8 | 8 |
| Hinge pin | SS 304 | Machining | 1 | 5 | 5 |
| Latch spring | Spring steel | Stamping | 1 | 3 | 3 |
| Assembly labor | — | Manual | — | 10 | 10 |
| Packaging | Cardboard | — | 1 | 7 | 7 |
| Overhead (15 %) | — | — | — | — | 14.7 |
| **TOTAL** | — | — | — | — | **112.70** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Production cost = ₹112.70**, comfortably below the ₹150 ceiling. Concept C3 is **technically and economically feasible.**

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — The Pahl–Beitz Conceptual Design Process

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram captures the **complete flow** of conceptual design as prescribed by Pahl & Beitz and adopted by KTU 2024. Each node represents a deliverable; each arrow represents a logical dependency.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRD and Requirements List] --> B[Abstraction of Problem]
    B --> C[Function Structure F equals f1 o f2 o f3 ...]
    C --> D[Working Principle Catalogue Search]
    D --> E[Morphological Matrix Construction]
    E --> F[Concept Variants Generation C1 to Cm]
    F --> G[Establish Selection Criteria and Weights]
    G --> H{Pugh Matrix OR Weighted Decision Matrix}
    H --> I[Identify Top Concept Cov]
    I --> J[Concept Hybridization if Needed]
    J --> K[First Embodiment: Layout and Material]
    K --> L[DFA Analysis]
    L --> M[FMEA Risk Mitigation]
    M --> N[Costed BOM]
    N --> O{Concept Passes Feasibility Gate?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Definitive Embodiment: Detailed Drawings]
    O -- No --> G
    P --> Q[Output to Detailed Design Phase]
```

---

## 4.2 Morphological Matrix Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **morphological matrix** as a Cartesian product of working-principle sets. Each cell intersection is a potential concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F1["Sub-function f1 HOLD"]
        A1[WP1a: Conical cavity]
        A2[WP1b: Clamp]
        A3[WP1c: Magnetic]
        A4[WP1d: Gravity well]
    end
    subgraph F2["Sub-function f2 COMPRESS"]
        B1[WP2a: Lever]
        B2[WP2b: Screw]
        B3[WP2c: Cam]
        B4[WP2d: Toggle]
    end
    subgraph F3["Sub-function f3 RUPTURE"]
        C1[WP3a: Tapered reamer]
        C2[WP3b: Serrated cone]
        C3[WP3c: Twin rollers]
        C4[WP3d: Bristles]
    end
    subgraph F4["Sub-function f4 CHANNEL"]
        D1[WP4a: Sieve]
        D2[WP4b: Funnel]
        D3[WP4c: Filter cone]
        D4[WP4d: Centrifuge]
    end
    F1 --> X[Concept Variants]
    F2 --> X
    F3 --> X
    F4 --> X
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Selection Loop (Iterative Refinement)

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the **convergent loop** of concept selection and how lower-scoring concepts can feed back into the top concept via hybridization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[C1 Concept] --> S[Selection Matrix]
    C2[C2 Concept] --> S
    C3[C3 Concept] --> S
    C4[C4 Concept Datum] --> S
    S --> Score[Compute Weighted Score Sj]
    Score --> Rank[Rank Concepts]
    Rank --> Top[Top Concept Cstar]
    Top --> Emb[Embodiment]
    Rank --> Low[Low Scored Concepts]
    Low --> Hybrid[Extract Best Working Principles]
    Hybrid --> Top
    Emb --> Verify{Feasibility OK?}
    Verify -- No --> S
    Verify -- Yes --> Out[Output Overall Concept]
```

---

## 4.4 Embodiment Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OvC[Overall Concept from Selection] --> PE[Preliminary Embodiment]
    PE --> Lay[Layout Sketch: Draft and Final]
    PE --> Mat[Material Selection via Ashby]
    PE --> DFA[DFA Part Count Optimization]
    PE --> FMEA[Failure Mode Analysis]
    Lay --> DE[Definitive Embodiment]
    Mat --> DE
    DFA --> DE
    FMEA --> DE
    DE --> Tol[Tolerance Stack Up]
    DE --> Proc[Manufacturing Process Selection]
    DE --> Cost[Costed BOM and Lifecycle Cost]
    Tol --> Ready[Ready for Detailed Design]
    Proc --> Ready
    Cost --> Ready
```
