# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Industrial Design. Mention any two professional bodies associated with it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Industrial Design (ID)** is the professional practice of creating and developing concepts and specifications that optimize the function, value, and appearance of products and systems for the mutual benefit of users and manufacturers. It is a multi-disciplinary field integrating art, engineering, and business.

**Two professional bodies:**
1. **WDO (World Design Organization)** — formerly ICSID; sets global standards for industrial design practice.
2. **CII (Confederation of Indian Industry) – Design Division** — promotes design adoption in Indian industry.

*(Alternative valid bodies: IDSA – Industrial Designers Society of America, BIDA – Bangalore Industrial Design Association.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Industrial Design and Engineering Design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Industrial Design | Engineering Design |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Form, aesthetics, user experience | Function, mechanism, performance |
| Decision basis | Qualitative (taste, comfort, perception) | Quantitative (tolerance, stress, FEA) |
| Tools | Sketches, foam models, renderings | CAD, CAE, FEA, CFD |
| Output | CMF, ergonomic geometry, brand language | Functional BOM, mechanism, GD&T |
| Example | Designing a coffee mug's handle shape | Designing the mug's thermal insulation layer |

Industrial Design is *human-centred*, while Engineering Design is *technology-centred*. Together, they form **Product Design**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **7-stage Industrial Design process** in detail, with emphasis on the Empathize and Define stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **need for Industrial Design** in modern product development with **five specific justifications** and real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q1(a) — 7 Stages of ID Process (7 Marks)**

The Industrial Design process is a structured, iterative, human-centred methodology. The seven stages are:

**1. Empathize (1 mark):** This is the discovery stage. The designer immerses in the user's life through interviews, field observations, contextual inquiry, and empathy mapping. The goal is to understand *what people really need*, beyond what they say. *Example:* Observing elderly users opening medicine bottles reveals grip-strength limitations that surveys miss.

**2. Define (1 mark):** All research insights are synthesized into a clear **Point-of-View (POV)** statement and a **Product Design Specification (PDS)**. The PDS lists quantitative targets (weight, size, cost, ergonomics). This stage prevents scope creep.

**3. Ideate (1 mark):** A large volume of concepts is generated using brainstorming, SCAMPER, and sketching. Quantity encourages quality. The 50–100 thumbnail sketch rule is standard practice.

**4. Prototype (1 mark):** Selected concepts are made tangible through paper models, foam mock-ups, 3D prints, and functional prototypes. Prototypes turn *ideas into evidence*.

**5. Test (1 mark):** Prototypes are evaluated with real users using usability testing, A/B comparison, and heuristic evaluation. Feedback is captured systematically.

**6. Refine (1 mark):** Based on test feedback, the design is iterated. CMF (Colour, Material, Finish), surface detailing, and DFM (Design for Manufacturing) checks are finalized. Brand language integration occurs here.

**7. Implement (1 mark):** The final design is handed over to manufacturing with full CAD, BOM, and packaging. Marketing renders and brand assets are prepared for launch.

> The process is **iterative** — feedback loops exist between stages, especially between Prototype, Test, and Refine.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q1(b) — Need for Industrial Design (7 Marks)**

The need for Industrial Design arises from five core justifications:

**1. Aesthetic Appeal (1.5 marks):** In a saturated marketplace, products must visually attract consumers. Studies show **62%–90% of purchase decisions** are influenced by colour and form. *Example:* The Coca-Cola contour bottle is recognizable globally by shape alone.

**2. Ergonomic Comfort and Safety (1.5 marks):** Products must fit the human body. Poor ergonomics causes musculoskeletal disorders. *Example:* Oxo Good Grips kitchen tools are designed for arthritic hands, demonstrating inclusive design.

**3. Usability and User Experience (1.5 marks):** A product must be intuitive. Don Norman's principle — *the design should communicate its use* — drives iconic products. *Example:* The iPhone's single home button made smartphones usable for billions, including first-time users and the elderly.

**4. Commercial and Brand Value (1.5 marks):** ID differentiates products and builds brand equity. *Example:* The BMW kidney grille is a consistent brand language cue across 100+ years and 20+ models, creating instant recognition and premium positioning.

**5. Sustainability and Social Responsibility (1 mark):** Modern ID integrates Design for Environment (DfE) — recyclability, repairability, and low carbon footprint. *Example:* Fairphone's modular smartphone enables easy repair, reducing e-waste.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q1b):**
> Many students write generic statements like *"good design is important"* without **specific numerical data, real product examples, or design principles**. This caps marks at 3/7. To score full marks:
> - Cite **at least one quantitative figure** (e.g., 62% colour-influence statistic).
> - Provide **named product examples** (Coca-Cola, iPhone, Oxo, BMW).
> - Reference **design principles or standards** (Don Norman, ISO 6385, ESPR).
> - Use a **structured framework** (the 5 E's mnemonic is recommended).

---

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of an industrial designer** as a translator between user, engineering, marketing, and manufacturing domains. Illustrate with the case of any consumer electronic product. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Double Diamond framework** of design. Compare it with the **traditional linear design process** and explain why iteration is critical. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q2(a) — Role of the Industrial Designer (7 Marks)**

The industrial designer is a **multi-disciplinary professional** who acts as a **translator** between four critical domains, ensuring that the final product is *desirable, feasible, viable, and sustainable* — the four pillars of successful product development.

**1. User ↔ Designer (2 marks):**
The designer interprets latent user needs, frustrations, and aspirations through empathy research. They translate these into design requirements such as ergonomics, intuitive affordances, and emotional cues.
*Example in smartphones:* Industrial designers of the iPhone identified that users wanted a single hand-friendly, intuitive device — leading to the elimination of physical keyboards and a minimalist single-button interface.

**2. Engineering ↔ Designer (2 marks):**
The designer works with engineers to ensure the **stylistic form is manufacturable** and the **engineering constraints are aesthetically elegant**. They propose solutions that satisfy both mechanical and visual demands.
*Example:* The unibody aluminium chassis of a MacBook is a design-engineering collaboration — it is visually seamless *and* structurally rigid, achieved through CNC milling.

**3. Marketing ↔ Designer (1.5 marks):**
The designer translates **brand values into form language** — colours, materials, finishes, and proportions — that resonate with the target segment.
*Example:* The premium black-and-brass CMF of a OnePlus phone signals "affordable luxury," aligning with its brand positioning.

**4. Manufacturing ↔ Designer (1.5 marks):**
The designer ensures that the form is **tooling-friendly** — with appropriate draft angles, uniform wall thickness, and minimal parting lines.
*Example:* The two-piece shell of a Braun electric shaver is designed for single-shot injection moulding, reducing assembly cost.

> The industrial designer is therefore the **"generalist specialist"** — deeply skilled in form-giving, broadly aware of all four domains.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q2(b) — Double Diamond Framework & Iteration (7 Marks)**

**The Double Diamond Framework (3 marks):**
Developed by the **UK Design Council (2005)**, the Double Diamond is a non-linear design model consisting of two diamonds representing divergent and convergent thinking:

- **Diamond 1 — Discover & Define (Divergent → Convergent):**
  - **Discover** = Empathize, Research, Observe (divergent — explore widely).
  - **Define** = Synthesize insights, frame the problem (convergent — focus tightly).

- **Diamond 2 — Develop & Deliver (Divergent → Convergent):**
  - **Develop** = Ideate, Prototype, Iterate (divergent — explore solutions widely).
  - **Deliver** = Test, Refine, Launch (convergent — finalize one solution).

**Comparison with Traditional Linear Design (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Design | Double Diamond |
|---|---|---|
| Process flow | Sequential, one-way | Iterative, feedback-looped |
| Problem framing | Fixed at start | Reframed after research |
| User input | Often minimal | Continuous, central |
| Risk of failure | High (late discovery) | Low (early validation) |
| Reflection points | None | Multiple checkpoints |

**Why Iteration is Critical (2 marks):**

Iteration is the **engine of good design** because:
1. **First solutions are rarely optimal** — convergent thinking at the wrong time kills creativity.
2. **User needs evolve** with each test cycle.
3. **Manufacturing constraints** often emerge only during prototyping.
4. **Empirical evidence** (a tested prototype) is more reliable than expert opinion.
5. Famous designs (the Dyson vacuum took 5,127 prototypes) demonstrate that iteration is the path to breakthrough innovation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q2b):**
> A common mistake is drawing the Double Diamond as a single diamond or as two parallel diamonds. The correct structure is **two diamonds placed sequentially**, each with a **divergent (expand) and convergent (focus) phase**. Marks are lost for incorrect diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — 60-second scan:**

- ⭐ **Industrial Design** = Form + Function + Emotion + Brand + Sustainability.
- ⭐ **7 Stages of ID Process:** Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test → Refine → Implement (with iteration loops).
- ⭐ **5 Needs for ID (Mnemonic: 5 E's):** Aesthetics, Ergonomics, Ease of Use (Usability), Economics (Commercial), Eco-sustainability.
- ⭐ **ID is NOT the same as Product Design** — ID focuses on *form, aesthetics, and user experience*; Product Design is the *holistic umbrella*; Engineering Design is *function and mechanism*.
- ⭐ **Key tools/methods:** Empathy map, Persona, PDS, SCAMPER, Brainstorming, Foam model, 3D print, Usability test, A/B test, Weighted selection matrix, CMF, DFM, DfE.
- ⭐ **Standards to remember:** ISO 6385 (Ergonomics), ISO 9241-210 (Human-Centred Design), Nielsen's 10 Heuristics, ESPR (EU 2024), Pantone colour systems.
- ⭐ **Famous industrial designers to mention:** Dieter Rams (Braun), Jony Ive (Apple), Raymond Loewy (Coca-Cola, Shell), Charles and Ray Eames (Eames Chair), Jonathan Ive.
- ⭐ **Double Diamond** = Two diamonds; Diamond 1 (Discover + Define), Diamond 2 (Develop + Deliver). Divergent and convergent thinking.
- ⭐ **Weighted Selection Matrix Formula:** $S_{total}^{(j)} = \sum w_i \cdot s_{i,j}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ⭐ **Iteration is not failure** — it is learning. The Dyson vacuum took 5,127 prototypes to succeed.
- ⭐ **Difference between Engineering Design, Industrial Design, and Product Design** is a high-probability KTU Part A question.
- ⭐ **CMF** = Colour, Material, Finish. Always specify all three in any design description.
- ⭐ **Designers translate** between User, Engineering, Marketing, and Manufacturing — remember the **"generalist specialist"** phrase.
- ⭐ **DFM = Design for Manufacturing; DfE = Design for Environment/End-of-life** — know both.
- ⭐ **Persona, Empathy Map, User Journey Map** are *Empathize-stage* deliverables.
- ⭐ **PDS (Product Design Specification)** is a *Define-stage* deliverable with quantitative targets.
- ⭐ **Mood board, Storyboard, Concept sketches** are *Ideate-stage* deliverables.
- ⭐ **Foam model, 3D print, Mock-up** are *Prototype-stage* deliverables.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Industrial Design: Process and Need**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Course PEMET837 — Product Design and Innovation.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robust Design: Taguchi Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robust Design: Taguchi Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robust Design** is a structured, statistically-driven engineering methodology developed by **Dr. Genichi Taguchi** (a Japanese quality engineer) that seeks to make products and processes *insensitive* (robust) to sources of variation commonly called **noise factors**. The core objective is to achieve a target performance with minimum variance, by optimally setting the **design parameters (control factors)** rather than by tightening tolerances or increasing manufacturing cost.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Robust Design is positioned as a **Quality-by-Design (QbD)** technique under the broader umbrella of Design for Six Sigma (DFSS) and Design for X (DFX). It bridges the **concept design stage** with the **detailed design stage** in the product development lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Central Philosophy:** *"A product or process should be designed so that it performs its intended function under varied conditions of use, wear, and abuse, without harm or excessive variation."*

### 1.2 Classification of Design Factors in the Taguchi System

Taguchi classifies all design parameters into three mutually exclusive categories:

| **Factor Class** | **Symbol** | **Nature** | **Example (Automotive Brake Pad)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control Factors | **X** | Designer can specify and hold | Friction material composition, pad thickness |
| Noise Factors | **Z** | Uncontrollable in real use | Ambient temperature, humidity, road dust, driver style |
| Signal Factors | **M** | Input desired by the user | Brake pedal force (the input we control) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The All-Terrain Shoe"

Imagine a sports shoe engineered to be comfortable.

- **Control factors** = the cushion material, sole thickness, arch geometry (the designer can pick them).
- **Noise factors** = the weight of the user, the temperature of the floor, walking speed, surface texture, hours of use, rain.
- **Signal factor** = the foot-strike force.
- **Quality characteristic (Y)** = the cushion comfort score.

A **non-robust** shoe feels great in a cool lab but becomes flat and uncomfortable when a heavy person walks fast on a hot, wet surface. A **robust** shoe, designed using Taguchi's method, feels comfortable for *all* users on *all* terrains because the control factors were optimized to suppress sensitivity to noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Robustness is achieved by **engineering the mean to the target AND minimizing the variance around that mean** — not by merely hitting the target once. This is a fundamental shift from classical acceptance sampling.

### 1.4 Quality Characteristics (The Three Categories)

Taguchi classifies all quality characteristics **Y** into one of three performance objectives:

1. **Nominal-is-Best (NB)** — Target value is desirable; deviation in either direction is bad. *Example: A 10kΩ resistor (not 9.9 kΩ and not 10.1 kΩ).*
2. **Smaller-is-Better (SB)** — Smaller magnitude is always better. *Example: Brake squeal noise, surface roughness, emissions.*
3. **Larger-is-Better (LB)** — Larger magnitude is always better. *Example: Battery life, tensile strength, fuel efficiency.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taguchi's Loss Function — The Theoretical Foundation

Classical quality theory says: *"If a product is within specification, there is no loss."* Taguchi refuted this. He stated that **any deviation from the target value causes a loss to society**, and that loss increases quadratically with deviation.

#### The Quadratic Loss Function:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^{2}$$

Where:
- $L(y)$ = Loss incurred by the consumer/society when the quality characteristic takes value $y$.
- $m$ = Target value (the ideal, nominal function value).
- $y$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic.
- $k$ = Proportionality constant (a monetary coefficient, derived from replacement/repair costs).

The constant $k$ is calculated using the specification limits. If $\Delta_0$ is the allowable deviation from target $m$ at which total loss equals a known cost $A_0$:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}$$

The expected average loss across a population of products becomes:

$$E[L] = k \left[ (\mu - m)^{2} + \sigma^{2} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a KTU-favorite line of reasoning: the expected loss is the sum of (a) squared bias from the target and (b) the variance. Robust design must attack **both** terms.

### 2.2 The Two-Stage Optimization (The P-Diagram Logic)

Taguchi decomposed robust design into a **two-stage optimization** problem:

- **Stage 1 (Locate):** Adjust control factors to bring the mean $\mu_Y$ of the quality characteristic to the target $m$.
- **Stage 2 (Minimize Spread):** Once the mean is on target, adjust control factors to minimize the variance $\sigma_Y^{2}$ caused by noise factors.

This logic is best understood through the **P-Diagram (Parameter Diagram)**, which is mandated in many KTU Module 2 question papers.

### 2.3 The Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio

The **S/N Ratio** is Taguchi's single-number performance metric. It is NOT the engineering S/N ratio of communication theory; rather, it is a logarithmic transform of the mean-squared-deviation that simultaneously reflects both location and dispersion.

The general form is:

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{Mean Square of Desired Effect}}{\text{Mean Square of Undesired Effect}}\right)$$

The units are **decibels (dB)**. A **higher** S/N ratio is always better.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — The Master Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Quadratic Loss | $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ | Monetary units |
| Loss Coefficient | $k = A_0 \big/ \Delta_0^{2}$ | Monetary per unit squared |
| Expected Loss | $E[L] = k[(\mu - m)^{2} + \sigma^{2}]$ | Average over population |
| S/N Ratio (NB) | $\eta = 10 \log_{10} \big( \bar{y}^{2} \big/ s^{2} \big)$ | Nominal-is-Best |
| S/N Ratio (SB) | $\eta = -10 \log_{10} \big( \tfrac{1}{n} \sum y_i^{2} \big)$ | Smaller-is-Better |
| S/N Ratio (LB) | $\eta = -10 \log_{10} \big( \tfrac{1}{n} \sum (1/y_i^{2}) \big)$ | Larger-is-Better |
| Sum of Squares Total | $SS_{T} = \sum y_i^{2} - (\sum y_i)^{2}/n$ | Sum of Squares Total |
| Sum of Squares Mean | $SS_{m} = (\sum y_i)^{2}/n$ | Used in ANOVA |
| Sum of Squares Error | $SS_{e} = SS_{T} - SS_{m}$ | Error term |
| Degrees of Freedom | $d.o.f. = n - 1$ | For S/N analysis |
| ANOVA F-ratio | $F = MS_{\text{factor}} \big/ MS_{e}$ | Test significance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** In the formula table above, the absolute-value bars have been replaced with parenthesization and `\big/` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. In your own KTU answer scripts, you may write the formulas naturally on a fresh line.

### 2.5 Orthogonal Arrays (OA) — The Experimental Skeleton

A traditional full factorial experiment with $k$ factors at 3 levels each would require $3^{k}$ runs. For $k = 4$, that is 81 runs. Taguchi's **Orthogonal Arrays** drastically reduce this through combinatorial symmetry.

**Notation:** $L_{a}(b^{c})$
- $a$ = number of experimental runs (rows)
- $b$ = number of levels
- $c$ = number of columns (factors + interactions that can be studied)

**Common Arrays Used in KTU Problems:**

- $L_{4}(2^{3})$ — 4 runs, 2 levels, 3 factors
- $L_{8}(2^{7})$ — 8 runs, 2 levels, up to 7 factors
- $L_{9}(3^{4})$ — 9 runs, 3 levels, 4 factors
- $L_{16}(4^{5})$ — 16 runs, 4 levels, 5 factors
- $L_{18}(2^{1} 3^{7})$ — 18 runs, mixed levels

### 2.6 The Taguchi Robust Design Workflow (4 Phases)

1. **System Design** — Conceptual engineering (inventor's phase).
2. **Parameter Design** — Selection of control factor levels using OA + S/N ratio analysis.
3. **Tolerance Design** — Tightening tolerances *only* on those control factors that contribute most to variance.
4. **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
   - **Automotive:** Tuning suspension settings for variable road conditions.
   - **Electronics:** Designing circuits tolerant to temperature and voltage drift.
   - **Manufacturing:** Process parameter optimization for CNC machining.
   - **Biomedical:** Drug formulation robust to storage humidity.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Loss Function Constant $k$

**Given:**
- The customer tolerates a deviation of $\Delta_0$ from the target value $m$ (this is the specification limit).
- At deviation $\Delta_0$, the customer-side loss is known to be a monetary amount $A_0$ (e.g., cost of warranty replacement).

**Step 1:** Start with the generic quadratic form proposed by Taguchi:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the boundary condition $y - m = \Delta_0$ and $L(y) = A_0$:

$$A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the proportionality constant $k$:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}}$$

**Step 4:** Now consider the population mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The expected loss over $n$ products is:

$$E[L] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k(y_i - m)^{2} = k \cdot E[(y - m)^{2}]$$

**Step 5:** Expand the squared term:

$$E[(y - m)^{2}] = E[(\mu - m + y - \mu)^{2}] = (\mu - m)^{2} + 2(\mu - m)E[y - \mu] + E[(y - \mu)^{2}]$$

**Step 6:** The middle term vanishes because $E[y - \mu] = 0$. Therefore:

$$E[(y - m)^{2}] = (\mu - m)^{2} + \sigma^{2}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting back into the expected loss expression:

$$\boxed{E[L] = k \left[(\mu - m)^{2} + \sigma^{2}\right]}$$

This is the foundational equation that drives the **two-stage optimization**. The first squared term is minimized in **Stage 1** (locate the mean on target), and the second variance term is minimized in **Stage 2** (minimize spread).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the S/N Ratio for the "Smaller-is-Better" Case

**Premise:** For characteristics like wear, emission, noise — smaller values are always better. The ideal is $m = 0$.

**Step 1:** Define the Mean Squared Deviation (MSD) as the average of $y^{2}$ values across the $n$ repeated measurements in each trial:

$$MSD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2}$$

**Step 2:** The S/N ratio is defined as the ratio of signal (desirable — i.e., unity) to noise (undesirable — i.e., MSD), expressed in decibels:

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{MSD} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression for MSD:

$$\boxed{\eta_{SB} = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2} \right)}$$

The negative sign convention is adopted so that the S/N ratio is **maximized** when the output is minimized.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the S/N Ratio for the "Larger-is-Better" Case

**Premise:** For characteristics like strength, life — larger values are always better. We want $1/y$ to be small.

**Step 1:** Convert each observation $y_i$ to its reciprocal to transform the problem into a smaller-is-better form:

$$y_i' = \frac{1}{y_i^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the smaller-is-better S/N formula to the reciprocals:

$$\eta_{LB} = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i' \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\eta_{LB} = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{y_i^{2}} \right)}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the S/N Ratio for the "Nominal-is-Best" Case

**Premise:** A target value $m$ exists. Any deviation in either direction is bad.

**Step 1:** Taguchi uses the square of the sample mean over the sample variance as the "signal-to-noise" surrogate, because the sample mean measures the *bias* (squared) from zero, while the sample variance measures the *spread*:

$$\eta_{NB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\bar{y}^{2}}{s^{2}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** With $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_i$ and $s^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{NB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\bar{y}^{2}}{s^{2}} \right)}$$

This metric is maximized when $\bar{y}$ is large *relative* to $s$. For nominal characteristics where the mean should equal the target, we typically first shift the data so that the mean is near the target value.

---

### 3.5 Fully Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard 7-Mark Style

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC milling process is being tuned to minimize surface roughness $R_a$ (in micrometres) on an aluminum component. Three control factors are studied at two levels each using an $L_{4}$ orthogonal array. Two replicates are taken. The data table is as follows:

| **Run** | **A (Feed)** | **B (Speed)** | **C (Depth)** | $y_1$ | $y_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1.20 | 1.30 |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 0.80 | 0.90 |
| 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 1.10 | 1.00 |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 0.70 | 0.60 |

Find the optimal factor combination and predict the S/N ratio at optimum.

**Step 1 — Compute Mean Squared Deviation for each run (Smaller-is-Better):**

For Run 1: $MSD_1 = \frac{1.20^2 + 1.30^2}{2} = \frac{1.44 + 1.69}{2} = 1.565$

For Run 2: $MSD_2 = \frac{0.80^2 + 0.90^2}{2} = \frac{0.64 + 0.81}{2} = 0.725$

For Run 3: $MSD_3 = \frac{1.10^2 + 1.00^2}{2} = \frac{1.21 + 1.00}{2} = 1.105$

For Run 4: $MSD_4 = \frac{0.70^2 + 0.60^2}{2} = \frac{0.49 + 0.36}{2} = 0.425$

**Step 2 — Compute S/N ratio for each run:**

$\eta_1 = -10 \log_{10}(1.565) = -10 (0.1947) = -1.947 \, dB$

$\eta_2 = -10 \log_{10}(0.725) = -10 (-0.1397) = +1.397 \, dB$

$\eta_3 = -10 \log_{10}(1.105) = -10 (0.0434) = -0.434 \, dB$

$\eta_4 = -10 \log_{10}(0.425) = -10 (-0.3716) = +3.716 \, dB$

**Step 3 — Build the S/N Response Table (Factor Average):**

| **Factor** | **Level 1 Average** | **Level 2 Average** | **Delta (Max-Min)** | **Rank** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Feed) | $(-1.947 + 1.397)/2 = -0.275$ | $(-0.434 + 3.716)/2 = 1.641$ | 1.916 | **2** |
| B (Speed) | $(-1.947 - 0.434)/2 = -1.190$ | $(1.397 + 3.716)/2 = 2.557$ | 3.747 | **1** |
| C (Depth) | $(-1.947 + 3.716)/2 = 0.885$ | $(1.397 - 0.434)/2 = 0.482$ | 0.403 | 3 |

**Step 4 — Determine the Optimum:**

The optimum is at the level giving the **highest** S/N ratio for each factor.
- A → Level 2 (gives $+1.641$ dB)
- B → Level 2 (gives $+2.557$ dB)
- C → Level 1 (gives $+0.885$ dB)

**Optimal Combination: A2 B2 C1**

**Step 5 — Predict S/N at Optimum (additive model, Taguchi approximation):**

$$\eta_{opt} \approx \bar{\eta} + (\bar{A}_2 - \bar{\eta}) + (\bar{B}_2 - \bar{\eta}) + (\bar{C}_1 - \bar{\eta})$$

The grand mean:

$$\bar{\eta} = \frac{-1.947 + 1.397 - 0.434 + 3.716}{4} = \frac{2.732}{4} = 0.683 \, dB$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{opt} \approx 0.683 + (1.641 - 0.683) + (2.557 - 0.683) + (0.885 - 0.683)$$

$$\eta_{opt} \approx 0.683 + 0.958 + 1.874 + 0.202 = 3.717 \, dB$$

**Step 6 — Confirmation Run:**

The optimum $A_2 B_2 C_1$ corresponds to Run 4, whose S/N ratio is $3.716$ dB. The prediction matches the actual experiment — confirming the additive model and validating the optimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A predicted $\eta_{opt} = 3.717$ dB corresponds to an MSD of $10^{-3.717/10} = 0.426$, meaning the expected surface roughness $R_a \approx \sqrt{0.426} \approx 0.65 \, \mu m$. This is a **substantial reduction** from the baseline (Run 1 had $R_a \approx 1.25 \, \mu m$).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — The Decision Logic

| **If Quality Characteristic Is** | **Use This S/N Formula** | **Objective** | **Optimum Is At** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal-is-Best | $\eta = 10 \log(\bar{y}^{2}/s^{2})$ | Hit target, low spread | Max S/N |
| Smaller-is-Better | $\eta = -10 \log(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^{2})$ | Minimize value | Max S/N |
| Larger-is-Better | $\eta = -10 \log(\frac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_i^{2})$ | Maximize value | Max S/N |
| Targeted-as-Best | $\eta = 10 \log[\mu^{2}/(\sigma^{2} + (\mu - m)^{2})]$ | Track target $m$ | Max S/N |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Taguchi Robust Design Methodology (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Phase 1: System Design]:::phase1
    nodeB[Phase 2: Parameter Design]:::phase2
    nodeC[Phase 3: Tolerance Design]:::phase3
    nodeD[Phase 4: Production Readiness]:::phase4
    
    nodeE[Define Customer Requirements and Quality Loss Function]:::step
    nodeF[Identify Control Factors Noise Factors and Signal Factor]:::step
    nodeG[Select Orthogonal Array for Fractional Factorial Runs]:::step
    nodeH[Conduct Experiments with Noise Variation]:::step
    nodeI[Compute S/N Ratio for Each Run]:::step
    nodeJ[ANOVA and Factor Ranking on S/N Response]:::step
    nodeK[Predict Optimum Levels A2 B2 C1 etc]:::step
    nodeL[Run Confirmation Experiment]:::step
    nodeM[Tighten Tolerances on Critical Factors Only]:::step
    nodeN[Lock Design and Release to Manufacturing]:::step
    
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeN
    
    classDef phase1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef phase2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef phase3 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    classDef step fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242,color:#000
```

### 4.2 The P-Diagram (Parameter Diagram) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pInput[INPUT Signal Factor M]:::signal
    pOutput[OUTPUT Response Y Target Performance]:::output
    pControl[CONTROL FACTORS X1 X2 X3 Designer Choice]:::control
    pNoise[NOISE FACTORS Z1 Z2 Z3 Z4 Uncontrollable]:::noise
    pError[ERROR FACTORS Delta from Ideal]:::error
    pSystem[ENGINEERED SYSTEM PRODUCT OR PROCESS]:::system
    
    pInput --> pSystem
    pControl --> pSystem
    pNoise --> pSystem
    pError --> pSystem
    pSystem --> pOutput
    
    classDef signal fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    classDef error fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000
```

### 4.3 The Two-Stage Optimization — Stage Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sA[START with Preliminary Factor Levels]:::stage
    sB{Stage 1 Is Mean on Target m?}:::decision
    sC[Adjust INTERCEPT Factors]:::action
    sD[Fix Intercept at Best Level]:::action
    sE{Stage 2 Is Variance Minimized}:::decision
    sF[Adjust SLOPE Factors to Reduce Spread]:::action
    sG[Optimum Robust Setting Identified]:::final
    sH[Run Confirmation Test]:::final
    
    sA --> sB
    sB -- NO --> sC
    sC --> sB
    sB -- YES --> sD
    sD --> sE
    sE -- NO --> sF
    sF --> sE
    sE -- YES --> sG
    sG --> sH
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Robust Design. Explain its significance in the product development process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Robust Design is an engineering methodology introduced by Dr. Genichi Taguchi that aims to make a product or process *insensitive* to variations caused by uncontrollable **noise factors**, by properly choosing the levels of **control factors** during the design phase itself.

**Significance in product development:**
1. Reduces field failures and warranty costs by addressing variability at the *design* stage rather than the inspection stage.
2. Lowers manufacturing cost by enabling wider tolerances on components without sacrificing quality.
3. Increases customer satisfaction since the product performs consistently across a wide range of operating conditions and user profiles.
4. Aligns with **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** and supports the **KTU 2024 Course Outcome** of integrating statistical quality tools into conceptual design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between control factors, noise factors, and signal factors with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Factor Type** | **Definition** | **Example (Washing Machine Spin Dryer)** |
|---|---|---|
| Control Factor (X) | Design parameter whose level the engineer can set and hold. | Drum radius, motor RPM range, suspension spring stiffness. |
| Noise Factor (Z) | Uncontrollable parameter that causes variation in output during real use. | Load weight, imbalance, ambient temperature, water absorption in clothes. |
| Signal Factor (M) | The user-desired input that the system must respond to faithfully. | The selected spin cycle button pressed by the user. |

The robust design objective is to select X levels such that Y (vibration level, drying efficiency) is stable against variation in Z while remaining responsive to M.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the Taguchi Loss Function in detail. A critical dimension in an automotive component has a target value of $50.00$ mm. The customer's tolerance limit is $\pm 0.50$ mm, at which the warranty replacement cost is **₹4,000**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grinding process is being studied to improve surface finish. Three control factors (Wheel Grade A, Coolant Flow B, Work Speed C) are studied at two levels each using an $L_4$ orthogonal array. The smaller-is-better S/N ratios obtained for the four runs are:

| Run | A | B | C | S/N (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | -2.5 |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 2 | +1.8 |
| 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | -0.6 |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 1 | +3.4 |

Determine the optimal factor combination and predict the S/N ratio at optimum. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1:** The Taguchi quadratic loss function is:

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $k$ from boundary conditions.
- Target: $m = 50.00$ mm
- Tolerance: $\Delta_0 = 0.50$ mm
- Loss at tolerance: $A_0 = \text{₹}4{,}000$

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}} = \frac{4000}{(0.50)^{2}} = \frac{4000}{0.25} = \text{₹}16{,}000 \text{ per mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute expected loss for two scenarios.

*Scenario 1 (Poor design):* $\mu = 50.20$ mm, $\sigma = 0.10$ mm

$$E[L] = 16000 \left[(50.20 - 50.00)^{2} + (0.10)^{2}\right] = 16000 \left[0.04 + 0.01\right] = \text{₹}800$$

*Scenario 2 (Robust design):* $\mu = 50.00$ mm, $\sigma = 0.05$ mm

$$E[L] = 16000 \left[(0.00)^{2} + (0.05)^{2}\right] = 16000 \times 0.0025 = \text{₹}40$$

**Step 4:** Engineering interpretation — robust design reduces average loss by a factor of **20** while keeping the mean on target.

$$\boxed{k = \text{₹}16{,}000 \text{ per mm}^{2}, \quad E[L]_{\text{poor}} = \text{₹}800, \quad E[L]_{\text{robust}} = \text{₹}40}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Build the S/N response table.

| **Factor** | **Level 1 Avg** | **Level 2 Avg** | **Delta** | **Rank** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Wheel Grade) | $(-2.5 + 1.8)/2 = -0.35$ | $(-0.6 + 3.4)/2 = 1.40$ | 1.75 | 2 |
| B (Coolant Flow) | $(-2.5 - 0.6)/2 = -1.55$ | $(1.8 + 3.4)/2 = 2.60$ | 4.15 | 1 |
| C (Work Speed) | $(-2.5 + 3.4)/2 = 0.45$ | $(1.8 - 0.6)/2 = 0.60$ | 0.15 | 3 |

**Step 2:** Select the level with the maximum S/N ratio for each factor.
- A → Level 2 (gain $+1.40$ dB)
- B → Level 2 (gain $+2.60$ dB)
- C → Level 2 (gain $+0.60$ dB)

**Optimal Combination: A2 B2 C2**

**Step 3:** Compute the grand mean:

$$\bar{\eta} = \frac{-2.5 + 1.8 - 0.6 + 3.4}{4} = \frac{2.1}{4} = 0.525 \, dB$$

**Step 4:** Predict optimum S/N (additive model):

$$\eta_{opt} \approx 0.525 + (1.40 - 0.525) + (2.60 - 0.525) + (0.60 - 0.525)$$

$$\eta_{opt} = 0.525 + 0.875 + 2.075 + 0.075 = 3.55 \, dB$$

**Step 5:** Ranking discussion.
- Factor **B (Coolant Flow)** is the most dominant control factor (rank 1).
- Factor **A (Wheel Grade)** is the second-most important.
- Factor **C (Work Speed)** has a small delta (0.15) and may be assigned to its least-costly level in tolerance design phase.

$$\boxed{\text{Optimum: } A_2 B_2 C_2, \quad \eta_{opt} \approx 3.55 \, dB}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio concept in Taguchi's Robust Design. Derive the S/N ratio expressions for **(i)** Smaller-is-Better and **(ii)** Larger-is-Better quality characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **P-Diagram (Parameter Diagram)**. Explain how it is used to identify and classify design factors in a robust design study for a DC motor speed controller. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Concept:**
The S/N ratio is a logarithmic performance metric that quantifies the *robustness* of a quality characteristic against noise. It is expressed in decibels (dB) and is **always maximized** for an optimum design.

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\text{Desired Effect Power}}{\text{Undesired Effect Power}} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Smaller-is-Better Derivation:**
For characteristics like emissions, surface roughness, and wear, the target is zero. Define the Mean Squared Deviation:

$$MSD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2}$$

The S/N ratio is:

$$\eta_{SB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{MSD} \right) = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Larger-is-Better Derivation:**
For characteristics like battery life and strength, we transform via $y_i' = 1/y_i^{2}$ and apply the SB formula:

$$\eta_{LB} = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{y_i^{2}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Comparative Interpretation:**

| Type | Use When | Target | Transform Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| SB | Smaller value better | $m = 0$ | None |
| LB | Larger value better | $m = \infty$ | Reciprocal of squares |
| NB | Specific target | $m \neq 0$ | None; uses mean and variance |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — P-Diagram Concept:**
The Parameter Diagram is a 5-block visual tool that catalogs all input-output relationships, noise sources, and error states for the system under design. It was formalized by Taguchi to ensure all factor types are identified before experimentation.

**Step 2 — Application to DC Motor Speed Controller:**

| **P-Diagram Block** | **DC Motor Speed Controller Example** |
|---|---|
| **Input (Signal Factor M)** | The user-set speed reference voltage (e.g., 0–5 V analog input). |
| **Control Factors (X)** | PWM duty cycle range, current limit resistor, feedback gain $K_p$, controller IC part number. |
| **Noise Factors (Z)** | Battery voltage sag, ambient temperature, motor bearing wear, load torque variation, dust ingress. |
| **Error Factors ($\Delta$)** | Aging drift of components, manufacturing tolerance stack-up, solder joint resistance. |
| **Output (Response Y)** | Actual motor shaft RPM (target: linearly proportional to input M, with low ripple). |

**Step 3 — How the P-Diagram Drives the Experiment:**
1. Each block becomes a column or set of columns in the orthogonal array.
2. The Input and Output define the **ideal function** $Y = f(M)$.
3. Noise and Error factors are deliberately **perturbed** during experiments to measure robustness.
4. Control factors are varied across levels; their effect on S/N ratio is ranked.

**Step 4 — Engineering Outcome:**
By using the P-Diagram, the designer systematically identifies that PWM frequency and feedback gain $K_p$ are *intercept* factors (control the mean), while input capacitor ESR and motor inductance variation are *slope* factors (control the spread). The robust design seeks high $K_p$ and high PWM frequency to suppress noise effects.

$$\boxed{\text{P-Diagram is the primary scoping tool for KTU Module 2 design problems.}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Forgetting the negative sign in the SB and LB S/N formulas, leading to the *minimum* being chosen instead of the *maximum*. Always remember: $\eta$ is **maximized** in all three cases.
> 2. **Confusing P-Diagram with FMEA:** P-Diagram is a *factor identification* tool used in the design phase. FMEA is a *failure mode scoring* tool used in validation. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Mixing up factor types:** Treating noise factors as control factors (or vice versa) leads to completely wrong OA layouts. Always re-classify using the P-Diagram before assigning columns.
> 4. **Forgetting the additive model assumption:** The Taguchi prediction $\eta_{opt} = \bar{\eta} + \sum (\bar{X}_j^{\text{best}} - \bar{\eta})$ assumes factor interactions are negligible. Examiners award full marks only if this assumption is **explicitly stated**.
> 5. **Loss function coefficient mistakes:** Using $\Delta_0^{2}$ as $0.5$ (linear) instead of $0.25$ (squared) — the most common arithmetic slip.
> 6. **ANOVA omission:** Just computing S/N averages is not enough for 14-mark questions. Examiners expect a brief ANOVA on the S/N values to identify the *significant* factors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Taguchi's Robust Design** is a QbD technique that minimizes variance by *engineered choice* of control factors, not by tighter tolerances.
- The **Loss Function** $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ is the philosophical foundation: any deviation from target causes monetary loss to society.
- The **expected loss** has two components: $(\mu - m)^{2}$ (squared bias) and $\sigma^{2}$ (variance). Both must be minimized — this is the **two-stage optimization**.
- The **three quality characteristics** are: Nominal-is-Best (NB), Smaller-is-Better (SB), Larger-is-Better (LB). Each has a unique S/N formula.
- The **S/N ratio is in decibels (dB)** and is always **maximized** in Taguchi analysis.
- **Orthogonal Arrays** (e.g., $L_4, L_8, L_9, L_{16}, L_{18}$) drastically reduce the experimental runs from $3^{k}$ to as few as $4$ for four factors.
- The **P-Diagram** identifies the four parameter classes: Input Signal, Control Factors, Noise Factors, Error Factors, and Output Response.
- The **Taguchi workflow** is: System Design → Parameter Design → Tolerance Design → Production.
- **Confirmation runs** are mandatory to verify the predicted optimum against the additive model.
- The **additive prediction formula** is $\eta_{opt} = \bar{\eta} + \sum (\bar{X}_j^{best} - \bar{\eta})$, valid when interactions are negligible.
- **ANOVA on S/N data** identifies which control factors contribute significantly to robustness.
- Taguchi's philosophy integrates with **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)**, **Six Sigma**, and **Lean Product Development**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Experiments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Experiments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a systematic, statistical methodology for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled tests to evaluate the effects of multiple input variables (factors) on an output variable (response). It is formally defined as *"the process of planning an experiment so that appropriate data can be collected and analyzed by statistical methods, resulting in valid and objective conclusions."* — The foundational framework attributed to **Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1935)**.

In the context of **Product Design and Innovation (PEMET837)**, DOE serves as a critical engineering tool that allows designers to:
- Identify the **key factors** influencing product performance.
- Quantify the **main effects** and **interaction effects** between variables.
- Optimize product parameters with **minimum experimental runs**.
- Establish **robust design windows** insensitive to uncontrollable noise factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef's Kitchen" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine you are a chef perfecting a new cake recipe.** You suspect that baking temperature, baking time, and sugar content all affect the cake's taste score. Instead of randomly changing one thing at a time, you decide to run a structured experiment: bake 8 cakes where each cake uses a specific *combination* of high/low temperature, short/long time, and high/low sugar. You then taste-score each cake, and through statistical analysis, you find that *temperature × time* interaction is the critical factor. **This is DOE in action — structured, multivariate, and statistically validated experimentation.**

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Example (Product Design) |
|---|---|---|
| **Factor** | An independent input variable controlled by the experimenter | Pressure, Temperature, Speed |
| **Level** | A specific setting or value of a factor | Pressure = 2 bar, 4 bar |
| **Response** | The dependent output variable being measured | Product strength, surface finish |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels | (Pressure=2, Temp=150) |
| **Run** | A single experimental trial | One test piece produced |
| **Replication** | Repetition of an experiment to estimate variability | Making 3 samples per treatment |
| **Block** | A grouping of experimental units to control nuisance variables | Day-shift vs Night-shift batches |
| **Main Effect** | The average effect of one factor across levels of others | Effect of Pressure alone |
| **Interaction Effect** | The joint effect of two or more factors that is not additive | Pressure × Temperature synergy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** A well-designed DOE typically delivers a **5× to 10× reduction in test count** compared to the one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) approach, while simultaneously revealing *interactions* that OFAT cannot detect.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles of DOE (Fisher's Principles)

The entire edifice of modern DOE rests on **three pillars** introduced by **R.A. Fisher**:

### Principle 1: **Randomization**
- **What:** Every experimental run is assigned to a treatment in a purely random manner (using random number tables/computer generators).
- **Why:** Eliminates the influence of *uncontrolled nuisance variables* (e.g., humidity drift, machine warm-up) by spreading their effect equally across all treatments.
- **How:** In KTU exam answers, state: *"Randomization ensures that the error term is independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.), satisfying the foundational assumption of ANOVA."*

### Principle 2: **Replication**
- **What:** Each treatment combination is repeated multiple times ($n \geq 2$).
- **Why:** Allows estimation of **experimental error (σ²)**, which is the benchmark against which all factor effects are judged statistically significant.
- **How:** If total runs = $N$, treatments = $t$, then replications $r = N/t$.

### Principle 3: **Blocking**
- **What:** Grouping similar experimental units into *blocks* to isolate the variability due to a known nuisance factor.
- **Why:** Reduces the *within-block error variance*, increasing the **statistical power** of the experiment.
- **How:** Examples include blocking by day, batch, operator, or raw material lot.

## 2.2 Classification of Experimental Designs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** Students must be able to *draw* and *differentiate* between the following design families.

### A. **Classical / Inferential Designs (Fisher's Framework)**

| Design Type | Full Name | When to Use | Layout |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CRD** | Completely Randomized Design | Homogeneous experimental units, single factor | $t$ treatments, $r$ replications, fully randomized |
| **RBD** | Randomized Block Design | One nuisance factor present | $t$ treatments × $b$ blocks |
| **LSD** | Latin Square Design | Two nuisance factors (rows × columns) | $p \times p$ square with $p$ treatments |
| **GSD** | Graeco-Latin Square | Three nuisance factors | $p^2$ runs, $p$ treatments, 3 blocking variables |
| **BIBD** | Balanced Incomplete Block Design | When full blocks are impractical | Incomplete replication |

### B. **Factorial Designs (The Innovation Engine)**

| Design | Structure | Total Runs | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2^k Factorial** | 2 levels, k factors | $2^k$ | Detects all main effects & interactions |
| **3^k Factorial** | 3 levels, k factors | $3^k$ | Captures curvature (quadratic effects) |
| **Fractional Factorial ($2^{k-p}$)** | Subset of full factorial | $2^{k-p}$ | Screening when k ≥ 5 |
| **Taguchi Orthogonal Array** | Special fractional factorial | $L_n$ array | Robust design with OA tables |

### C. **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**
- **Central Composite Design (CCD)** — used to fit a second-order (quadratic) model.
- **Box-Behnken Design** — efficient 3-level design avoiding extreme corners.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Quick-Reference Table — Master these equations.**

| Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Degrees of Freedom (Total)** | $DF_{Total} = N - 1$ | $N$ = total runs |
| **DF (Treatments)** | $DF_{Trt} = t - 1$ | $t$ = number of treatments |
| **DF (Error)** | $DF_E = N - t$ | — |
| **DF (Blocks)** | $DF_{Blk} = b - 1$ | $b$ = number of blocks |
| **Correction Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{(\sum x_{ij})^2}{N} = \dfrac{G^2}{N}$ | $G$ = grand total |
| **Total SS** | $SS_{Total} = \sum x_{ij}^2 - CF$ | — |
| **SS (Treatments)** | $SS_{Trt} = \dfrac{\sum T_i^2}{r} - CF$ | $T_i$ = treatment total |
| **SS (Blocks)** | $SS_{Blk} = \dfrac{\sum B_j^2}{t} - CF$ | $B_j$ = block total |
| **SS (Error)** | $SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Trt} - SS_{Blk}$ | — |
| **Mean Square** | $MS = \dfrac{SS}{DF}$ | — |
| **F-Statistic** | $F = \dfrac{MS_{Trt}}{MS_E}$ | Compared with $F_{\alpha, t-1, N-t}$ |
| **Total Runs (Factorial)** | $N = 2^k$ | $k$ = number of factors |
| **Effect of Factor A** | $E_A = \bar{y}_{A^+} - \bar{y}_{A^-}$ | Contrast/2r |
| **Sum of Squares (Effect)** | $SS_{Effect} = \dfrac{(Contrast)^2}{N}$ | — |
| **Replication** | $r = \dfrac{N}{t}$ | $N$ = total, $t$ = treatments |
| **Coefficient of Determination** | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_E}{SS_{Total}}$ | Closer to 1 = better fit |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Map (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Automotive:** Optimizing engine parameters (fuel injection timing × compression ratio).
> - **Electronics:** Optimizing PCB soldering reflow profiles (peak temp × dwell time).
> - **Biomedical:** Formulation of drug tablets (binder concentration × compression force).
> - **Manufacturing:** Reducing defect rates in injection molding (pressure × cooling time).
> - **Materials:** Tensile strength of composites (fiber volume fraction × curing temp).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: ANOVA for One-Way Classification (CRD)

### Mathematical Foundation

Consider $t$ treatments each replicated $r$ times, giving $N = tr$ observations. Let $x_{ij}$ denote the observation in the $i$-th treatment and $j$-th replication.

**The Linear Additive Model:**

$$x_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean
- $\tau_i$ = effect of the $i$-th treatment
- $\epsilon_{ij}$ = random error, with $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

### Derivation of Sum of Squares Identity

Starting from the deviation of each observation from the grand mean:

$$x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{..} = (\bar{x}_{i.} - \bar{x}_{..}) + (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{i.})$$

Squaring both sides and summing over all $i, j$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{t} \sum_{j=1}^{r} (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{..})^2 = r\sum_{i=1}^{t} (\bar{x}_{i.} - \bar{x}_{..})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \sum_{j=1}^{r} (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{i.})^2$$

**The ANOVA Identity (Fundamental Partition):**

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatments} + SS_{Error}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern Problem)

> **Problem:** A product designer tests **4 different surface treatments** on metal parts, with **3 replications** each. The hardness (HRC) values are recorded. Conduct ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Hypotheses:**
- $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \mu_4$ (all treatments have equal mean hardness)
- $H_1:$ At least one treatment mean differs

**Step 1: Tabulate the data and compute treatment totals.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 45 + 47 + 44 = 136 \\
T_2 &= 49 + 51 + 50 = 150 \\
T_3 &= 42 + 44 + 43 = 129 \\
T_4 &= 48 + 46 + 47 = 141 \\
G &= 136 + 150 + 129 + 141 = 556 \\
N &= 4 \times 3 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Correction Factor (CF).**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(556)^2}{12} = \frac{309136}{12} = 25761.33$$

**Step 3: Compute $SS_{Total}$.**

First, $\sum x_{ij}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_{ij}^2 = &(45^2 + 47^2 + 44^2) + (49^2 + 51^2 + 50^2) \\
&+ (42^2 + 44^2 + 43^2) + (48^2 + 46^2 + 47^2) \\
&= 6050 + 7502 + 5489 + 6629 = 25670
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
45^2 + 47^2 + 44^2 &= 2025 + 2209 + 1936 = 6170 \\
49^2 + 51^2 + 50^2 &= 2401 + 2601 + 2500 = 7502 \\
42^2 + 44^2 + 43^2 &= 1764 + 1936 + 1849 = 5549 \\
48^2 + 46^2 + 47^2 &= 2304 + 2116 + 2209 = 6629 \\
\text{Sum} &= 6170 + 7502 + 5549 + 6629 = 25850
\end{aligned}
$$

$$SS_{Total} = 25850 - 25761.33 = 88.67$$

**Step 4: Compute $SS_{Treatments}$.**

$$SS_{Trt} = \frac{\sum T_i^2}{r} - CF = \frac{136^2 + 150^2 + 129^2 + 141^2}{3} - 25761.33$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
136^2 &= 18496 \\
150^2 &= 22500 \\
129^2 &= 16641 \\
141^2 &= 19881 \\
\text{Sum} &= 77518 \\
\frac{77518}{3} &= 25839.33 \\
SS_{Trt} &= 25839.33 - 25761.33 = 78.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $SS_{Error}$.**

$$SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Trt} = 88.67 - 78.00 = 10.67$$

**Step 6: Construct the ANOVA Table.**

| Source | SS | DF | MS | F-ratio | $F_{0.05, 3, 8}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Treatments** | 78.00 | 3 | 26.00 | **15.59** | 4.07 |
| **Error** | 10.67 | 8 | 1.33 | — | — |
| **Total** | 88.67 | 11 | — | — | — |

$$
F = \frac{MS_{Trt}}{MS_E} = \frac{26.00}{1.33} = 15.59
$$

**Step 7: Decision.**

Since $F_{calculated} = 15.59 > F_{critical} = 4.07$, **reject $H_0$**. The surface treatments have a statistically significant effect on hardness.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always explicitly state the critical F-value from the F-table corresponding to $(df_{Trt}, df_E)$ at the chosen significance level. A common error is writing only the calculated F.

## 3.2 Derivation: Main Effect in a $2^k$ Factorial Design

For a $2^2$ design with factors $A$ and $B$, the four runs give means:
- $(1)$: low A, low B
- $a$: high A, low B
- $b$: low A, high B
- $ab$: high A, high B

**Effect of A:**

$$E_A = \frac{ab + a - b - (1)}{2r}$$

The sign convention follows the **Yates algorithm** standard column of $(-1, +1, -1, +1)$ for factor $A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_A &= \frac{[ab + a - b - (1)]^2}{4r} = \frac{(\text{Contrast}_A)^2}{N} \\
SS_B &= \frac{[ab - a + b - (1)]^2}{4r} = \frac{(\text{Contrast}_B)^2}{N} \\
SS_{AB} &= \frac{[ab - a - b + (1)]^2}{4r} = \frac{(\text{Contrast}_{AB})^2}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **total degrees of freedom** for a $2^k$ design with $n$ replicates is $N - 1 = (2^k \cdot n) - 1$, distributed among the $2^k - 1$ effects plus error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The DOE Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition] --> B[Identify Response Variable Y]
    B --> C[List Potential Factors]
    C --> D[Screening Experiment]
    D --> E{Significant Factors?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Full Factorial or RSM]
    E -- No --> C
    F --> G[Conduct Experimental Runs]
    G --> H[Measure Response Data]
    H --> I[Compute ANOVA]
    I --> J{F-value Significant?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Identify Main and Interaction Effects]
    J -- No --> L[Increase Replication or Re-design]
    K --> M[Optimize via RSM / Desirability]
    M --> N[Confirmatory Runs]
    N --> O[Robust Optimal Settings]
    L --> G
```

## 4.2 Classical DOE Design Family — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Number of Factors?] --> Q1[1 Factor]
    P --> Q2[2-4 Factors]
    P --> Q3[5+ Factors]
    Q1 --> R1[CRD / RBD]
    Q2 --> R2[Full Factorial 2k]
    Q3 --> R3[Fractional Factorial or Taguchi OA]
    R1 --> S1[ANOVA + Mean Comparison]
    R2 --> S2[Factorial ANOVA with Interactions]
    R3 --> S3[Screening then Optimization]
```

## 4.3 Two-Factor Factorial Design Layout (2^2 Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Factor_Levels
        A1[A = Low -1]
        A2[A = High +1]
        B1[B = Low -1]
        B2[B = High +1]
    end
    A1 & B1 --> R1[Run 1: 1]
    A2 & B1 --> R2[Run 2: a]
    A1 & B2 --> R3[Run 3: b]
    A2 & B2 --> R4[Run 4: ab]
    R1 --> M1[Mean Y1]
    R2 --> M2[Mean Ya]
    R3 --> M3[Mean Yb]
    R4 --> M4[Mean Yab]
    M1 --> EFX[Compute Effects and Interactions]
    M2 --> EFX
    M3 --> EFX
    M4 --> EFX
```

## 4.4 Taguchi Philosophy — Quality Loss Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Nominal Target T] --> Q1[Deviation from Target]
    Q1 --> Q2[Quality Loss L = k times y minus T squared]
    Q2 --> Q3[Minimize Loss by Robust Design]
    Q3 --> Q4[Control Factors to Insensitize against Noise]
    Q4 --> SOL[Optimal Settings within Design Window]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `R1`, `M1`, `EFX`) to comply with Mermaid parser rules. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`**

**Q: Define Design of Experiments. List the three basic principles of DOE laid down by R.A. Fisher.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Design of Experiments (DOE) is a structured, statistical methodology for planning experiments such that valid data can be collected and analyzed using statistical techniques, particularly ANOVA, to draw objective conclusions about the effects of input factors on output responses.

> **Three Principles of Fisher:**
> 1. **Randomization** — Allocation of treatments to experimental units is done randomly to eliminate bias due to uncontrolled factors.
> 2. **Replication** — Each treatment is repeated multiple times to obtain an estimate of experimental error and improve precision.
> 3. **Blocking** — Grouping of homogeneous experimental units into blocks to control the variability due to known nuisance variables.

### Question 2 (CO2, Bloom Level: Understand)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`**

**Q: Distinguish between One-Factor-at-A-Time (OFAT) and Factorial Design. Why is OFAT considered inefficient?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | OFAT Approach | Factorial Design |
|---|---|---|
| Variables varied | One at a time | All factors simultaneously |
| Interactions detected | **Not possible** | **Detected and quantified** |
| Number of runs | Usually more | Significantly fewer |
| Validity of optimum | Local optimum | **Global optimum** |
| Statistical efficiency | Low | High (orthogonal structure) |

> **Why OFAT is inefficient:** OFAT fails to capture **interaction effects** between factors. The optimal setting found by OFAT is often a *local* optimum, not the *global* optimum. Factorial designs provide more information per experimental run and are statistically more rigorous.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [CHOICE OPTION A]
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`**

**Q: A product design team is investigating the effect of three welding parameters — Voltage (V), Wire Feed Rate (F), and Gas Flow Rate (G) — on the tensile strength (MPa) of a welded joint. They conduct a $2^3$ full factorial design with 2 replications. The 16 observations (in MPa) are tabulated below. Conduct the ANOVA and identify the significant factors at $\alpha = 0.05$. Also compute the main effects.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Construct the ANOVA table and test for significance of all effects.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the main effects of all three factors and their two-factor interactions. Comment on the most influential factor for product optimization.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Compute the **grand total (G)** and **correction factor (CF)**:

$$G = \sum_{i=1}^{16} x_i, \quad CF = \frac{G^2}{16}$$

Let the 16 observations (standardized example) yield:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Run 1 (1)} &= 32, \text{Run 2 (a)} = 45, \text{Run 3 (b)} = 38, \text{Run 4 (ab)} = 50, \\
\text{Run 5 (c)} &= 35, \text{Run 6 (ac)} = 48, \text{Run 7 (bc)} = 42, \text{Run 8 (abc)} = 55, \\
\text{Run 9 (1) rep} &= 34, \text{Run 10 (a) rep} = 47, \text{Run 11 (b) rep} = 40, \text{Run 12 (ab) rep} = 52, \\
\text{Run 13 (c) rep} &= 36, \text{Run 14 (ac) rep} = 49, \text{Run 15 (bc) rep} = 43, \text{Run 16 (abc) rep} = 56
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= (32+45+38+50+35+48+42+55) + (34+47+40+52+36+49+43+56) \\
&= 345 + 357 = 702 \\
N &= 16 \\
CF &= \frac{702^2}{16} = \frac{492804}{16} = 30800.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $SS_{Total}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i^2 &= 32^2 + 45^2 + 38^2 + 50^2 + 35^2 + 48^2 + 42^2 + 55^2 \\
&\quad + 34^2 + 47^2 + 40^2 + 52^2 + 36^2 + 49^2 + 43^2 + 56^2 \\
&= 1024 + 2025 + 1444 + 2500 + 1225 + 2304 + 1764 + 3025 \\
&\quad + 1156 + 2209 + 1600 + 2704 + 1296 + 2401 + 1849 + 3136 \\
&= 15309 + 16351 = 31660 \\
SS_{Total} &= 31660 - 30800.25 = 859.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **contrasts** using Yates sign columns. For a $2^3$ design, the standard sign columns are:

| Run | (1) | A | B | AB | C | AC | BC | ABC | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | +1 | +1 | −1 | 33 |
| 2 | +1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | +1 | 46 |
| 3 | +1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | +1 | 39 |
| 4 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | −1 | −1 | 51 |
| 5 | +1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | 35.5 |
| 6 | +1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | +1 | +1 | −1 | −1 | 48.5 |
| 7 | +1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | +1 | −1 | 42.5 |
| 8 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | +1 | 55.5 |

**Contrast for A:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_A = -33 + 46 - 39 + 51 - 35.5 + 48.5 - 42.5 + 55.5 = 51
$$

$$SS_A = \frac{(51)^2}{16} = \frac{2601}{16} = 162.56$$

**Contrast for B:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_B = -33 - 46 + 39 + 51 - 35.5 - 48.5 + 42.5 + 55.5 = 25
$$

$$SS_B = \frac{(25)^2}{16} = \frac{625}{16} = 39.06$$

**Contrast for AB:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_{AB} = 33 - 46 - 39 + 51 + 35.5 - 48.5 - 42.5 + 55.5 = -1
$$

$$SS_{AB} = \frac{(-1)^2}{16} = \frac{1}{16} = 0.0625$$

**Contrast for C:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_C = -33 - 46 - 39 - 51 + 35.5 + 48.5 + 42.5 + 55.5 = 13
$$

$$SS_C = \frac{(13)^2}{16} = \frac{169}{16} = 10.56$$

**Contrast for AC:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_{AC} = 33 - 46 + 39 - 51 - 35.5 + 48.5 - 42.5 + 55.5 = 1
$$

$$SS_{AC} = \frac{1^2}{16} = 0.0625$$

**Contrast for BC:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_{BC} = 33 + 46 - 39 - 51 - 35.5 - 48.5 + 42.5 + 55.5 = 3
$$

$$SS_{BC} = \frac{9}{16} = 0.5625$$

**Contrast for ABC:**

$$
\text{Contrast}_{ABC} = -33 + 46 + 39 - 51 + 35.5 - 48.5 - 42.5 + 55.5 = 1
$$

$$SS_{ABC} = \frac{1}{16} = 0.0625$$

**Step 4:** Compute $SS_{Error}$:

$$SS_{Error} = SS_{Total} - (SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_C + SS_{AC} + SS_{BC} + SS_{ABC})$$

$$SS_{Error} = 859.75 - (162.56 + 39.06 + 0.0625 + 10.56 + 0.0625 + 0.5625 + 0.0625) = 859.75 - 212.93 = 646.82$$

**Step 5:** Construct the ANOVA table.

| Source | SS | DF | MS | F-ratio | $F_{0.05, 1, 8}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A (Voltage)** | 162.56 | 1 | 162.56 | **2.01** | 5.32 |
| **B (Feed Rate)** | 39.06 | 1 | 39.06 | 0.48 | 5.32 |
| **AB** | 0.0625 | 1 | 0.0625 | 0.0008 | 5.32 |
| **C (Gas Flow)** | 10.56 | 1 | 10.56 | 0.13 | 5.32 |
| **AC** | 0.0625 | 1 | 0.0625 | 0.0008 | 5.32 |
| **BC** | 0.5625 | 1 | 0.5625 | 0.007 | 5.32 |
| **ABC** | 0.0625 | 1 | 0.0625 | 0.0008 | 5.32 |
| **Error** | 646.82 | 8 | 80.85 | — | — |
| **Total** | 859.75 | 15 | — | — | — |

$$
F_A = \frac{162.56}{80.85} = 2.01
$$

**Step 6:** **Decision**: Since $F_A = 2.01 < F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$, none of the effects are statistically significant at $\alpha = 0.05$. However, factor **A (Voltage)** has the largest F-ratio and is the *most influential* factor.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Main Effect of A (Voltage):**

$$E_A = \frac{\text{Contrast}_A}{2r \cdot 2^{k-1}} = \frac{51}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2^{3-1}} = \frac{51}{8} = 6.375 \text{ MPa}$$

**Main Effect of B (Feed Rate):**

$$E_B = \frac{25}{8} = 3.125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Main Effect of C (Gas Flow):**

$$E_C = \frac{13}{8} = 1.625 \text{ MPa}$$

**Two-Factor Interaction AB:**

$$E_{AB} = \frac{-1}{8} = -0.125 \text{ MPa (negligible)}$$

**AC:** $E_{AC} = 1/8 = 0.125$ MPa  
**BC:** $E_{BC} = 3/8 = 0.375$ MPa  
**ABC:** $E_{ABC} = 1/8 = 0.125$ MPa

**Conclusion:** Factor **A (Voltage)** is the most influential factor, with a main effect of **6.375 MPa**. None of the interactions are practically significant, confirming that the factors act **independently**. For product optimization, the designer should prioritize setting Voltage to its high level (+1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign errors in Yates columns** are the #1 reason for losing 3-4 marks. Always verify by checking: the *first column is always all +1*, and column signs follow the standard pattern.
> 2. Students often forget to **divide by N (not 4r)** when computing SS for a $2^k$ design with replications. Use $SS_{Effect} = (\text{Contrast})^2 / N$ where $N$ is the total number of runs.
> 3. Do not declare a factor "significant" based on F-ratio alone; **always compare with the critical F-value** from the table.
> 4. A common mistake is treating a *small main effect* as a *significant factor* because of large absolute value. The F-test is what determines significance, not the effect magnitude alone.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [INTERNAL CHOICE OPTION B]
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`**

**Q: Explain the Taguchi philosophy of robust design. Discuss the concept of the Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio with its three categories. An injection molding process is to be optimized using an $L_9$ orthogonal array. List the control factors and their levels, and explain the steps of experimentation.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain Taguchi's robust design philosophy and the three categories of S/N ratios with appropriate formulas.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design an $L_9$ orthogonal array for an injection molding process with 4 control factors (Mold Temperature, Injection Pressure, Holding Time, Cooling Time) each at 3 levels. Outline the experimental procedure.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Taguchi's Robust Design Philosophy:**

> **Genichi Taguchi's** central idea is that a product/process should be designed to be **insensitive to uncontrollable noise factors** (humidity, raw material variation, operator skill). This is achieved by selecting the levels of **control factors** such that the product performance has minimum variation around the target value.

> The **Quality Loss Function** quantifies the cost to society when a product deviates from its target:

$$L(y) = k(y - T)^2$$

Where $y$ is the actual quality characteristic, $T$ is the target value, and $k$ is a constant. Even small deviations from $T$ incur a loss proportional to the *square of deviation* — this is the philosophical foundation of Taguchi's approach.

**Three Categories of S/N Ratio:**

| Category | Use Case | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal-the-Best (NTB)** | Target value exists (e.g., dimensions) | $\dfrac{S}{N} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$ |
| **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | Defects, wear, emissions | $\dfrac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ |
| **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | Strength, yield, efficiency | $\dfrac{S}{N} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ |

> **Key insight:** A higher S/N ratio always indicates a *more robust* design. The objective is to **maximize the S/N ratio**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**$L_9$ Orthogonal Array Design for Injection Molding:**

**Step 1: Identify factors and levels.**

| Factor | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A: Mold Temp (°C)** | 60 | 80 | 100 |
| **B: Injection Pressure (MPa)** | 80 | 100 | 120 |
| **C: Holding Time (s)** | 5 | 10 | 15 |
| **D: Cooling Time (s)** | 15 | 20 | 25 |

**Step 2: Construct the $L_9$ array.**

| Run | A | B | C | D | S/N ratio (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | $\eta_1$ |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | $\eta_2$ |
| 3 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 3 | $\eta_3$ |
| 4 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | $\eta_4$ |
| 5 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 1 | $\eta_5$ |
| 6 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 2 | $\eta_6$ |
| 7 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 2 | $\eta_7$ |
| 8 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3 | $\eta_8$ |
| 9 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | $\eta_9$ |

**Step 3: Experimental Procedure (Taguchi's 8-step protocol).**

1. **Define the problem** and the quality characteristic (e.g., tensile strength — LTB).
2. **Identify the control factors, noise factors, and their levels.**
3. **Select the appropriate orthogonal array** ($L_9$ fits 4 factors at 3 levels).
4. **Conduct the experiments** in randomized order to avoid time-related bias.
5. **Measure the response** and compute the S/N ratio for each run.
6. **Compute the mean S/N ratio** for each factor at each level: $\bar{\eta}_{A1} = (\eta_1 + \eta_2 + \eta_3)/3$, etc.
7. **Identify optimal levels** — the level with the highest mean S/N for each factor is optimal.
8. **Conduct confirmatory runs** at the optimal setting and verify improvement.

**Step 4: ANOVA on S/N ratios** to identify the most influential factor and the percentage contribution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (for Taguchi):**
> 1. Confusing the **S/N ratio formula** for the three categories is the most common error. Memorize: *LTB uses $1/y^2$ in the sum; STB uses $y^2$ in the sum; NTB uses $\mu^2 / \sigma^2$*.
> 2. Do NOT confuse the **orthogonal array subscript** ($L_9$ = 9 runs) with the number of factors. $L_9$ handles up to 4 three-level factors.
> 3. Always state the **category of S/N ratio chosen** and **justify** why it suits your response (e.g., strength → LTB, roughness → STB, dimension → NTB).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Card — Read 3 times before exam.**

- ✅ **DOE = Statistics + Engineering Insight** — never apply DOE without a clear engineering hypothesis.
- ✅ **Three principles of Fisher:** Randomization, Replication, Blocking — *non-negotiable* in any valid DOE.
- ✅ **CRD** = simplest, requires homogeneous units. **RBD** = one nuisance factor. **LSD** = two nuisance factors. **GSD** = three nuisance factors.
- ✅ **ANOVA partition identity:** $SS_{Total} = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Error}$ (CRD) or $= SS_{Trt} + SS_{Blk} + SS_E$ (RBD).
- ✅ **F-test logic:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha,\, df_1,\, df_2}$. Always cite the critical value.
- ✅ **Factorial total runs:** $N = 2^k$ (full factorial) or $2^{k-p}$ (fractional). For $k = 3$ → $N = 8$ runs.
- ✅ **Main effect formula:** $E_A = \bar{y}_{A^+} - \bar{y}_{A^-}$ for any factorial design.
- ✅ **Yates algorithm** is the standard computational tool for $2^k$ designs — practice with sign columns.
- ✅ **Taguchi philosophy:** *Minimize variation around the target*, not just shift the mean.
- ✅ **S/N ratio categories:** LTB (strength), STB (defects), NTB (dimensions) — choose correctly or your optimization is wrong.
- ✅ **Confirmatory run** is mandatory after Taguchi optimization — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for this.
- ✅ **Resolution in fractional factorial:** Resolution III = main effects confounded with two-factor interactions (screening only). Resolution IV or V required for estimating interactions.
- ✅ **$R^2$ interpretation:** closer to 1 = model explains more variability. For product design, $R^2 \geq 0.80$ is acceptable.
- ✅ **Practical significance vs statistical significance:** A small effect can be statistically significant with large $n$; a large effect may not be significant with small $n$. Always interpret in engineering context.
- ✅ **Interaction plots** are mandatory visual outputs in $2^k$ analysis — *parallel lines = no interaction, non-parallel = interaction present*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Designing for Manufacturing and Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Designing for Manufacturing and Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFM/DFA)** is a systematic, integrated engineering methodology that optimizes the product design process by simultaneously considering manufacturing processes, assembly operations, and downstream lifecycle requirements during the earliest conceptual design phases. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, this approach is treated as a foundational pillar of **Design for X (DFX)** philosophy, where "X" represents manufacturing, assembly, environment, cost, quality, recycling, and disassembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET837 – Module 3):**
> *Design for Manufacturing and Assembly encompasses the principles, guidelines, and quantitative methodologies used to simplify product structure, reduce part count, lower production costs, and improve quality by embedding manufacturability and assemblability constraints into the product geometry, tolerances, and material selection decisions.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a LEGO® Model vs. Custom-Sculpted Clay**
> Imagine you are designing a toy car for mass production. You have two extreme choices:
>
> 1. **Sculpted Approach (Bad Design):** Carve the entire car from a single block of wood, hand-paint it, and fit a custom screw into a custom hole. The car looks beautiful, but it takes **40 minutes to build one**, requires **3 skilled workers**, and breaks easily.
> 2. **LEGO® Approach (Good DFM/DFA):** Use **standardized snap-fit bricks**, snap the body and wheels together in **5 seconds**, and use **standard colors and materials**.
>
> **DFM/DFA is the engineering science of turning the "Sculpted Approach" into the "LEGO® Approach" — without losing function, strength, or aesthetics.** The designer acts as the "instruction manual writer" for the factory.

## 1.3 Scope of DFM/DFA in KTU 2024 Scheme

| DFX Discipline | Focus | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **DFM** | Ease of Fabrication (Casting, Machining, Forming) | Reduces production cost |
| **DFA** | Ease of Assembly (Bolt count, Handling, Insertion) | Reduces assembly time |
| **DFC** | Cost Optimization | Lifetime cost reduction |
| **DFE** | Environment / Disposal | Sustainability (NEP 2020) |
| **DFS** | Service / Maintenance | Reliability engineering |

**Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU values to remember in bold):**

- **DFA Index (Efficiency, η):** Ratio of theoretically minimum parts to actual parts, expressed as a percentage. KTU target ≥ **70%**.
- **Assembly Time:** Best practice target is **< 30 seconds per part** for high-volume products.
- **Cost Reduction:** Industry standard DFM/DFA implementation yields **30%–60%** reduction in total product cost.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of DFM/DFA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Simplicity** – Minimize part count (Boothroyd's principle).
> 2. **Standardization** – Use stock materials, standard fasteners, common tolerances.
> 3. **Symmetry & Self-Alignment** – Design parts that can only be assembled the *correct* way (poka-yoke / mistake-proofing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Sequential Phases of DFM/DFA Implementation

DFM and DFA are **NOT applied simultaneously in random order**. KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 follows a strict **two-phase sequential methodology**:

### Phase 1 — Design for Assembly (DFA) First
Always perform **DFA before DFM**. The logic:
> If a part is unnecessary and can be eliminated, the manufacturing cost of that part (machining, casting, finishing) becomes **zero** — this is the *most effective* cost reduction.

### Phase 2 — Design for Manufacturing (DFM) Second
Apply DFM to the **remaining (essential) parts** to optimize their individual manufacturing processes.

## 2.2 Boothroyd's DFA Methodology (The Most Tested Concept)

**Geoffrey Boothroyd** (University of Massachusetts, founder of Boothroyd Dewhurst Inc.) developed the most widely accepted quantitative DFA method, which is a **high-weightage KTU question topic**.

### Step-by-Step Boothroyd DFA Logic:

1. **Generate the Assembly Diagram** – Draw the product and identify every component.
2. **Minimum Part Count Analysis** – For each part, ask the three "Minimum Part Criteria" questions:
   - **Q1:** Does the part move relative to other parts during operation? (Yes → Essential)
   - **Q2:** Must the part be of a different material than adjacent parts? (Yes → Essential)
   - **Q3:** Must the part be separable from adjacent parts for servicing/disassembly? (Yes → Essential)
3. **If answer to all three is NO → the part is a CANDIDATE for elimination** (combine with adjacent part).
4. **Calculate the DFA Efficiency Index.**
5. **Generate the Assembly Cost Worksheet** (Column-by-column time analysis).

## 2.3 Boothroyd DFM Methodology

For each retained part, select the **best manufacturing process** by comparing:
- Material wastage (Buy-to-Fly ratio)
- Tooling cost
- Cycle time
- Surface finish achievable
- Tolerance capability vs. required tolerance

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DFA Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100$ | **%** | Direct measure of design simplicity. Target: **η ≥ 70%** |
