# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 FMEA — Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Full Derivation

The **RPN** quantifies the relative risk of a failure mode. Let us derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1:** Identify a *failure mode* $F_i$ for a given component or process.

**Step 2:** Assign a **Severity score** $S$ on a scale of $1$ (negligible) to $10$ (catastrophic, safety hazard).

**Step 3:** Assign an **Occurrence score** $O$ on a scale of $1$ (very unlikely) to $10$ (very high, almost certain).

**Step 4:** Assign a **Detection score** $D$ on a scale of $1$ (very high detectability) to $10$ (impossible to detect before customer impact). Note: a *low* $D$ means *easy* detection.

**Step 5:** Compute the product of the three ordinal ratings:

$$
RPN_i = S_i \times O_i \times D_i
$$

**Step 6:** Rank all failure modes by descending $RPN$. The conventional **threshold** for action is $RPN \ge 100$, though modern practice recommends focusing on high $S$ alone (severity $\ge 9$).

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a **bracket** in a 3D printer assembly with the following failure modes:

| # | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bracket cracks under load | 8 | 4 | 3 | 96 |
| 2 | Threads strip on screw hole | 7 | 5 | 6 | 210 |
| 3 | Surface corrosion | 5 | 6 | 4 | 120 |
| 4 | Misaligned mounting hole | 6 | 3 | 5 | 90 |

The highest RPN is **210** for the *stripped thread* mode — design action is mandatory (e.g., add a metal insert).

### 3.1.3 Range and Resolution Analysis

The maximum theoretical RPN is $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$. Resolution: $RPN \in \mathbb{N}$, range $1$ to $1000$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write $D$ as *detectability* increasing with risk. The correct convention is that $D$ = *difficulty of detection* (higher = worse). Always read the question carefully.

---

## 3.2 QFD — House of Quality (HoQ) — Full Derivation

The **House of Quality** is a matrix that maps **Customer Requirements (CRs)** to **Engineering Characteristics (ECs)**.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1:** List *What's* (Customer Requirements) as rows, *How's* (Engineering Characteristics) as columns.

**Step 2:** Customer Importance Weights $w_i$ are assigned to each CR (1–5 scale).

**Step 3:** The **relationship matrix** $r_{ij}$ captures the strength of interaction:

- Strong = **9**
- Moderate = **3**
- Weak = **1**
- None = **0**

**Step 4:** Compute the **raw score** for each Engineering Characteristic:

$$
Raw_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times r_{ij}
$$

**Step 5:** Normalize to obtain the **Technical Importance Rating (TIR)**:

$$
TIR_j = \dfrac{Raw_j}{\max(Raw)} \times 5
$$

This places TIR on a comparable 0–5 scale.

**Step 6:** The **Roof** of the house captures the *correlations* between ECs (++, +, –, or blank). Negative correlations (–) are design conflicts (TRIZ territory).

**Step 7:** The **competitive benchmark** assigns target values to each EC.

### 3.2.2 Numerical Example

Suppose a **portable blender** has 3 CRs and 3 ECs. Let $w = (5, 4, 3)$ and the relationship matrix be:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 3 & 1 \\ 3 & 9 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute raw scores:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Raw_1 &= 5 \times 9 + 4 \times 3 + 3 \times 1 = 45 + 12 + 3 = 60 \\
Raw_2 &= 5 \times 3 + 4 \times 9 + 3 \times 0 = 15 + 36 + 0 = 51 \\
Raw_3 &= 5 \times 1 + 4 \times 0 + 3 \times 9 = 5 + 0 + 27 = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum raw score is $Raw_{max} = 60$. Normalize:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_1 &= \dfrac{60}{60} \times 5 = 5.00 \\
TIR_2 &= \dfrac{51}{60} \times 5 = 4.25 \\
TIR_3 &= \dfrac{32}{60} \times 5 \approx 2.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** EC₁ (e.g., "Motor Power") has the highest technical priority.

---

## 3.3 Pugh Matrix — Concept Selection (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Choose a **baseline (datum)** concept.
**Step 2:** List **selection criteria** (e.g., cost, weight, ergonomics, manufacturability).
**Step 3:** Compare each alternative against baseline:

- **+1** = Better than baseline
- **0** = Same as baseline
- **–1** = Worse than baseline

**Step 4:** Sum scores per concept: $Score_k = \sum_j s_{kj}$.

**Step 5:** Highest $Score$ wins.

### 3.3.1 Numerical Example

For a new electric kettle design, baseline = "Existing Model A":

| Criterion (weight not used) | Concept B | Concept C | Concept D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | +1 | 0 | –1 |
| Ergonomics | +1 | +1 | +1 |
| Aesthetics | 0 | +1 | +1 |
| Manufacturing | –1 | +1 | 0 |
| **Total Score** | **+1** | **+3** | **+1** |

**Concept C** wins. → Proceed to detailed design.

---

## 3.4 AHP — Analytical Hierarchy Process — Pairwise Comparison

Saaty's **AHP** uses a **1–9 scale** for pairwise comparisons.

**Step 1:** Build a pairwise matrix $A$ where $a_{ij} = 1/a_{ji}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the **priority vector** $w$ as the normalized principal eigenvector:

$$
A \cdot w = \lambda_{max} \cdot w
$$

For small $n$, approximate $w$ by normalizing the column means (geometric mean method):

$$
w_i = \dfrac{\left( \prod_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} \right)^{1/n}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( \prod_{j=1}^{n} a_{kj} \right)^{1/n}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute **Consistency Index**:

$$
CI = \dfrac{\lambda_{max} - n}{n - 1}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute **Consistency Ratio**:

$$
CR = \dfrac{CI}{RI}
$$

where $RI$ is the random index ($n=3: RI=0.58$; $n=4: RI=0.90$; $n=5: RI=1.12$).

**Step 5:** Accept if $CR < 0.10$; otherwise revise the matrix.

### 3.4.1 Worked Example

A $3 \times 3$ pairwise matrix comparing *Cost, Quality, Time* for a product concept:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 1/3 & 1 & 2 \\ 1/5 & 1/2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute column sums: $1 + 0.333 + 0.2 = 1.533$ (col 1); $3 + 1 + 0.5 = 4.5$ (col 2); $5 + 2 + 1 = 8$ (col 3).

Normalize each column:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{norm} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.652 & 0.667 & 0.625 \\ 0.217 & 0.222 & 0.250 \\ 0.130 & 0.111 & 0.125 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Row averages (priority vector):

$$
w = \begin{bmatrix} 0.648 \\ 0.230 \\ 0.122 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Cost has **64.8%** priority. To verify, $Aw$ and compute $\lambda_{max}$:

$$
Aw = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 0.648 + 3 \cdot 0.230 + 5 \cdot 0.122 \\ \ldots \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.948 \\ 0.692 \\ 0.367 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\lambda_i = \dfrac{(Aw)_i}{w_i} \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 1.948/0.648 = 3.006
$$

Averaging gives $\lambda_{max} \approx 3.004$. Then:

$$
CI = \dfrac{3.004 - 3}{2} = 0.002 \quad ; \quad CR = \dfrac{0.002}{0.58} \approx 0.0034 < 0.10
$$

**Matrix is consistent.** ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Tools

| Tool | Phase of Use | Output Type | Quantitative? | KTU Module Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brainstorming | Concept | Idea list | No | Module 4 |
| SCAMPER | Concept | Idea variants | No | Module 4 |
| Morphological Chart | Concept | Combination matrix | Semi | Module 4 |
| QFD (HoQ) | Concept → Detail | TIR, targets | Yes | Module 4 |
| Pugh Matrix | Concept Selection | Ranked concepts | Yes | Module 4 |
| AHP | Concept Selection | Priority weights | Yes | Module 4 |
| FMEA | Detail Design | RPN / AP | Yes | Module 4 |
| DFM/DFA | Detail Design | Efficiency % | Yes | Module 4 |
| CAD | All | 3D model, drawings | Yes (file metrics) | Module 4 |
| FEA | Validation | Stress/displacement | Yes | Module 4 |
| Ergonomics | Validation | RULA/REBA score | Yes | Module 4 |
| 3D Printing | Prototype | Physical part | N/A | Module 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tools for Product Design — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the *sequential application* of tools across the **Product Development Funnel**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Empathize & Define] --> B[Ideation Tools]
    B --> B1[Brainstorming]
    B --> B2[SCAMPER]
    B --> B3[Morphological Chart]
    B --> B4[TRIZ]
    B1 --> C[Concept Generation]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Selection Tools]
    D --> D1[Pugh Matrix]
    D --> D2[AHP]
    D --> D3[Kano Model]
    D1 --> E[Selected Concept]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Analytical Tools]
    F --> F1[QFD House of Quality]
    F --> F2[FMEA]
    F --> F3[DFM and DFA]
    F1 --> G[Detailed Design Specs]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Visualization Tools]
    H --> H1[CAD Solid Modeling]
    H --> H2[Rendering]
    H --> H3[Technical Drawings]
    H1 --> I[Prototype Realization]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> I1[3D Printing]
    I --> I2[CNC Machining]
    I --> I3[Vacuum Forming]
    I1 --> J[Validation and Testing]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> J1[FEA Simulation]
    J --> J2[Ergonomic Assessment]
    J --> J3[User Testing]
    J1 --> K[Final Product]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows a **left-to-right flow** from divergent ideation (left) to convergent validation (right). Tools are deployed in overlapping waves, not strictly sequential.

---

## 4.2 FMEA Process — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Identify Component] --> node2[List Potential Failure Modes]
    node2 --> node3[Assign Severity S]
    node3 --> node4[Assign Occurrence O]
    node4 --> node5[Assign Detection D]
    node5 --> node6[Compute RPN]
    node6 --> node7{RPN >= 100 or S >= 9}
    node7 -->|Yes| node8[Trigger Design Action]
    node7 -->|No| node9[Monitor and Document]
    node8 --> node10[Revise Design]
    node10 --> node2
    node9 --> node11[Update FMEA Sheet]
```

---

## 4.3 QFD House of Quality — Schematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Customer Requirements] --> blockB[Importance Weights W]
    blockB --> blockC[Relationship Matrix R]
    blockC --> blockD[Technical Characteristics]
    blockD --> blockE[Correlation Roof]
    blockE --> blockF[Engineering Targets]
    blockF --> blockG[Final Specifications]
```

---

## 4.4 Prototyping Tool Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Need for Prototype] --> p2{Quantity Required}
    p2 -->|1 to 5 units| p3[3D Printing FDM or SLA]
    p2 -->|5 to 50 units| p4[Vacuum Forming or CNC]
    p2 -->|More than 50| p5[Injection Molding]
    p1 --> p6{Functional Test}
    p6 -->|Yes| p7[Functional Prototype]
    p6 -->|No| p8[Visual Prototype]
    p7 --> p9[Alpha Prototype]
    p8 --> p10[Looks Like Model]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define FMEA. Explain the significance of the RPN in product design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a systematic, proactive analytical tool used to identify potential failure modes in a product or process, evaluate their impact, and prioritize corrective actions. It was first developed by the U.S. military in the 1940s and is now standard in **automotive (IATF 16949)**, **aerospace (AS9100)**, and **medical device (ISO 13485)** industries.

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is computed as:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), and $D$ = Detection (1–10). A high RPN signals that the failure mode demands immediate engineering intervention — material change, design redundancy, or added inspection. It enables objective ranking of qualitative risks, ensuring limited engineering resources are deployed on the most critical issues.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain any three ideation tools used in product design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Brainstorming:** A group-based, judgment-free ideation method following four rules — no criticism, quantity over quality, build on others' ideas, freewheeling welcomes wild ideas. *(1 mark)*

2. **SCAMPER:** A checklist-based technique using seven prompts — **S**ubstitute, **C**ombine, **A**dapt, **M**odify, **P**ut to another use, **E**liminate, **R**earrange. Each prompt nudges the designer toward a different cognitive angle. *(1 mark)*

3. **Morphological Chart:** A matrix that decomposes a system into key parameters (axes) and lists possible solutions (cells). All combinations are then explored, often yielding novel hybrid concepts. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **House of Quality (HoQ)** in QFD with a neat block diagram. Explain its construction in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a **rechargeable electric toothbrush**, three customer requirements are: (i) Battery Life, (ii) Brushing Effectiveness, (iii) Ergonomic Grip. The engineering characteristics are: (a) Motor Power, (b) Battery Capacity, (c) Handle Diameter. Customer weights are $w = (5, 4, 3)$. The relationship matrix is given. Compute the **Technical Importance Ratings (TIRs)** and identify the highest-priority engineering characteristic. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** The **House of Quality (HoQ)** is a matrix-based planning tool introduced by **Yoji Akao (1972)** that translates customer needs (Whats) into engineering characteristics (Hows). It is the first matrix of QFD's "Four Houses."

**Construction Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Left Wall (Whats):** List customer requirements and assign importance weights $w_i$ (1–5 scale).
2. **Ceiling (Hows):** List engineering characteristics in measurable units.
3. **Relationship Matrix (Center):** Symbol/quantify interaction between Whats and Hows (Strong=9, Moderate=3, Weak=1).
4. **Roof:** Inter-relationship between Hows (Strong positive ++, positive +, negative –, none).
5. **Right Wall (Competitive Benchmark):** Compare with competitor products on each CR.
6. **Floor (Hows Targets):** Specify target values for each EC.
7. **Importance Ratings:** Compute $Raw_j$ and $TIR_j$.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the House of Quality schematic in Section 4.3 of these notes.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

$$
w = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 3 & 1 \\ 3 & 9 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Here, $w_1 = 5$ (Battery Life), $w_2 = 4$ (Brushing Effectiveness), $w_3 = 3$ (Ergonomic Grip).

EC₁ = Motor Power, EC₂ = Battery Capacity, EC₃ = Handle Diameter.

**Step 1:** Compute raw scores (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Raw_1 &= 5(9) + 4(3) + 3(1) = 45 + 12 + 3 = 60 \\
Raw_2 &= 5(3) + 4(9) + 3(0) = 15 + 36 + 0 = 51 \\
Raw_3 &= 5(1) + 4(0) + 3(9) = 5 + 0 + 27 = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify maximum (1 mark): $Raw_{max} = 60$.

**Step 3:** Normalize to TIR (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_1 &= \frac{60}{60} \times 5 = 5.00 \\
TIR_2 &= \frac{51}{60} \times 5 = 4.25 \\
TIR_3 &= \frac{32}{60} \times 5 \approx 2.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Ranking (1 mark):

| Rank | Engineering Characteristic | TIR |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor Power | 5.00 |
| 2 | Battery Capacity | 4.25 |
| 3 | Handle Diameter | 2.67 |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** **Motor Power (EC₁)** has the highest technical importance with a TIR of 5.00. Engineering effort should focus on optimizing motor specifications — torque, RPM, brush-head oscillation pattern. Battery Capacity is a close second (TIR = 4.25) and should be the secondary focus. Handle Diameter has the lowest TIR (2.67), indicating a lesser (but not negligible) priority.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **normalize** the raw scores. Always present both raw and TIR values. Also, units of measurement (e.g., mAh for battery) should be specified to earn full marks on the target-values section.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain **FMEA** in detail. List and define the three rating parameters used in calculating RPN. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a **handheld power drill**, four failure modes have been identified. The S, O, D values are:

- F₁: Battery overheating — $S=9, O=4, D=3$
- F₂: Chuck slippage — $S=7, O=5, D=6$
- F₃: Trigger switch failure — $S=6, O=6, D=5$
- F₄: Plastic housing crack — $S=5, O=7, D=4$

Compute the RPN for each, rank them, and recommend the top-priority failure mode for corrective action. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is a structured analytical method to **identify**, **evaluate**, and **prioritize** potential failure modes of a product or process. It was formalized in **MIL-STD-1629 (1949)** and is now mandated in industries like **automotive (AIAG-VDA)** and **aerospace**.

**Three Rating Parameters (5 marks):**

1. **Severity (S):** Quantifies the seriousness of the *consequence* of a failure to the customer or system. Scale: 1 (negligible) to 10 (catastrophic — loss of life, safety hazard). Note: $S = 9$ or $10$ demands action *regardless* of RPN. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Occurrence (O):** Estimates the *likelihood* that the failure mode will actually occur. Scale: 1 (very rare — less than 1 in a million) to 10 (almost inevitable — more than 1 in 2). Based on field data, similar products, or expert judgment. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Detection (D):** Assesses the *probability* that the failure will be detected *before* reaching the customer. Scale: 1 (almost certain detection) to 10 (no inspection method can detect it). Lower $D$ = better detection. *(2 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Formula (1 mark):** $RPN = S \times O \times D$

**Step 1: RPN Calculations (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_1 &= 9 \times 4 \times 3 = 108 \\
RPN_2 &= 7 \times 5 \times 6 = 210 \\
RPN_3 &= 6 \times 6 \times 5 = 180 \\
RPN_4 &= 5 \times 7 \times 4 = 140
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Ranking (2 marks)**

| Rank | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F₂ Chuck slippage | 7 | 5 | 6 | **210** |
| 2 | F₃ Trigger switch failure | 6 | 6 | 5 | 180 |
| 3 | F₄ Plastic housing crack | 5 | 7 | 4 | 140 |
| 4 | F₁ Battery overheating | 9 | 4 | 3 | 108 |

**Step 3: Recommendation (2 marks)**

Although F₂ has the highest RPN (210), **F₁ (Battery overheating)** has $S = 9$ (high severity — a potential fire/safety hazard). Per the **AIAG-VDA FMEA 2019 philosophy**, *severity should be the primary driver of action*, even if RPN is lower. **Dual recommendation:**

- **Critical Priority (Safety):** Address F₁ — add thermal cutoff, venting, and BMS protection.
- **High RPN Priority (Quality):** Address F₂ — improve chuck keyless design, add torque-limiting clutch.

**Final engineering decision:** F₁ takes precedence due to safety implications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mistake:** Students blindly rank by RPN and ignore the **severity rule**. KTU examiners specifically test this nuance. Always comment on $S \ge 9$ failures as separate critical items, even if their RPN is moderate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **FMEA:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$; range $1$–$1000$. Threshold for action: $RPN \ge 100$ or $S \ge 9$.
- [ ] **QFD House of Quality:** $TIR_j = \dfrac{\sum_i w_i r_{ij}}{\max(\sum_i w_i r_{ij})} \times 5$.
- [ ] **Pugh Matrix:** Scores use $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ against a baseline; sum to rank.
- [ ] **AHP:** Use Saaty 1–9 scale; check $CR < 0.10$; $CI = \dfrac{\lambda_{max} - n}{n-1}$.
- [ ] **SCAMPER:** 7 prompts — Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put-to-another-use, Eliminate, Rearrange.
- [ ] **Morphological Chart:** Zwicky matrix; parameters × solutions; combinatorial explosion.
- [ ] **TRIZ:** 40 inventive principles; contradiction matrix; ideality = benefits / (cost + harm).
- [ ] **3D Printing methods:** FDM (cheap, slow), SLA (resin, high detail), SLS (no support), DMLS (metal).
- [ ] **CAD formats:** `.STEP` (neutral), `.IGES` (legacy), `.STL` (mesh, 3D print), `.SLDPRT` (SolidWorks native).
- [ ] **DFA — Boothroyd:** $\eta = (N_{min} / N_a) \times 100\%$; goal $\eta \ge 60\%$.
- [ ] **NIOSH Lifting:** 6 multipliers, all $\le 1$; default $LC = 23$ kg.
- [ ] **Kano Model:** Basic (must-be), Performance (one-dimensional), Excitement (delighters).
- [ ] **Pugh vs. AHP:** Pugh is *faster*; AHP is *more rigorous* with consistency check.
- [ ] **Ergonomics:** RULA (posture), REBA (whole body), NIOSH (lifting).
- [ ] **FEA vs. CFD:** FEA = structural/thermal; CFD = fluid flow + heat transfer.
- [ ] **Cost lock-in:** ~**70–80%** of life-cycle cost is committed in the design phase → *shift-left* with tools.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question involving FMEA or QFD, always draw the **matrix/table** *and* show the **formula** *and* do the **numerical computation** *and* write a **2-line engineering conclusion**. Skipping any one of these will cost 1–2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drafting and Modelling Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Drafting and Modelling Software # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Drafting and Modelling Software** refers to the class of **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** and **Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE)** digital tools used by product designers and engineers to **create, modify, analyse, optimise, document, and communicate** the geometry and structure of a physical product across its entire design lifecycle — from conceptual ideation to manufacturing release. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEMET837**, drafting and modelling software is treated as the **digital backbone of the modern product development process (PDP)**. It bridges the gap between *conceptual ideation* (sketches, mood boards) and *engineering realisation* (prototyping, tooling, manufacturing). The software domain is broadly classified into four interacting layers: | Layer | Function | Typical Examples | |---|---|---| | **2D Drafting** | Flat technical drawings with dimensions, GD&T, tolerances | AutoCAD, DraftSight, LibreCAD | | **3D Solid Modelling** | Parametric, feature-based solid geometry | SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo (Pro/E), Autodesk Inventor, NX | | **Surface / Freeform Modelling** | Complex organic, aesthetic, or aerodynamic skins | Rhino 3D, Alias, CATIA Surface, Blender | | **CAE / Simulation Add-ons** | FEA, CFD, motion, topology optimisation | ANSYS, Abaqus, SolidWorks Simulation, COMSOL | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Sculptor's Workshop" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for a first-time learner:** > Imagine you are a **master carpenter designing a luxury chair**. Before cutting a single piece of wood, you would: > 1. Draw a flat sketch on paper (this is **2D drafting**). > 2. Build a 1:1 wooden mock-up by gluing blocks together — adding legs, carving the seat, hollowing the back (this is **3D solid modelling**, where every cut, extrude, or fillet is a *feature*). > 3. Sculpt a flowing, curved armrest from clay that flows organically (this is **surface / freeform modelling**). > 4. Drop the chair into a wind tunnel and stress-test every joint virtually (this is **CAE simulation**). > 5. Send an instruction manual with exploded views to the factory (this is **drawing / documentation**). > > *Drafting and Modelling Software is the digital workshop where all five of these happen on a screen, with millimetre precision, in hours instead of weeks.* ## 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU Product Design Curriculum The 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs) for Module 4 specifically require the student to be able to **select the appropriate modelling strategy** for a given product, **interpret vendor-neutral neutral file formats**, and **defend a software choice** during design review. Hence, mastering the *taxonomy* of these tools is as important as operating them. > [!TIP] > **Key Terms You Must Know Before Proceeding:** > - **Feature** — a recognisable geometric operation (extrude, cut, fillet, hole, shell). > - **Constraint** — a design rule that ties geometry together (parallel, tangent, coincident, equal). > - **Parametric** — dimensions drive geometry; change one number, the model updates. > - **History Tree / Feature Tree** — the chronological log of every modelling step. > - **B-Rep (Boundary Representation)** — the underlying mathematical representation of a solid using faces, edges, and vertices. > - **CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry)** — solids built by Boolean union, subtract, intersect of primitives. > - **Kernel** — the low-level geometric engine (e.g., Parasolid, ACIS, CGM). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Modelling Paradigms Modern CAD software is built on one (or a hybrid) of the following geometric paradigms. Each has a "personality" — strengths, weaknesses, and ideal product categories. ### A. 2D Drafting Paradigm - Operates on flat Cartesian planes (X, Y). - Geometry is described by **primitives**: lines, arcs, polylines, splines, hatches, dimensions. - Output is the **engineering drawing** — the legally binding artefact for manufacturing. - Strengths: lightweight, universal, low hardware demand. - Weaknesses: no 3D visualisation, ambiguous interpretations, error-prone revisions. ### B. Wireframe Modelling - The earliest 3D paradigm; describes edges only (vertices + edges). - No information about faces, volumes, or material. - Ambiguous — many wireframes can produce the same screen image. - *Mostly obsolete today; only historically relevant.* ### C. Surface Modelling - Builds **zero-thickness skins** draped over curves (NURBS are the mathematical core). - Ideal for **aesthetic / organic / aerodynamic** products: car bodies, mobile phone enclosures, consumer electronics, footwear. - Cannot natively represent mass, weight, or interior cavities. ### D. Solid Modelling (the industry workhorse) - Fully **closed, watertight, volumetric** geometry. - Three mathematical engines coexist: - **CSG** — Boolean tree of primitives. - **B-Rep** — faces, edges, vertices with topological connectivity (used by Parasolid and ACIS kernels). - **Implicit / Function representation** — equation-based (used in topology optimisation). ### E. Parametric vs. Direct (Explicit) Modelling > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parametric Modelling** — The model's geometry is driven by **named dimensions and constraints**. Changing the value of a dimension cascades through the entire feature tree. > **Direct (Explicit) Modelling** — Geometry is pushed/pulled like clay; no feature history required. Excellent for legacy data, imported meshes, and rapid concept iterations. ### F. Hybrid / Sub-Divisional Modelling - Subdivision surfaces (Sub-D) blend NURBS smoothness with polygonal editability. - Used in industrial design (ID) studios; eventually converted to NURBS for engineering. ## 2.2 The Modelling Workflow — Standard Industrial Pipeline The product design process follows a **closed-loop V-model** of drafting and modelling operations: 1. **Concept Sketch** — freehand ideation (paper, Wacom tablet, software like SketchBook). 2. **2D Layout** — dimensioned concept sketches with critical-to-function (CTF) features marked. 3. **3D Solid Generation** — feature-based construction in parametric environment. 4. **Surfacing Pass** — class-A surfaces for visible/cosmetic faces. 5. **Assembly Modelling** — mating components, kinematics, interference checks. 6. **Drafting / Detailing** — generating manufacturing drawings with GD&T. 7. **CAE Validation** — FEA, CFD, thermal, motion simulation. 8. **Design Review & Iteration** — feedback loop back to step 3. 9. **Manufacturing Release** — STEP / IGES / 3D PDF / 2D DWG output. ## 2.3 File Formats & Data Interoperability | Format | Extension | Use Case | Neutral? | |---|---|---|---| | **STEP** | .step / .stp | Product model exchange (ISO 10303) | Yes — most universal | | **IGES** | .igs | Older 2D/3D exchange (ANSI/USPRO) | Yes — legacy | | **Parasolid** | .x_t / .x_b | Solid kernel data | Vendor (Siemens) | | **ACIS** | .sat | Solid kernel data | Vendor (Dassault/Spatial) | | **STL** | .stl | 3D printing mesh (triangulated) | Yes — mesh only | | **OBJ** | .obj | Polygon mesh with UVs/textures | Yes | | **JT** | .jt | Lightweight 3D for visualisation | Open ISO | | **DWG / DXF** | .dwg / .dxf | 2D AutoCAD drawings | Vendor (Autodesk) | > [!NOTE] > For KTU exams, **always recommend STEP (.step)** as the neutral answer for cross-software data exchange unless the question specifies a legacy or specialised context. ## 2.4 Comparison Matrix — Leading CAD Software Suites The following KTU-oriented cheat sheet consolidates the **strategic comparison** students are expected to recall: | Criterion | SolidWorks | CATIA | Creo (Pro/E) | Autodesk Inventor | Siemens NX | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Kernel | Parasolid | CGM (own) | ACIS / own | ACIS | Parasolid | | Parametric | Yes | Yes | Yes (pioneered) | Yes | Yes | | Direct Edit | Yes (SWX 2019+) | Yes | Yes (Freestyle) | Limited | Yes (Synchronous Tech) | | Surface Quality | High | Best-in-class (Class A) | High | Medium | High | | Automotive Use | Medium | Highest (BMW, Airbus) | Medium | Low | High | | Aerospace Use | Medium | Highest | High | Medium | High | | Consumer Goods Use | Highest | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | | Learning Curve | Easiest | Steepest | Steep | Easy | Steep | | Typical KTU Lab Tool | Yes | — | — | Yes | — | > [!TIP] > **Selection Heuristic for KTU Case-Study Answers:** > - *Aesthetic consumer product (phone, helmet)* → **Rhino / Alias / CATIA Surface** > - *Mechanical assembly (gearbox, engine)* → **SolidWorks / Creo** > - *Aerospace structural assembly* → **CATIA / NX** > - *Sheet metal / fabrication* → **SolidWorks / Inventor** > - *Architectural / civil concept* → **Revit / AutoCAD** > - *Topology-optimised lightweighting* → **SolidWorks + nTopology / Inspire / ANSYS** ## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice - **Shorter time-to-market**: a feature that took 2 weeks to draw manually now takes 2 hours. - **Design Re-use**: parametric templates and configurators allow mass-customisation. - **Concurrent Engineering**: multiple designers can work on the same assembly simultaneously using PDM/PLM systems (SolidWorks PDM, Teamcenter, Enovia). - **Single Source of Truth**: the 3D model IS the drawing — 2D drawings are auto-generated views. - **Closed-loop validation**: integrated CAE eliminates the historical "throw it over the wall" syndrome between design and analysis teams. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Logic > [!NOTE] > Because *Drafting and Modelling Software* is a descriptive-engineering topic, the "derivation" here is a **deterministic workflow derivation** — i.e., how an engineer logically proceeds from raw concept to manufacturing-ready model. Every transition is annotated. ## 3.1 The Feature-Based Modelling Algorithm (Pedagogical Form) The standard parametric workflow can be expressed as a deterministic finite-state machine. Let: $$ M = \{F_1, F_2, F_3, \dots, F_n\} $$ represent the model $M$ as a *finite ordered set of features*. Each feature $F_i$ is defined as: $$ F_i = (T_i, S_i, P_i, C_i) $$ where: - $T_i$ = **Type** of feature (extrude, cut, fillet, hole, shell, draft, rib, loft, sweep). - $S_i$ = **Sketch profile** — a closed 2D region. - $P_i$ = **Parameters** — numerical inputs (depth, angle, radius). - $C_i$ = **Constraints** — geometric relations (perpendicular, equal, coincident). The modelling algorithm proceeds as: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & M_0 \leftarrow \varnothing \quad \text{(empty model state)} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Choose a base reference frame and origin } O. \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Sketch } S_1 \text{ on a reference plane } \Pi_1. \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Apply dimensional and geometric constraints to } S_1. \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Select } T_1 \text{ (e.g., EXTRUDE), set } P_1 \text{ (depth } d_1). \\ \text{Step 6:} \quad & M_1 \leftarrow M_0 \cup F_1 \quad \text{(commit feature to history)}. \\ \text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Repeat Steps 3–6 for } F_2, F_3, \dots, F_n. \\ \text{Step 8:} \quad & M = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} F_i \quad \text{(final model)}. \\ \text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Validate with topological checks: } \partial M = \text{closed manifold}. \\ \text{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Derive 2D drawings } D_k = \text{View}(M, \theta_k, \phi_k). \end{aligned} $$ **Annotation of Key Transitions:** - *Steps 3 → 4:* A sketch is **never fully defined** until the colour of all entities turns from blue (under-defined) to black (fully defined). KTU examiners love asking this. - *Step 6:* The feature is **appended** to the history tree. *You cannot retroactively insert a feature in the middle without downstream regeneration warnings.* - *Step 9:* Topological validity ensures the model can be meshed, manufactured, and 3D-printed. An open boundary $\partial M \neq \varnothing$ means the model has holes (a leak). - *Step 10:* $D_k$ are orthographic projections generated by rotating the view camera. $\theta_k$ is the azimuth angle (0°, 90°, 180°, 270° for principal views) and $\phi_k$ is the elevation. ## 3.2 Example — Derivation of a Bracket's Feature Count > **Problem:** A simple L-bracket is made by: > 1. Extruding a 60 × 40 mm rectangle to 10 mm thickness. > 2. Cutting a 20 mm diameter hole through it. > 3. Filleting all edges with R3 mm. > 4. Adding a 5 mm rib from base to vertical face. > > **Derive the model's feature set $M$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sketch: } S_1 & = \text{Rectangle}(60 \text{ mm}, 40 \text{ mm}), \text{ on Plane}_{front}. \\ \text{Feature 1: } F_1 & = (\text{EXTRUDE}, S_1, d_1 = 10 \text{ mm}, \text{normal} = +\hat{z}). \\ M_1 & = M_0 \cup F_1. \\[4pt] \text{Sketch: } S_2 & = \text{Circle}(\phi = 20 \text{ mm}) \text{ on top face of } M_1. \\ \text{Feature 2: } F_2 & = (\text{CUT\_EXTRUDE}, S_2, d_2 = 10 \text{ mm}, \text{through all}). \\ M_2 & = M_1 \cup F_2. \\[4pt] \text{Feature 3: } F_3 & = (\text{FILLET}, \partial M_2, R = 3 \text{ mm}). \\ M_3 & = M_2 \cup F_3. \\[4pt] \text{Sketch: } S_4 & = \text{Triangle} \text{ on side face}. \\ \text{Feature 4: } F_4 & = (\text{RIB\_EXTRUDE}, S_4, \text{thickness} = 5 \text{ mm}). \\ M & = M_3 \cup F_4. \\[4pt] \therefore \quad \vert M \vert & = 4 \text{ features}. \end{aligned} $$ > *Where the cardinality $\vert M \vert$ denotes the number of features in the history tree.* ## 3.3 Worked Logic — Parametric Update Cascade **Problem:** The user changes the hole diameter from 20 mm to 25 mm. What happens? $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial: } \quad \phi_2 & = 20 \text{ mm}. \\ \text{Input: } \quad \phi_2' & = 25 \text{ mm}. \\ \text{Kernel update: } \quad & \text{Parasolid recomputes } F_2 \text{ with new sketch dimension}. \\ \text{Cascade check: } \quad & F_3 \text{ (fillet) regenerates only if edge topology changed}. \\ & F_4 \text{ (rib) is independent — no cascade}. \\ \text{Result: } \quad & \Delta t_{\text{regen}} < 1 \text{ s for a 4-feature model}. \\ & \Delta t_{\text{regen}} \text{ may rise to minutes for assemblies of } > 10{,}000 \text{ features}. \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering insight:** This cascade is the *very reason* parametric CAD was revolutionary — manual redrawing would take hours; the kernel does it in seconds. ## 3.4 Worked Logic — Interference Detection in Assemblies For an assembly of $n$ parts, Boolean interference is tested pairwise: $$ \text{Interference}(P_i, P_j) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } P_i \cap P_j \neq \varnothing \text{ and } i \neq j, \\ 0, & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} $$ Total interference pairs to check: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{pairs}} & = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}. \end{aligned} $$ For an assembly of $n = 20$ parts: $$ N_{\text{pairs}} = \frac{20 \times 19}{2} = 190 \text{ pairs}. $$ Each pair is evaluated in $O(\log m)$ using BVH (Bounding Volume Hierarchy) acceleration. This is why large assemblies benefit from *simplified representations* (envelope, silhouette) in KTU projects. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — CAD Ecosystem ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Conceptual Sketches
Pen and Paper] --> B[2D Drafting
AutoCAD DraftSight] B --> C[3D Solid Modelling
SolidWorks Creo CATIA NX] A --> D[Industrial Design Surfacing
Rhino Alias Blender] D --> C C --> E[Assembly Modelling
Mates Constraints Sub-assemblies] E --> F[CAE Simulation
FEA CFD Motion Thermal] F --> G{Design Validated?} G -- No --> C G -- Yes --> H[Engineering Drawings
GD&T Tolerances BOM] H --> I[Manufacturing Release
STEP IGES DWG STL] I --> J[Manufacturing
CNC 3D Print Injection Mold] J --> K[Field Feedback] K --> A ``` ## 4.2 Modelling Kernel and Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph UI[User Interface Layer] UI1[Sketcher] UI2[Feature Toolbar] UI3[Assembly Manager] UI4[Drawing View Manager] end subgraph LOGIC[Application Logic Layer] LOG1[Constraint Solver] LOG2[Feature History Manager] LOG3[Parametric Engine] end subgraph KERNEL[Geometric Kernel] K1[Parasolid] K2[ACIS] K3[CGM Core] end subgraph IO[File I/O Translators] IO1[STEP Translator] IO2[IGES Translator] IO3[DWG Translator] IO4[STL Mesh Exporter] end UI1 --> LOG1 UI2 --> LOG2 UI3 --> LOG3 LOG1 --> K1 LOG2 --> K1 LOG3 --> K2 K1 --> IO1 K2 --> IO2 K3 --> IO3 K1 --> IO4 ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — Parametric Feature Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Define Base Sketch
on reference plane] --> S2{Step 2: Sketch
Fully Constrained?} S2 -- No --> S3[Add Dimensions and Relations] --> S2 S2 -- Yes --> S4[Step 3: Select Feature Type
Extrude, Cut, Revolve, Sweep, Loft] S4 --> S5[Step 4: Set Feature Parameters
depth angle radius] S5 --> S6[Step 5: Commit Feature
Append to History Tree] S6 --> S7{Step 6: Model
Complete?} S7 -- No --> S1 S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 7: Apply Dress-up Features
Fillet, Chamfer, Shell, Draft] S8 --> S9[Step 8: Validate Topology
Check Watertight] S9 --> S10[Step 9: Generate Drawings
Orthographic Isometric Sections] S10 --> S11[Step 10: Release for Manufacturing
STEP or DWG Export] ``` ## 4.4 Comparison — Parametric vs. Direct Modelling Philosophy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PARAM[Parametric Modelling Approach] P1[Input Dimensions and Constraints] --> P2[Kernel Builds Feature Tree] P2 --> P3[Change Dimension] P3 --> P4[Tree Regenerates Model] P4 --> P5[Updated Geometry] end subgraph DIRECT[Direct Modelling Approach] D1[Select Face Edge or Vertex] --> D2[Push Pull Move Delete] D2 --> D3[Geometry Updates Immediately] D3 --> D4[No History Tree Required] end ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Differentiate between parametric modelling and direct (explicit) modelling. Give one suitable software example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Parametric Modelling | Direct Modelling | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Geometry driven by named dimensions and constraints | Geometry manipulated directly without history | | **Change Propagation** | Automatic via feature tree regeneration | Immediate push/pull action | | **Best Use** | Clean designs from scratch, mass-customisation | Imported mesh/legacy data, rapid ideation | | **Example** | SolidWorks, Creo, CATIA | Rhino (Direct Edit mode), SpaceClaim, Fusion 360 | *(Award 1 mark each for any three correct points. Award ½ mark each for the software examples.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the STEP file format? Why is it preferred over proprietary formats for design data exchange?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **STEP** (Standard for the Exchange of Product model data) is an **ISO 10303** compliant neutral file format used to exchange **3D product model data** between different CAD software packages without loss of geometry, topology, product manufacturing information (PMI), and metadata. **Reasons for preference over proprietary formats:** 1. **Vendor-neutral** — not tied to any single CAD vendor (unlike .sldprt of SolidWorks or .prt of CATIA). 2. **Complete data preservation** — transfers B-Rep solid + surfaces + assemblies + PMI. 3. **ISO standard** — internationally recognised; legally acceptable in long-term archival. 4. **Future-proof** — readable by any modern CAD, even decades later. *(Award 1½ marks for definition, 1½ marks for the preference reasoning — distribute appropriately.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students *list* software names instead of *justifying* their selection against product requirements, cost, training, and interoperability. **Justification always earns more marks than memorised lists.** Always back claims with reasoning. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **A startup is developing a wearable fitness tracker with a curved, ergonomic polymer enclosure that wraps around the wrist. Discuss the suitability of (i) SolidWorks, (ii) CATIA, and (iii) Rhino 3D as candidate software platforms. Recommend the optimal choice and justify with three engineering reasons.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Identify the design challenge (1 mark):** The product requires *organic, ergonomic, freeform surface geometry* with high aesthetic class-A finish, combined with internal electronics packaging and DFM for injection moulding. **Step 2 — Software-by-software evaluation (4 marks):** - **(i) SolidWorks:** Excellent for mechanical internals, mould design, and DFM analysis. However, its *surface modelling module* is competent but not class-leading for organic freeform shapes. Best for: *internal mechanics, PCB mounts, battery housing*. - **(ii) CATIA:** Industry benchmark for automotive and aerospace surfacing. Pivotal Class-A surfacing tools (e.g., ICEM Surf). However, *expensive*, *steep learning curve*, overkill for a small startup. Best for: *high-end surface continuity*. - **(iii) Rhino 3D:** Built on **NURBS mathematics**; ideal for organic, ergonomic, freeform shapes. Affordable for startups (lower licence cost), vast plugin ecosystem (Grasshopper for generative design, V-Ray for visualisation). Best for: *external ergonomic skin*. **Step 3 — Recommendation with justification (2 marks):** **Recommended choice: Rhino 3D as the primary surfacing tool, integrated with SolidWorks for mechanical and DFM work** via STEP exchange. **Three engineering reasons:** 1. **Cost-effectiveness for a startup** — Rhino's perpetual licence cost is significantly lower than CATIA, preserving runway capital. 2. **Surface quality** — NURBS engine handles the wrist-curve continuity with G2/G3 surface transitions, essential for skin contact comfort. 3. **Interoperability** — Rhino's STEP/IGES export enables clean handoff to SolidWorks for tolerance analysis, mould flow, and DFM, giving the best of both worlds. *(Award 2 marks for concluding recommendation + 1 mark for cross-tool workflow logic.)* #### (b) [7 Marks] **Explain the concept of a *feature tree* in parametric CAD. How does it enable design change management? Illustrate with a stepped shaft having three diameters (D1 = 30 mm, D2 = 50 mm, D3 = 40 mm) and lengths (L1 = 40 mm, L2 = 25 mm, L3 = 30 mm).** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** A **feature tree** (or **history tree**) is the chronological, ordered list of every modelling operation performed on a part, displayed typically on the left pane of the CAD GUI. Each entry corresponds to a *feature* (sketch + operation + parameters) that, when evaluated in order, reconstructs the solid. **Step 2 — Stepped shaft feature tree (3 marks):** | Order | Feature | Operation | Sketch | Parameters | |---|---|---|---|---| | F1 | Base shaft | Revolve | Circle (Ø30 mm) | Axis = centreline, 360° | | F2 | Step 1 enlargement | Extrude boss | Circle (Ø50 mm) | Depth = 40 mm, from right face | | F3 | Step 2 reduction | Extrude cut / Lathe turn | Circle (Ø40 mm) | Depth = 25 mm, from outer right | | F4 | Fillet at transitions | Fillet | Edges at steps | R = 2 mm | | F5 | Chamfer ends | Chamfer | End edges | 2 mm × 45° | **Step 3 — Change management illustration (2 marks):** - *If L2 is to change from 25 mm to 35 mm* — the user edits parameter L2 in feature F3. The kernel regenerates F3, then propagates through F4 (fillet) and F5 (chamfer) automatically. Total regeneration time is sub-second. - *If D2 must change from 50 mm to 55 mm* — the user edits parameter D2 in feature F2's sketch dimension. Cascade is similar. - *If a new feature F6 (keyway) must be added between F3 and F4* — the user uses "Roll Back" to position the cursor between F3 and F4, sketches the keyway, and rolls forward. Existing F4 and F5 regenerate against the new topology. **Conclusion:** The feature tree turns the model into a *time-ordered equation* of design intent, making parametric edits declarative rather than manual. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** #### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Compare 2D drafting and 3D solid modelling as documentation methods for a manufacturing firm. State five advantages of moving from 2D to 3D.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):** - **2D drafting** is the creation of flat technical drawings (plans, elevations, sections) on paper or in CAD using lines, arcs, polylines, dimensions, and annotations. - **3D solid modelling** is the creation of fully closed volumetric digital geometry using feature-based parametric operations, from which 2D drawings are derived. **Step 2 — Five advantages of moving from 2D to 3D (5 marks, 1 each):** 1. **Single Source of Truth:** The 3D model IS the master definition; drawings become auto-generated views, eliminating inconsistency between the part and its drawing. 2. **Automatic Interference Detection:** Assemblies are checked for collisions digitally — impossible to detect certain errors from 2D drawings alone. 3. **Mass Property Calculation:** Volume, mass, centre of gravity, and moment of inertia are computed directly, aiding FEA and shipping calculations. 4. **Direct CAE Handoff:** The same 3D model feeds into FEA, CFD, and motion simulation without re-modelling. 5. **Design Re-use and Configurators:** Parametric templates allow rapid variant generation (e.g., family of pump housings with different port sizes). **Conclusion:** While 2D drafting remains mandatory for shop-floor manufacturing drawings, 3D modelling has become the *upstream master artefact* driving accuracy, simulation, and lifecycle management. #### (b) [7 Marks] **Describe the B-Rep (Boundary Representation) data structure used in solid modelling kernels. Explain how faces, edges, and vertices maintain topological consistency with the Euler–Poincaré characteristic.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — B-Rep data structure (3 marks):** **Boundary Representation (B-Rep)** is the most widely used solid modelling scheme. It describes a solid as a closed shell of *faces*, each bounded by *edges*, each edge terminating at *vertices*. Each entity has both **geometric data** (NURBS surface for a face, curve for an edge, point for a vertex) and **topological data** (adjacency pointers). A typical B-Rep data structure: | Entity | Geometric Attribute | Topological Attributes | |---|---|---| | **Vertex (V)** | Point (x, y, z) | Adjacent edges | | **Edge (E)** | 3D curve (line, arc, spline) | Start vertex, end vertex, adjacent faces, direction | | **Face (F)** | Surface (plane, cylinder, NURBS) | Bounding loop(s), orientation (outward normal) | | **Loop (L)** | Closed sequence of edges | Adjacent face | | **Shell (S)** | Set of connected faces | Bounding solid | **Step 2 — Euler–Poincaré Characteristic (3 marks):** For a closed, simply-connected, orientable solid (genus 0, no through-holes), the **Euler equation** must hold: $$ \begin{aligned} V - E + F & = 2 \quad (\text{Euler--Poincaré for genus } 0). \end{aligned} $$ For solids with *through-holes* and *handles* (genus $g$): $$ \begin{aligned} V - E + F - H & = 2 - 2g, \end{aligned} $$ where $H$ is the number of inner loops (holes) and $g$ is the genus. **Worked example:** A solid cube has $V = 8$, $E = 12$, $F = 6$. $$ V - E + F = 8 - 12 + 6 = 2 \;\; \checkmark $$ A solid cube with one cylindrical through-hole has $H = 2$ (two circular loops — top and bottom). The kernel adds 2 new vertices per hole, and 2 new edges per hole ring: $$ V - E + F - H = (8+0) - (12+2) + (6+2) - 2 = 2 \;\; \checkmark $$ **Step 3 — Consistency check by the kernel (1 mark):** Parasolid / ACIS continually verify this invariant during every Boolean operation. If violated, the operation is rejected, preventing invalid (e.g., non-manifold) geometry from entering the model. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Drafting and Modelling Software** - ✅ Drafting & Modelling software = the digital layer of the **Product Development Process (PDP)** that performs 2D drawing, 3D modelling, assembly, simulation, and documentation. - ✅ Four major software categories: **2D drafting, solid modelling, surface modelling, CAE simulation**. - ✅ Three solid-modelling paradigms: **CSG, B-Rep, Implicit**. - ✅ Two leading CAD kernels: **Parasolid (Siemens)** and **ACIS (Dassault/Spatial)**. - ✅ Parametric vs. Direct — parametric uses **feature tree + dimensions**; direct uses **push/pull on history-free geometry**. - ✅ Class-A surfacing → **Rhino / Alias / CATIA ICEM**; mechanical assembly → **SolidWorks / Creo / NX**. - ✅ Neutral file formats: **STEP (preferred), IGES, STL**. Vendor formats: **Parasolid .x_t, ACIS .sat, DWG**. - ✅ Feature tree = chronological history of operations; enables **parametric regeneration** on dimension edits. - ✅ Fully constrained sketch = all sketch entities turn **black**; under-constrained = **blue**. - ✅ B-Rep uses **Vertex, Edge, Face, Loop, Shell, Solid** topology. - ✅ Euler–Poincaré invariant for genus-0 closed solid: $V - E + F = 2$. - ✅ Watertight models are mandatory for **3D printing, FEA meshing, CNC toolpath generation**. - ✅ Interference detection in assemblies uses **pairwise Boolean intersection** accelerated by **BVH**. - ✅ KTU examiners reward **justified selection** of software over mere naming — always cite cost, learning curve, surface quality, and interoperability.

topics.title: Patents and IP Acts: Overview and Disclosure Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET837 - PRODUCT DESIGN AND INNOVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Patents and IP Acts: Overview and Disclosure Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intellectual Property (IP) — Formal KTU Definition

**Intellectual Property (IP)** refers to the legal rights that arise from intellectual, creative, or commercial activity in the industrial, scientific, literary, or artistic fields. IP is protected through statutory instruments — primarily **patents, trademarks, copyrights, designs, trade secrets, and geographical indications** — granting the creator an exclusive, time-bound monopoly over the use, manufacture, sale, or licensing of the protected subject matter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the Indian Patents Act, **1970** (as amended in 2005), patents grant a **20-year** exclusive right from the date of filing, provided renewal fees are paid annually.

## 1.2 Patent — Formal KTU Definition

A **Patent** is a statutory, territorial, and time-limited right granted by the government (Patent Office) to an inventor (or assignee) for a **novel, non-obvious, and industrially applicable invention**, enabling the patent holder to exclude others from making, using, selling, or importing the patented invention without authorization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Patentability Criteria (Section 2(1)(j), Indian Patents Act, 1970):**
> 1. **Novelty** — Must not have been published/used anywhere in the world before the priority date.
> 2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — Must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PSITA).
> 3. **Industrial Application** — Must be capable of being made or used in an industry.
> 4. **Patentable Subject Matter** — Must NOT fall under the excluded categories (Section 3 & 4).

## 1.3 Patent Disclosure — Definition

**Patent Disclosure** refers to the formal written specification and accompanying documents submitted to the Patent Office that fully, clearly, concisely, and accurately describe the invention, the best method of performing it, and the scope of the legal protection sought (claims). Disclosure is the **technical-legal heart** of a patent application.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe in a Locked Book"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you invent a magical cake recipe.
> - **The Patent** = A government-issued *certificate* that gives you the *exclusive right* to bake and sell that cake for 20 years.
> - **The Disclosure** = The *recipe book* you must submit to the government. You must write it so clearly that any skilled baker (a "person skilled in the art") can replicate your cake from your words alone.
> - **The Claims** = The exact boundaries drawn around your cake. For example: "A cake made of flour, eggs, chocolate, and a 7-minute microwave step." Anything outside these ingredients/methods is *not* protected.
> - **Why disclose?** Because the patent system is a **social contract**: the public gets full technical knowledge of your invention, and in return, you get a **monopoly** for 20 years. After that, the cake enters the public domain — anyone can bake it.

## 1.5 Other Forms of IP — Quick Reference

| IP Form | Protects | Typical Duration (India) |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Inventions (processes, products, methods) | **20 years** from filing |
| **Trademark** | Brand names, logos, symbols | **10 years** (renewable indefinitely) |
| **Copyright** | Literary, artistic, musical, software works | Life of author + **60 years** |
| **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic appearance of an article | **15 years** (extendable by 5) |
| **Geographical Indication (GI)** | Goods tied to a specific region | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Trade Secret** | Confidential business knowledge | Indefinite (if secrecy maintained) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The four pillars of IP that are commonly asked in exams are: **Patent, Trademark, Copyright, and Trade Secret.** Always pair each with its statutory Act (Patents Act 1970, Trade Marks Act 1999, Copyright Act 1957, and Common Law/Contractual protection respectively).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Indian Patents Act, 1970 — Structural Breakdown

The Act governs the grant, registration, and enforcement of patents in India. Below are its key structural components, aligned to KTU examination expectations:

### 2.1.1 Classification of Patentable vs. Non-Patentable Inventions

**Section 3 & Section 4** of the Act define what **CANNOT** be patented in India:

> [!WARNING]
> **Non-Patentable Subject Matter (Section 3 Highlights — frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **3(a)** — Frivolous inventions contrary to natural laws.
> - **3(b)** — Inventions contrary to public order, morality, or harmful to human/animal/plant life.
> - **3(c)** — Discovery of any living or non-living substance occurring in nature.
> - **3(d)** — Mere discovery of a new form of a known substance (no enhanced efficacy).
> - **3(e)** — Mere admixture or rearrangement of known components.
> - **3(f)** — Mere arrangement/rearrangement of known devices (no functional improvement).
> - **3(i)** — Methods of agriculture, horticulture, or animal husbandry.
> - **3(k)** — Computer programs *per se* (algorithms alone are not patentable; technical application IS).
> - **3(l)** — Topographies of integrated circuits (covered by separate **Semi-Conductor IC Layout Design Act, 2000**).
> - **3(m)** — Abridgements of time, mental processes, business methods (Post-2020, business methods linked to technical contribution are patentable — *Ferid Allani* judgment).

### 2.1.2 The TRIPS Agreement (1995)

India is a **WTO signatory** and thus bound by the **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS)** agreement. This forced the **2005 amendment** to the Patents Act, restoring **product patents in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals**.

### 2.1.3 Patent Term, Renewal & Working Requirements

- **Term:** 20 years from the date of filing the application (Section 53).
- **Renewal Fees:** Payable annually from the **3rd year** onwards. Non-payment leads to lapse.
- **Compulsory License (Section 84):** Can be issued after 3 years if the patent is not "worked" (i.e., not manufactured in India) or if reasonable requirements of the public are not met.
- **Revocation (Section 64):** Can be sought on grounds of non-patentability, non-disclosure, or fraud.

## 2.2 Patent Disclosure — Why It Matters

The disclosure serves **three critical functions** in the patent ecosystem:

1. **Informs the Public** — Adds to the body of technical knowledge (the "consideration" in the social contract).
2. **Defines the Scope** — The claims define what is legally protected; everything else is free for public use.
3. **Enables Examination** — The Patent Examiner uses the disclosure to assess novelty and inventive step against prior art.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Element | Specification | KTU Examination Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent Duration** | **20 years** from filing date | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Renewal Start Year** | **3rd year** (annually thereafter) | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Grace Period for Novelty** | **12 months** for prior publication by inventor | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Convention Application (Paris)** | **12 months** priority from first foreign filing | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **PCT National Phase Entry** | **30/31 months** from priority date | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **PCT International Phase** | Up to **30/31 months** | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Compulsory License Earliest** | After **3 years** from grant | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Copyright Duration (Literary)** | Life + **60 years** | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Trademark Duration** | **10 years** (renewable) | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Industrial Design Duration** | **15 + 5 years** | ⭐ Low |
| **GI Duration** | **10 years** (renewable) | ⭐ Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always remember the **12-month grace period** rule (Section 11A and proviso to Section 2(1)(j)) — this is the most-frequently-tested edge case. The inventor can disclose (publish, present at a conference, exhibit) and still file within 12 months *without losing novelty*.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Patents in Engineering

In the B.Tech Product Design context, patents are not abstract legal instruments — they are **strategic engineering tools**:

- **Defensive Patenting** — Prevents competitors from copying core product innovations.
- **Licensing Revenue** — A patented design can be licensed to manufacturers (e.g., Qualcomm's licensing model).
- **Investor Confidence** — Startups with granted patents attract **3x higher VC funding** on average (industry studies).
- **Academic-to-Industry Bridge** — Patents under the **Bayh-Dole Act (US)** / **IPR 2022 Policy (India)** allow universities to commercialize publicly-funded research.
- **Technology Transfer** — Patents enable structured licensing between global firms (e.g., pharma patent pools during COVID-19).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Disclosure Preparation Process

## 3.1 Anatomy of a Patent Disclosure Document

A complete disclosure (as filed before the Indian Patent Office under Form 2) consists of **five mandatory sections**:

### Step 1: Title of the Invention
- Concise (typically 5–15 words).
- Must reflect the core inventive contribution.
- Avoid marketing language ("revolutionary," "best").

### Step 2: Field of the Invention
- Broad technical area (e.g., "The present invention relates to a solar-powered water purification system, and more particularly, to a photocatalytic module used therein").

### Step 3: Background & Prior Art
- Existing solutions in the field.
- Drawbacks / limitations of the prior art.
- Establishes the **problem the invention solves**.

### Step 4: Summary of the Invention
- One-paragraph technical summary stating the essence of the invention.
- Lists the key objects and advantages.

### Step 5: Detailed Description
- Complete description with reference to **drawings/figures**.
- Describes **embodiments** (preferred ways of carrying out the invention).
- States the **best method** known to the applicant (mandatory under Section 10(4)).

## 3.2 Writing the Patent Specification — Two Types

**Type 1: Provisional Specification**
- Filed to establish **priority date**.
- Does NOT require claims or detailed description.
- Valid for **12 months** — must be followed by a complete specification within this period.
- **Use case:** When the invention is at a nascent stage and the inventor wants to secure a filing date quickly.

**Type 2: Complete Specification**
- Mandatory filing within **12 months** of provisional.
- Contains: Title, Field, Background, Summary, Detailed Description, Claims, Abstract, Drawings.
- This is what gets examined and ultimately granted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** Under the **Patents (Amendment) Rules, 2024**, the timeline for examination has been streamlined. Once Request for Examination (RFE) is filed, the First Examination Report (FER) is typically issued within 6–18 months.

## 3.3 Drafting Claims — The Legal Boundary

Claims are the **most critical legal element** of a patent. They come in three hierarchical types:

### 3.3.1 Independent Claims
Define the broadest scope of protection. Example structure:
$$
\text{[Preamble]} + \text{[Transitional Phrase]} + \text{[Body/Element 1, Element 2, ...]}
$$

**Example (Mechanical Engineering):**
> "A vibration-damping mount for a rotary machine, **comprising**: a base plate; a viscoelastic damper element coupled to said base plate; and a tunable mass-spring assembly having an adjustable natural frequency."

### 3.3.2 Dependent Claims
Narrow down an independent claim by adding further limitations.

### 3.3.3 Process / Method Claims
Cover steps of a manufacturing or operational method.

## 3.4 Prior Art Search — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify International Patent Classification (IPC) codes for the invention.
**Step 2:** Search free databases:
- **Indian:** Indian Patent Advanced Search System (IPAS) — ipindia.gov.in.
- **Global:** Google Patents, Espacenet (European Patent Office), WIPO PATENTSCOPE.
**Step 3:** Use keyword + classification combinations.
**Step 4:** Screen results for **D1 (most relevant)**, **D2**, **D3** prior art references.
**Step 5:** Update the Background section to distinguish novelty.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Step — Citation Graph Analysis:** Use tools like **PatentSight**, **Derwent Innovation**, or free **Lens.org** to map citation networks and identify white spaces.

## 3.5 Disclosure Checklist (Pre-Filing)

| # | Checklist Item | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Title of Invention | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | Field of Invention | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | Background / Prior Art | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | Summary | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | Detailed Description with Drawings | ✅ Yes |
| 6 | Claims (at least 1 independent) | ✅ Yes |
| 7 | Abstract (≤150 words) | ✅ Yes |
| 8 | Declaration of Inventorship | ✅ Yes |
| 9 | Form 5 (Declaration of Inventorship) | ✅ Yes |
| 10 | Form 2 (Provisional or Complete Spec) | ✅ Yes |
| 11 | Form 3 (Statement of foreign filings) | ✅ Yes (if applicable) |
| 12 | Form 18 (Request for Examination) | ✅ Yes (within 48 months) |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Drafting Example

Suppose the invention is: *"A self-cooling helmet for two-wheeler riders using phase-change material."*

**Step 1 — Title:**
> "A Phase-Change Material Based Self-Cooling Helmet for Two-Wheeler Riders"

**Step 2 — Field:**
> The present invention relates to personal protective equipment for motorcyclists, and more particularly to a thermally regulated helmet employing encapsulated phase-change material.

**Step 3 — Background:**
> Conventional helmets trap heat, causing discomfort and fatigue. Existing solutions like battery-operated fans are power-hungry and add weight. There exists a need for a passive, lightweight cooling solution.

**Step 4 — Summary:**
> The present invention provides a helmet comprising an inner liner embedded with microencapsulated phase-change material (PCM) capsules, capable of absorbing body heat during continuous riding without external power.

**Step 5 — Claim (Independent):**
> 1. A self-cooling helmet, comprising:
>    (a) an outer shell;
>    (b) an inner liner; and
>    (c) a plurality of microencapsulated paraffin-based PCM capsules distributed within said inner liner, having a phase transition temperature between **30°C and 36°C**.

This single independent claim defines the legal scope. Everything outside this exact combination is unprotected.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define Intellectual Property Rights. List any four forms of IPR with their respective governing Acts in India.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) are the legal rights conferred upon creators/inventors for their intellectual creations in the industrial, scientific, literary, and artistic fields, granting them exclusive control over the use of such creations for a defined period.

**Four Forms of IPR with Governing Acts (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

| IPR Form | Governing Act |
|---|---|
| Patent | Indian Patents Act, **1970** |
| Trademark | Trade Marks Act, **1999** |
| Copyright | Copyright Act, **1957** |
| Industrial Design | Designs Act, **2000** |

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**State the three essential criteria for an invention to be patentable under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

1. **Novelty:** The invention must be new — i.e., it must not have been published or used anywhere in the world before the priority date of the patent application.

2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness):** The invention must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PSITA) — it must involve a technical advance or economic significance.

3. **Industrial Application:** The invention must be capable of being made or used in some kind of industry (Section 2(1)(ac)).

---

## 📙 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 with emphasis on:
- (i) Patentable and non-patentable subject matter under Section 3 & 4. **(4 Marks)**
- (ii) Rights of a patentee under Section 48. **(3 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Provisional Specification** and **Complete Specification**. Discuss the step-by-step structure of a complete specification. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer (Question A):**

### (a)(i) Patentable & Non-Patentable Subject Matter (4 Marks)

**Patentable Subject Matter:**
The Act permits patents on any invention that meets the criteria of **novelty, inventive step, and industrial application** and is not excluded under Sections 3 & 4.

**Non-Patentable Subject Matter (Section 3 — Key Categories):**
- **3(a)** Frivolous inventions or those contrary to natural laws.
- **3(b)** Inventions prejudicial to public order, morality, or human/animal/plant life.
- **3(d)** Discovery of a new form of a known substance (without enhanced efficacy).
- **3(e)** Mere admixture resulting in aggregation of known properties.
- **3(k)** Computer programs *per se* (algorithms alone).
- **3(l)** Topographies of integrated circuits.

### (a)(ii) Rights of Patentee under Section 48 (3 Marks)

Where a patent is granted, the patentee shall have **the exclusive right** to:
- Where the patent is for a **product**: prevent third parties from making, using, offering for sale, selling, or importing the patented product.
- Where the patent is for a **process**: prevent third parties from using the process, and from using, offering for sale, selling, or importing the product obtained directly by that process.

**Note (1 mark):** These rights are **negative in nature** — they allow the patentee to exclude others, not to practise the invention themselves.

### (b) Provisional vs Complete Specification (7 Marks)

| Feature | Provisional Specification | Complete Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Establishes priority date | Full technical + legal disclosure |
| **Claims** | Not required | Mandatory |
| **Drawings** | Optional | Mandatory (if needed) |
| **Validity** | 12 months | Filed within 12 months of provisional |
| **Content** | Brief description | Title, Field, Background, Summary, Detailed Description, Claims, Abstract |
| **Examined?** | No | Yes |

**Step-by-Step Structure of a Complete Specification (as per Section 10, Patents Act, 1970):**

1. **Title of the Invention** — Concise, descriptive.
2. **Field of the Invention** — Broad technical area.
3. **Background / Prior Art** — Existing solutions and their drawbacks.
4. **Summary** — One-paragraph essence of the invention.
5. **Detailed Description** — Full technical disclosure with reference to drawings.
6. **Best Method** — Best mode known to the applicant (mandatory under Section 10(4)).
7. **Claims** — Legal boundaries defining the scope.
8. **Abstract** — Concise technical summary (≤150 words).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is a **Patent Disclosure**? List and briefly explain the **mandatory components** of a patent specification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of TRIPS** in shaping Indian patent law. Discuss the **significance of the 2005 amendment** in the context of **product patents in pharmaceuticals**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer (Question B):**

### (a) Patent Disclosure & Mandatory Components (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A Patent Disclosure is the formal written specification and accompanying documents submitted to the Patent Office that fully, clearly, concisely, and accurately describe the invention, the best method of performing it, and the scope of legal protection sought. It is governed by **Section 10** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

**Mandatory Components (5 Marks — 1 Each):**

1. **Title** — Precise technical title reflecting the inventive contribution.
2. **Field of Invention** — Broad area to which the invention belongs.
3. **Background / Prior Art** — Existing solutions + the problem to be solved.
4. **Detailed Description** — Complete technical disclosure with drawings; must enable a person skilled in the art to perform the invention.
5. **Claims** — Legally enforceable statements defining the scope of protection.
6. **Abstract** — Concise summary (≤150 words).
7. **Best Method** — Best mode known to the inventor for carrying out the invention (mandatory under Section 10(4)).

### (b) TRIPS & the 2005 Amendment (7 Marks)

**TRIPS Agreement (3 Marks):**
The **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS)** agreement, enforced under the WTO from **January 1, 1995**, set minimum IP protection standards for all WTO member nations. Article 27 requires patents to be granted for inventions (product AND process) in all fields of technology, without discrimination, for a **minimum term of 20 years** from filing.

**Impact on India:**
- Prior to TRIPS, India maintained **process-only patents** in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals (a safeguard for affordable generic medicine production).
- To comply with TRIPS, India amended its Patents Act in **1999, 2002, and finally 2005**.

**2005 Amendment — Significance (4 Marks):**

- Restored **product patents** in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals (the famous "mailbox" provisions were a transitional bridge).
- Affected India's **generic drug industry**, led to **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** regarding Section 3(d).
- Introduced **compulsory licensing** safeguards (Section 84) — used in **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** for Nexavar.
- Strengthened **data exclusivity** provisions for agrochemical and pharmaceutical test data.
- Set new compliance regimes for **exclusive marketing rights (EMRs)** during the transition.

**Key Takeaway (1 Mark):** The 2005 amendment shifted India from a "process-patent regime" to a **TRIPS-compliant product-patent regime**, balancing innovation incentives with public health safeguards.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up durations:** Patent = 20 years, Trademark = 10 years, Copyright = Life+60 — these are commonly confused.
> 2. **Forgetting the 12-month grace period:** The grace period for the inventor's own disclosure is a HIGH-WEIGHTAGE edge case — always mention it in novelty questions.
> 3. **Not citing the Section number:** Always cite **Section 10** (specification), **Section 48** (rights), **Section 53** (term), **Section 84** (compulsory license). KTU examiners award marks for correct Section references.
> 4. **Missing the difference between Provisional and Complete Specifications:** This is a 14-mark favorite — tabulate the differences.
> 5. **Writing vague "claims":** Claims are legal — use precise "comprising/including" language, not marketing fluff.
> 6. **Not distinguishing Industrial Property from Copyright:** Many students lump all IP together — KTU expects clear taxonomy.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**
>
> ✅ **Patent Duration:** 20 years from filing date (Section 53).
>
> ✅ **Patentability Criteria:** Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application.
>
> ✅ **Renewal Fees:** Payable from 3rd year onwards (annually).
>
> ✅ **Grace Period:** 12 months for inventor's own disclosure.
>
> ✅ **PCT National Phase Entry:** 30–31 months from priority date.
>
> ✅ **Convention Application Priority:** 12 months from first foreign filing.
>
> ✅ **Compulsory License Earliest:** 3 years from date of grant (Section 84).
>
> ✅ **Specification Types:** Provisional (priority date) + Complete (examination).
>
> ✅ **Section 3(k):** Computer programs *per se* NOT patentable; technical application IS.
>
> ✅ **TRIPS Compliance:** India amended Patents Act in **2005** to allow product patents in pharma.
>
> ✅ **Rights of Patentee (Section 48):** Negative right — to *exclude* others.
>
> ✅ **Disclosure Components:** Title, Field, Background, Summary, Detailed Description, Claims, Abstract, Best Mode.
>
> ✅ **Trademark Duration:** 10 years (renewable indefinitely).
>
> ✅ **Copyright Duration (Literary):** Life of author + 60 years.
>
> ✅ **Disclosure's Social Contract:** Public gets knowledge; patentee gets 20-year monopoly.
